If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: 22 carrot gold is
questions.explanation: 22 carrot gold is a homogeneous mixture of gold or silver or Gold or Copper. Since pure gold is very soft, it cannot be used to make jewelry, and thus with 22 parts of gold 2 parts of silver or copper are mixed to make it hard.

questions.question: Two miscible liquids A and B are present in solution. The boiling point of A is 60oC and that of B is 90oC. Which of the following method is used to separate these two liquids?
questions.explanation: Two miscible liquids are separated by distillation process in which if the difference in boiling point temperature is more than 25oC or K then the simple distillation is applied and if the difference is less than 25oC or K then we use fractional distillation. Since there is a difference in boiling point temperature is 30oC so, we should use a simple distillation process.

questions.question: Which method is applied to separate the colored material from flower petals?
questions.explanation: Chromatography is a technique used for the separation of those solutes that dissolve in the same solvent. The coloured material from flower petals can be separated by chromatography process.

questions.question: NH4Cl is separated from NaCl by which one of the following methods?

options.html: By heating the solid mixture in a test tube
By dissolving the mixture in CS<sub>2</sub>
By dissolving in water and evaporation
By moving a magnet through the mixture.

questions.explanation: NH4Cl is separated from NaCl by heating the solid mixture in a test tube. NH4Cl being sublimable; changes into vapour which can be collected by cooling it. NaCl remains in the test tube.

questions.question: What is the correct order of the methods you apply to separate the components of a mixture of NH4Cl, common salt and sand?

options.html: Sublimation, dissolving in water, filtration and evaporation
Dissolving in water, evaporation, and sublimation
Moving a magnet, dissolving in water and sublimation.
Dissolving in water, filtration, evaporation, and sublimation.

questions.explanation: Firstly, ammonium chloride can be separated by sublimation. Then dissolve the mixture of sand and salt in water and separate sand by using filtration and salt can be separated from water by evaporation.

questions.question: What will be the boiling point of water at the top of a mountain where the atmospheric pressure is less than 1 atm?
questions.explanation: At an atmospheric pressure of exactly 760mm Hg (1 atm), the temperature at which a liquid boils is called the normal boiling point of the liquid. For water, the vapour pressure reaches the standard atmospheric pressure of 1 atmosphere at 100oC. So the normal boiling point of water is 100°C (212oF or 373K). As the altitude increases, the boiling point of water reduces. The boiling point of water at a high altitude is less than 100 degrees. So, the boiling point of water at the top of a mountain where the atmospheric pressure is less than 1 atm less than 100 degrees.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(a) Separation of cream from milk (i) Separating funnel
(b) Separation of oil from water (ii) Fractional distillation
(c) Separation of pigment from natural colours (iii) Chromatography
(d) Separation of components of air (iv) Centrifugation
questions.explanation:
• Centrifugation is used to separate denser particles and lighter particles from a mixture by using a centrifuging machine. The principle is that the denser particles are forced to the bottom and the lighter particles stay at the top when spun rapidly. It is used in dairies and home to separate butter from cream.
  • A separating funnel can be used to separate the components of the mixture of immiscible liquids. Separation of oil from water by separating funnel.
  • Chromatography is the technique used for separation of the components of a mixture that are soluble in the same solvent, which is absorbed differently on the stationary phase, thus leading to their separation. So, Separation of pigment from natural colors by the fractional distillation process.
  • Fractional distillation is suitable for the separation of miscible liquids with a boiling point difference of 25K  or less. Separation of components of air done by fractional distillation method.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(a) Separation of ammonium chloride from salt (i) Sublimation
(b) Separation of salt from water (ii) Magnetic separation
(c) Separation of sand & iron filings (iii) Evaporation
(d) Separation of benzene & toluene (iv) Fractional distillation
questions.explanation:
• Sublimation is the phase transition of a substance directly from the solid to the gas phase without passing through the intermediate liquid phase and vice versa. Separation of ammonium chloride from salt done by the sublimation process.
  • Evaporation is a type of vaporization of a liquid that occurs from the surface of a liquid into a gaseous phase that is not saturated with the evaporating substance. Separation of salt from water done by the evaporation process.
  • Magnetic separation is a process in which magnetically susceptible material is extracted from a mixture using a magnetic force. So, sand separated from iron filings by the magnetic separation process.
  • Simple distillation is a procedure by which two liquids with moderately large (around 50oC boiling points can be separated. If the difference in their temperatures is less than 30 degrees centigrade, the fractional distillation process is used in the separation of a liquid mixture into fractions differing in boiling point in the case of Benzene and Toluene.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(a) Fine mud particles suspended in water (i) Centrifugation
(b) Purification of water  (ii) Loading & sedimentation
(c) Small pieces of metal in the engine of a car (iii) Filtration
(d) Tea leaves from tea extract (iv) Chlorination
questions.explanation:
• Centrifugation is a process which involves the application of the centrifugal force for the sedimentation of heterogeneous mixtures with a centrifuge, Fine mud particles suspended in water can be separated by centrifugation process.
  • Water chlorination is the process of adding chlorine (Cl2 or hypochlorite ) to water. This method is used to kill certain bacteria and other microbes in tap water as chlorine is highly toxic.
  • Sedimentation is the process of removal of suspended particles that are heavier than water by gravitational settling. Small pieces of metal in the engine of a car can be separated by the sedimentation process.
  • Filtration is a process by which insoluble solids can be removed from a liquid by using a filter paper. Tea leaves extract from tea by the filtration process.

questions.question:
Statement A: Brass is a solution while gun powder is not.
Statement B: Air represents a solution in terms of science.
Which of the two statements is true?

options.html: Neither Statement A nor Statement B.

questions.explanation:
Brass is a homogeneous mixture of copper and zinc. Gun powder is not a powder. It is a simple mixture of potassium nitrate, sulfur, and charcoal. A solution is a homogeneous mixture of two or more substances. Air represents a solution in terms of science. Air is a mixture of gases as gases are liquified and then separated by fractional distillation.
Statement A is false and Statement B is true.

questions.question: Find the incorrect statement

options.html: No energy changes occur when the constituent of air tried to be mixed.
Cesium and gallium are liquids above 30<sup>o</sup>C.
The mixture can be called as a single substance.
The purity of compounds can be tested by determining their melting points.

questions.explanation: Mixtures are a substance that consists of two or more pure substances. So the given statement is incorrect.

questions.question: Which one is a physical change?
questions.explanation: Adding of common salt (NaCl) to water is physical change as no new substance is formed and no heat is evolved during the addition of salt in water. Also, salt can be obtained by evaporation.

questions.question: Which of the following are chemical changes?

options.html: Drying of wet clothes in sun light

questions.explanation: The cooking of vegetables is a chemical change because a new substance is formed having new properties and heat is used to change the state. The cooked vegetable cannot be reversed back as a raw vegetable.

questions.question:
What happens on adding dilute HCl to a mixture of iron filling and sulphur powder?

  1. H2S is formed.
  2. A colourless and odourless gas is formed.
  3. A greenish solution appears.
  4. FeS is formed.
questions.explanation:
Fe(s) + 2HCl(aq) → FeCl2(aq) + H2(g)
Sulfur will not react with HCl and it will not conduct electricity.
FeCl2 is a Pale blue-green. And H2 is a colourless and odourless gas.
So, Statement B and C are the correct statements.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements are incorrect

  1. The properties of a compound are different from its constituents elements
  2. A mixture is homogenous but a compound is heterogeneous
  3. Formation of a compound is a chemical change
  4. Formation of a mixture is a chemical change
questions.explanation:
• A compound is a substance formed when two or more elements are chemically bonded together. The property of the compound has always different from its constituents. A true solution is a homogeneous mixture.
  • The compound has a fixed composition. The mixture has a variable composition. So, the compound is homogenous and the mixture is heterogeneous.
  • A compound is a substance formed when two or more elements are chemically bonded together. So, the formation of a compound is a chemical change.
  • Constituents of the mixture can be separated by physical methods. So, the Formation of a mixture is a physical change.
  • So, statements (B) and (D) are incorrect statements.

tests.name: Atoms and Molecules Test-01
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Atoms and Molecules Test-01 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Who suggested that if we go on the dividing matter, a stage will come when particles obtained can’t be divided further?
questions.explanation: Leucippus and Democritus are widely regarded as the first atomists in the Greek tradition. They had suggested that if we go dividing matter, a stage will come when particles obtain can’t be divided further.

questions.question: The diameter of an atom of Hydrogen is:
questions.explanation: The diameter of the Hydrogen atom is 10-10 m. It is the smallest atom among all elements.

questions.question: Who was the first to use the symbols for elements?
questions.explanation: Dalton was the first scientist who used the symbols to represent the elements of modern chemistry. The modern symbols are different from the symbols proposed by Dalton. Dalton, considered as the father of modern atomic theory, made a logbook entry that day titled, "Observations on the Ultimate Particles of Bodies and their Combinations."

questions.question: What is the Latin name of Potassium?
questions.explanation: The Latin name of the Potassium element is Kalium from which the symbol of Potassium is derived as K.

questions.question: How many atoms are present in {tex}{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}^{ - {\text{3}}}{/tex}ion?
questions.explanation: There are 5 atoms in the phosphate ion, -1 atom of Phosphorus, and 4 atoms of Oxygen.

questions.question: What is the name given to the shorthand representation of an element?
questions.explanation: Shorthand representation of an element is called a symbol. The symbol of an element is derived from an English name or Latin name.

questions.question: What is the Latin name of sodium?
questions.explanation: The Latin name of sodium is Natrium. Therefore, the symbol of Sodium is Na.

questions.question: Argentum is the Latin name of:
questions.explanation: The Latin name of Silver is Argentum. The symbol of Silver is Ag.

questions.question: The formula of a compound is Ca3(PO4)2. What is the valency of Ca in it?
questions.explanation:
The valency of Ca in Ca3(PO4)2 is 2.
The chemical formula of a compound is formed by interchanging the valency number of the ions or the radicals. The valency number is shifted to the lower right of the ion or the radical.

questions.question: Phosphorus is:
questions.explanation: Phosphorus is tetra-atomic. Each molecule of phosphorus contains 4 atoms.

questions.question: How many times is an atom of Sulphur heavier than an atom of Carbon?
questions.explanation: The atomic mass of Sulphur is 32 u and the atomic mass of Carbon is 12 u. So, an atom of Sulphur is {tex}\frac {32}{12}{/tex} times or {tex}\frac 83{/tex} times heavier than an atom of Carbon.

questions.question: What is the atomicity of ammonia?
questions.explanation:
The number of atoms present in one molecule of a substance is called its atomicity.
Atomicity of Ammonia (NH3) is 4 (1 atom of Nitrogen and 3 atoms of Hydrogen).

questions.question: An element X has valency 3 while the element Y has valency 2. Write the formula of the compound formed by X and Y.
questions.explanation: The formula of an ionic compound is formed by interchanging the valencies of the constituent atoms. The formula of the compound having atoms of X and Y is X2Y3. The valency (2) of Y forms the subscript of X and the valency (3) of X forms the subscript of Y.

questions.question: An element A forms an oxide with formula A2O3. What is its valency?
questions.explanation: The formula of an ionic compound is formed by interchanging the valencies of the constituent atoms. Therefore, the valency of element A is 3 and that of element O is 2.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(1) Ozone (A) Monoatomic
(2) Helium (B) Tetraatomic
(3) Oxygen (C) Diatomic
(4) Phosphorous (D) Triatomic
questions.explanation:
An element, like Helium, is termed mono-atomic since it can exist independently as a single atom. Oxygen is di-atomic while ozone is tri-atomic.

 (1) Ozone  (D) forms a tri-atomic molecule.
 (2) Helium  (A) is mono-atomic.
 (3) Oxygen  (C) forms a di-atomic molecule.
 (4) Phosphorous  (B) forms a tetra-atomic molecule.

tests.name: Atoms and Molecules Test-02
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Atoms and Molecules Test-02 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

 (1) Cobalt  (A) S
 (2) Copper  (B) Co
 (3) Sulphur  (C) Na
 (4) Sodium  (D) Cu
questions.explanation:
A Symbol is a short hand representation of an element.

 (1) Symbol of Cobalt  (B) Co
 (2) Symbol of Copper  (D) Cu
 (3) Symbol of Sulphur  (A) S
 (4) Symbol of Sodium  (C) Na

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) The instrument used to produce images of atoms (i) Compound
(b) The atomic theory of matter (ii) John Dalton
(c) An allotrope of carbon (iii) STM
(d) Potassium Sulphate (iv) Fullerene
questions.explanation:
The Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM) is a type of electron microscope that shows three-dimensional images of a sample. In the STM, the structure of a surface is studied using a stylus that scans the surface at a fixed distance from it. Fullerene is an allotrope of carbon - a form of carbon having a large spheroidal molecule consisting of a hollow cage of sixty or more carbon atoms. Buckminsterfullerene was the first known example. Fullerenes are produced chiefly by the action of arc discharge between carbon electrodes in an inert atmosphere.

(a) The instrument used to produce images of atoms (iii) STM
(b) The atomic theory of matter (ii) John Dalton
(c) An allotrope of carbon (iv) Fullerene
(d) Potassium Sulphate (i) Compound

questions.question:
What according to Dalton’s atomic theory is true among the following:

  1. Atom is divisible into protons, electrons, and neutrons.
  2. Atoms of the same element have different atomic masses.
  3. Atoms are the ultimate indivisible particle of matter.
  4. Atoms of the same element have the same atomic masses, size, and chemical properties.
questions.explanation: According to Dalton’s atomic theory, atoms are the ultimate indivisible particle of matter, and atoms of the same element have the same atomic masses, size, and chemical properties.

questions.question: During a chemical reaction, the sum of the masses of the reactants and products remains unchanged. This is known as ________.
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of mass states that matter can neither be created not destroyed. Therefore, during a chemical reaction, the sum of the masses of the reactants and products remains unchanged.

questions.question:
Which of the following statement is true?
Statement A: Atoms can exist independently.
Statement B: The law of constant proportions is applicable only to pure chemical compounds.

options.html: Neither statement A nor statement B

questions.explanation:
Atom is not able to exist independently. The law of constant proportions is applicable only to pure chemical compounds.
So, statement B is correct.

questions.question: Which postulate of Dalton’s atomic theory is the basis of the law of conservation of mass?

options.html: Atoms are the smallest particles of matter.
Atoms can neither be created nor destroyed.
Every matter is made up of small particles.

questions.explanation:
The basis of the Law of conservation of mass is the following postulate given by Dalton:
"Atoms can neither be created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction."

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(1) Smallest particle ‘parmanu’ (A) Dalton’s atomic theory
(2) Atoms are indivisible (B) Law of constant proportions
(3) Balanced chemical equation (C) Law of conservation of mass
(4) Elements combine in fixed ratios (D) Maharshi Kannad
questions.explanation:
Dalton propounded the tenets of his atomic theory in his 1805 work - "A New System of Chemical Philosophy". According to him:

  • The chemical elements are made of small indivisible particles called "atoms".
  • The atoms of an element are identical in mass.
  • Atoms of different elements have different masses.
  • Atoms combine only in small, whole number ratios like 1:1, 1:2, 2:3, etc.
  • Atoms cannot be created or destroyed.
 (a) Smallest particle ‘parmanu’  (iv) The idea was given by Maharshi Kannad
 (b) Atoms are indivisible  (i) The postulate of Dalton’s atomic theory
 (c) Balanced chemical equation  (iii) The outcome of the Law of conservation of mass
 (d) Elements combine in fixed ratios  (ii) Feature of Law of constant proportions

questions.question:
Which of the following is/are anion(s)?

  1. Magnesium
  2. Sulphate
  3. Oxalate
  4. Ammonium
questions.explanation: Sulphate and oxalate ions are anions while magnesium and ammonium ions are cations.

questions.question: What is the ratio of magnesium and sulphur by mass in magnesium sulphide?
questions.explanation: The atomic mass of magnesium is 24. The atomic mass of sulphur is 32. Therefore, the ratio of magnesium and sulphur by mass in magnesium sulphide (MgS) is 24:32 or 3:4.

questions.question: Calculate the formula unit mass of ZnCl2? (nearest approximation)
questions.explanation:
Formula unit mass of ZnCl2 is (66 + 35.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 137u) 
The atomic mass of Zn is 66 and the atomic mass of Cl is 35.5

questions.question: “Gram atomic mass of an element and the gram molecular mass of a compound contains the same number of molecules”. This is a ________.
questions.explanation: The gram atomic mass of an element and the gram molecular mass of a compound contains the same number of molecules, which is equal to 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules.

questions.question:
Which of the two statements is true?
Statement A: X – rays are electromagnetic waves of very short wavelength.
Statement B: The maximum number of electrons that can be accommodated in the L shell is 8.

options.html: Both the statements - A and B
Neither statement A nor statement B.

questions.explanation: Both statements are true. X-rays are electromagnetic waves of high energy and very short wavelength. They are able to pass through many materials that are opaque to light; therefore they are used to take images of the human body for medical purposes. The maximum number of electrons that can be accommodated in the L shell is 8.

questions.question: The sample of water from a well is analysed. What will be the ratio of hydrogen and oxygen in it by mass?
questions.explanation: The atomic mass of Hydrogen is 1 u and that of Oxygen is 16 u. The formula of water is H2O. Hence, the ratio of Hydrogen and Oxygen by mass in water will be 2:16 or 1:8. The ratio will be the same irrespective of the source of the water.

questions.question: Identify the incorrect statement(s).

options.html: Atoms of the same element may have different masses.
Atoms of different elements may have same masses.
Atoms have been found to be made up of sub-atomic particles.

questions.explanation:
• Atoms of different elements may have the same masses. E.g. Argon and Calcium.
  • Atoms of the same element may have different masses as in the case of isotopes.
  • Atoms are made up of sub-atomic particles - electron, proton, and neutron.

All the statements are correct.

questions.question:
Which of the two statement(s) is/are true?
Statement A: Atomic mass of an element is not its actual mass but relative mass.
Statement B: With the help of STM, it is possible to take the photograph of some atoms.
questions.explanation:
The atomic mass of an element is not its actual mass but relative mass compared to the mass of a carbon-12 atom.
Photographs of some atoms can be taken by using an STM (Scanning Tunneling Microscope). The scanning tunneling microscope (STM) is a type of electron microscope that shows three-dimensional images of a sample. In the STM, the structure of a surface is studied using a stylus that scans the surface at a fixed distance from it.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question:
What information do we get from the molecular formula?

  1. It represents one molecule of the substance.
  2. It does not tell the name of the substance.
  3. It tells about the type of atoms.
  4. It represents the formula mass unit of the substance.
questions.explanation: The molecular formula of a substance (an element or a compound) is a symbolic representation of the actual number of atoms present in one molecule of that substance. It represents the formula mass unit of the substance. It also conveys the name of the substance. Therefore, (a), (c) and (d) are correct.

questions.question: How many moles are present in 40 g of He?
questions.explanation: Number of moles of Helium = Mass of Helium / Molar mass of Helium = {tex}40 \over 4{/tex} = 10 moles. Atomic mass or molar mass of Helium is 4 u.

questions.question:
Which of the following elements are present in Quick lime?

  1. Calcium, Oxygen
  2. Sodium, Hydrogen, Oxygen
  3. Calcium, Bromine
  4. Calcium chloride
questions.explanation: The chemical formula of the Quick lime is CaO. So, Calcium (Ca) and Oxygen (O) elements are present in Quick lime.

questions.question: What is the formula of calcium phosphide?
questions.explanation: The valency of Calcium is 2 (Ca2+)and the valency of the Phosphide ion is 3 (P3-). A chemical compound is always electrically neutral; so the positive and negative valencies of the ions or radicals present in calcium phosphide should add up to zero. Therefore, the chemical formula of calcium phosphide is Ca3P2.

questions.question: What is the mass of 0.5 moles of a Hydrogen atom?
questions.explanation: The atomic mass of hydrogen is 1 u. Mass of one mole of Hydrogen is equal to 1 g. Therefore, the mass of 0.5 moles of Hydrogen atom will be 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex}1 = 0.5g

questions.question: How many atoms of Sulphur are present in 0.5 moles of SO2?
questions.explanation:
Each molecule SO2 contains one atom of Sulphur and two atoms of Oxygen.
So, 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules of SO2 will contain 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms of Sulphur.
6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules of {tex}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex} make up one mole of SO2,
Since 1 mole of SO2 contains 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms of Sulphur,
Therefore, 0.5 moles of SO2 will contain  0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms or 3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms of Sulphur.

questions.question: How many moles are present in 2.3 g of Na?
questions.explanation:
The molecular mass of Sodium is 23 u. So, 23 g of Sodium (Na) is equal to 1 mole of Na.
Number of moles of Na present in 2.3 g of Na = Given mass of Na / Molar mass of Na = {tex}2.3 \over23{/tex} = 0.1 mole

questions.question: What is the name given to 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023?
questions.explanation: The fixed number of atoms or molecules present in the gram atomic mass of an atom or molecules is 6.022{tex}\times{/tex}1023. This number is known as Avogadro constant.

questions.question: Calculate the number of particles in 0.01 mole of carbon atoms.
questions.explanation:
The number of particles in one mole of carbon atom = 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 atoms
Therefore, the number of particles present in 0.01 mole of carbon = 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01 = 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1021 atoms.

questions.question: A sample of CaCO3 contains 3.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 ions of Ca+2 and {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{3}}}^{ - {\text{2}}}{/tex}. The mass of the sample is:
questions.explanation: The mass of one mole of CaCO3 is equal to 100 g. 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 ions are equivalent to one mole. Therefore, mass of 3.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 ions will be equivalent to ({tex}100 \over 2{/tex}) g or 50 g.

questions.question: What mass of CO2 will 3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules contain?
questions.explanation:
The molecular mass of CO2 is 44 (12 + 26 {tex}\times{/tex} 2).
6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules of carbon will contain 44 g.
Hence, 3.011 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules of CO2 will contain a mass of 22 g.

questions.question: Which of the following has the maximum number of molecules?
questions.explanation: 1 g of H2 (Molar mass 2 g) contains {tex}{\text{3}}.0{\text{11}} \times {\text{1}}{0^{{\text{23 }}}}{/tex}molecules, 22 g of {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex} (Molar mass 44 g) contains {tex}{\text{3}}.0{\text{11}} \times {\text{1}}{0^{{\text{23 }}}}{/tex}molecules, 14 g of {tex}{{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex}(Molar mass 28 g) contains {tex}{\text{3}}.0{\text{11}} \times {\text{1}}{0^{{\text{23 }}}}{/tex}molecules and 8 g of {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{4}}}{/tex}(Molar mass 16 g) contains {tex}{\text{3}}.0{\text{11}} \times {\text{1}}{0^{{\text{23 }}}}{/tex}molecules.

questions.question: Calculate the number of moles in 17 g of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2).
questions.explanation: The molecular mass of H2O2 is 34 u. So, 34 g of hydrogen peroxide will contain 1 mole. Therefore, 17 g of hydrogen peroxide will contain {tex}17\over34{/tex} moles or {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mole.

questions.question: What weight of Calcium contains the same number of atoms as are present in 2g of Sulphur?
questions.explanation:
No. of moles present in 2g of Sulphur = {tex}\frac {2}{32}{/tex} = 0.0625 moles.
The atomic mass of Calcium = 40u.
Mass of Calcium containing 0.0625 moles of atoms = 0.0625 {tex}\times{/tex} 40 = 2.5 g

questions.question: Which has more molecules - 4g of methane or 11 g of CO2?

options.html: Both have the same number of moles.

questions.explanation:
4 g of methane will contain {tex}4\over16{/tex} moles of molecules = 0.25 moles
11 g of CO2 will contain {tex}11\over44{/tex} moles = 0.25 moles.
Hence, both will contain an equal number of moles and an equal number of molecules.

tests.name: Atoms and Molecules Test-04
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Atoms and Molecules Test-04 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: One mole of N2 is equal to ________.
questions.explanation: One mole of N2 is equal to 28 g of nitrogen or 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules.

questions.question: Calculate the number of Mg atoms in 0.024 g of Mg,
questions.explanation:
Atomic mass of Mg is 24. Number of atoms in 24 g of Mg = NA = {tex}6.022 \times {10^{23}}{/tex}
Therefore, number of atoms in 0.024 g of Mg = {tex}\frac{{0.024}}{{24}} \times 6.022 \times {10^{23}}{/tex} = {tex}{\text{6}}.0{\text{22 }} \times {\text{ 1}}{0^{{\text{2}}0}}{/tex}

questions.question:
Which of the following two statement(s) is/are true?
Statement A: Mole is quite often known as chemist's dozen. 
Statement B: The mass of one-twelfth ({tex}1\over12{/tex}) of the mass of one atom of Carbon is taken as 1 u.

options.html: Both the statements - A and B
Neither Statement A nor Statement B.

questions.explanation:
A mole is also known as a chemist's dozen. A mole, just like a dozen, is a common counting unit. A counting unit is a convenient number that makes it easier to count objects. The mass of one-twelfth of the mass of one atom of Carbon is taken as 1u.
Both statements are correct.

questions.question: How many molecules are present in one gram molecular mass of a substance?
questions.explanation:
Gram molecular mass is the mass in grams of one mole of a molecular substance. 
One mole contains  6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules. This is known as the Avogadro number.
Therefore, one gram molecular mass of a substance has 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules.

questions.question: The atomic mass of calcium is 40. The number of moles in 60 g of Calcium are:
questions.explanation: Number of moles in 60 g of Calcium = {tex}Given\ mass\ of\ Calcium \over Atomic \ mass \ of \ Calcium {/tex}= {tex}60\over40{/tex} = 1.5 mol

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(1) Nitrate (A) {tex}{P^{3 - }}{/tex}
(2) Phosphide (B) {tex}P{O_4}^{3 - }{/tex}
(3) Nitrite (C) {tex}N{O_2}^ - {/tex}
(4) Phosphate (D) {tex}N{O_3}^ - {/tex}
questions.explanation:
(1) Nitrate (D) {tex}N{O_3}^ - {/tex}
(2) Phosphide (A) {tex}{P^{3 - }}{/tex}
(3) Nitrite (C) {tex}N{O_2}^ - {/tex}
(4) Phosphate (B) {tex}P{O_4}^{3 - }{/tex}

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(1) {tex}PC{l_5}{/tex} (A) Dinitrogen tetraoxide
(2) {tex}{N_2}{O_5}{/tex} (B) Phosphorous pentachloride
(3) {tex}{N_2}{O_4}{/tex} (C) Carbon tetrachloride
(4) {tex}CC{l_4}{/tex} (D) Dinitrogen pentaoxide
questions.explanation:
(1) {tex}PC{l_5}{/tex}  (B) Phosphorous pentachloride
(2) {tex}{N_2}{O_5}{/tex}  (D) Dinitrogen pentaoxide
(3) {tex}{N_2}{O_4}{/tex}  (A) Dinitrogen tetraoxide
(4) {tex}CC{l_4}{/tex}  (C) Carbon tetrachloride

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(1) 1 mole of {tex}{O_2}{/tex} (A) {tex}6.022 \times {10^{23}}{/tex}molecules
(2) 1 mole of N (B) 12 g
(3) 1 mole of C (C) {tex}6.022 \times {10^{23}}{/tex}atoms
(4) 1 mole of {tex}{H_2}O{/tex} (D) 18 g
questions.explanation:
(1) 1 mole of {tex}{O_2}{/tex} (A) {tex}6.022 \times {10^{23}}{/tex}molecules
(2) 1 mole of N (C) {tex}6.022 \times {10^{23}}{/tex}atoms 
(3) 1 mole of C (B) 12 g
(4) 1 mole of {tex}{H_2}O{/tex} (D) 18 g

questions.question:
Which is not true about H2SO4?

  1. It is composed of 2 Hydrogen, 1 Sulphur and 4 Oxygen atoms.
  2. Its relative molecular mass is 98.
  3. It is composed of one molecule of H2, one atom of S and two molecules of O2.
  4. Its relative molecular mass is 108.
questions.explanation: H2SO4 is composed of 2 Hydrogen, 1 Sulphur and 4 Oxygen atoms. It relative molecular mass is 98.

questions.question:
Which of the following is the correct definition of a mole?

  1. It is equal to the gram atomic mass of the substance.
  2. It contains 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1020 atoms of a given substance.
  3. It is equal to the gram molecular mass of the substance.
  4. It contains 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 molecules of a given substance.
questions.explanation: A mole is defined as the amount of a substance that contains the same number of elementary entities (e.g. atoms, molecules, ions, electrons, photons) as atoms in 0.012 kilograms of carbon-12. Gram atomic mass is the mass (in grams) of one mole of atoms in a monatomic element. It is numerically equal to the relative atomic mass (or atomic weight) in grams. Gram molecular mass is the mass (in grams) numerically equal to the molecular weight of a substance or the sum of all the atomic masses in its molecular formula.

questions.question:
Which of the following is/are the incorrect chemical formulas of the given compound?

  1. Zinc Phosphate - Zn3(PO4)2
  2. Ferric carbonate - Fe3(CO3)2
  3. Calcium Phosphate - Ca3PO4
  4. Sodium Sulphide- Na2S
questions.explanation:
Zinc Phosphate - Zn3(PO4)2 and Sodium Sulphide -Na2S is correct chemical formulae.
Fe3(CO3)2is an incorrect formula for Ferric Carbonate. The correct chemical formula would be Fe2(CO3)3.
Ca3PO4 is an incorrect formula for Calcium Phosphate. The correct chemical formula would be Ca3(PO4)2.

questions.question: Identify the correct statement:

options.html: The molecules of noble gas elements are diatomic in nature.
Dalton was the first to state that the smallest indivisible portion of matter is an atom.
The law of conservation of mass was given by Louis Proust.
The law of constant proportions was stated by A Lavoisier.

questions.explanation:
Dalton was the first to state that the smallest indivisible portion of matter is an atom. 
The law of conservation of mass was given by A.Lavoisier. The law of constant proportions was stated by Louis Proust. The molecules of noble gas elements are monoatomic in nature.

questions.question:
Which of the two statements is/are true?

Statement A : Positive ion migrates towards the cathode on passing electric current.
Statement B : Some of the atoms of the elements have fractional mass.

options.html: Neither statement A nor statement B

questions.explanation: Positive (+ve) ions migrate towards (negatively charged) cathode on passing electric current.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(1) Ions (A) Negatively charged
(2) Anions (B) Polyatomic ion
(3) Cations (C) Positively charged
(4) Radical (D) Charged particles
questions.explanation:
(1) Ions (D) Charged particles
(2) Anions (A) Negatively charged 
(3) Cations (C) Positively charged
(4) Radical (B) Polyatomic ion

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response:

(1) A dozen of pencil (A) Mole
(2) Avogadro constant (B) 12
(3) Unit used for calculation of amount of chemical substances (C) Carbon-12
(4) Reference atom (D) {tex}6.022 \times {10^{23}}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
(1) A dozen of pencil  (B) 12
(2) Avogadro constant  (D) {tex}6.022 \times {10^{23}}{/tex}
(3) Unit used for calculation of amount of chemical substances  (A) Mole
(4) Reference atom  (C) Carbon-12

tests.name: Structure Of The Atoms Test-01
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Structure Of The Atoms Test-01 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Which of the following is/are regarded as a universal particle(s)?
questions.explanation: Electrons are present in atoms of all the elements. They are distributed around the nucleus in an atom. The charge/mass (e/m) ratio of electrons is constant in all elements. Hence, an electron is regarded as a universal particle.

questions.question: Who discovered anode rays?
questions.explanation: Anode rays were discovered by E. Goldstein as canal rays. These rays consist of positively charged particles. Goldstein called these positive rays channel rays" or canal rays because they were produced by the holes or channels in the cathode.

questions.question: “Electrons present in the extranuclear portion are not stationary rather they revolve around the nucleus at high speed following a circular path”. Who said this?
questions.explanation: In his model of the atom, Rutherford stated that electrons present in the extranuclear portions are not stationary, rather they revolve around the nucleus at high speed and follow a circular path.

questions.question: Find the incorrect statement.

options.html: A neutron is slightly heavier than a proton.
Most of the space in atom is empty.
Neutrons were unknown at the time when Rutherford performed the scattering experiment.

questions.explanation: Proton is not considered as a universal particle. An electron is considered as a universal particle.

questions.question: How many times is the radius of the extranuclear portion more than that of the nucleus of an atom?
questions.explanation: The nuclear region is a small portion of the atom as compared to the space inside an atom.

questions.question: Who received the Noble Prize for the work on the structure of an atom?
questions.explanation: Neils Bohr received the Noble Prize in Physics in 1922 for his work on the structure of an atom. He proposed the Bohr model of an atom having discrete shells in which the electrons are distributed.

questions.question: Out of L and M shells, which is nearer to the nucleus of an atom?

options.html: Both are equally near to the nucleus of an atom
Both are not equally near to the nucleus of an atom

questions.explanation: The order of shells around the nucleus is K, L, M, N from the nucleus. So, L shell is nearer to the nucleus than M shell.

questions.question: What was the source of alpha particles in Rutherford's scattering experiment?
questions.explanation: In Rutherford's {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle scattering experiment, the source of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles was a Helium nucleus.

questions.question: Who discovered neutron?
questions.explanation: Neutron was discovered by James Chadwick. Neutron is a neutral sub-atomic particle having a mass almost equal to the mass of proton.

questions.question: ‘Christmas Pudding Model’ of an atom was suggested by:
questions.explanation: The Christmas pudding model of an atom was proposed by J.J.Thomson. According to this model, an atom is a positively charged sphere in which negatively charged electrons are embedded.

questions.question: Which of the following air pressures in the discharge tube is appropriate for the production of cathode rays?
questions.explanation: Cathode rays are produced in cathode tube having a very high voltage and a low pressure. A pressure of 0.001 mm is appropriate for the production of cathode rays.

questions.question: Rutherford’s alpha ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) particles scattering experiment’ resulted into the discovery of _________ in the atom.
questions.explanation: Rutherford’s alpha-particle experiment was responsible for the discovery of the nucleus of an atom. The alpha particles passing close to the nucleus deviated from their path and the ones hitting the "central charged region" bounced back.

questions.question: According to 2n2 rule, which shell in an atom can accommodate 32 electrons:
questions.explanation:
K shell means n = 1, L means n = 2 and M shell means n = 3 and N shell means n = 4.
Number of electrons in N-shell = 2n2 = 2(4)2 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 16 = 32 electrons.

questions.question: If K and L shells of an atom are full, then what would be the number of electrons in the atom?
questions.explanation: K shell (n = 1) of an atom contains a maximum of 2 electrons and L shell (n = 2) contains a maximum of 8 electrons. Therefore, if K and L shells of an atom are full, then the number of electrons in the atom is 2 + 8 = 10.

questions.question: What is the {tex}\frac e m{/tex} value of an electron?
questions.explanation: The charge on an electron is 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C and the mass of one electron is 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg. Therefore, {tex}e\over m{/tex} ratio will be {tex}1.6 \times 10^{-19} C \over 9.1 \times 10^{-31}{/tex} kg = 1.76 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 C/kg.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: The main subatomic particles of a nucleus are:
questions.explanation:
The main subatomic particles of a nucleus are protons and neutrons. These are collectively called nucleons.
Electrons are located in different shells outside the nucleus of an atom.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response :

 (1) Electron  (A) E.Goldstein
 (2) Proton  (B) Rutherford
 (3) Neutron  (C) James Chadwick
 (4) Atomic nucleus  (D) J.J.Thomson
questions.explanation:
(1) Electron  (D) Discovered by J.J.Thomson
 (2) Proton  (A) Discovered by E.Goldstein
 (3) Neutron  (C) Discovered by James Chadwick
 (4) Atomic nucleus  (B) Discovered by Rutherford

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response : 

(1) Canal rays consist of positively charged particles protons  (A) Rutherford
(2) Electrons are distributed in shells  (B) J.J.Thomson
(3) Centre of an atom is dense  (C) J.Dalton
(4) Atom is indivisible  (D) Neils Bohr
questions.explanation:
(1) Canal rays consist of positively charged particles protons  (B) J.J.Thomson
(2) Electrons are distributed in shells  (D) Neils Bohr
(3) Centre of an atom is dense  (A) Rutherford
(4) Atom is indivisible  (C) J.Dalton

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response :

(1) Discovery of subatomic particles (A) Positively charged
(2) Energy levels (B) Orbits
(3) Universal particles (C) Discharge tube
(4) Canal rays (D) Electrons
questions.explanation:
(1) Discovery of subatomic particles (C) Discharge tube 
(2) Energy levels (B) Orbits
(3) Universal particles (D) Electrons
(4) Canal rays (A) Positively charged

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response :

 (1) Valency  (A) Combining capacity of an atom
 (2) Metals  (B) Gain electrons to became stable
 (3) Non-metals  (C) Lose electrons to become stable
 (4) Noble gases  (D) They have stable configuration
questions.explanation:
(1) Valency  (A) Combining capacity of an atom
 (2) Metals  (C) Lose electrons to become stable
 (3) Non-metals  (B) Gain electrons to became stable
 (4) Noble gases  (D) They have stable configuration

questions.question:
Which of the following is not observed by Rutherford in {tex}\alpha {/tex}-particle scattering experiment?

  1. Most of the {tex}\alpha {/tex}-particles rebound after hitting the gold foil.
  2. Some of the particles deflect by their path.
  3. Some of the particles do not pass through the gold foil.
  4. Most of the particles pass straight through the gold foil.
questions.explanation: Rutherford observed that some of the {tex}\alpha {/tex}-particles were deflected from their path after hitting the gold foil while most of the particles went straight through the gold foil.

questions.question:
Which of the following is correct about an atom?

  1. It is the smallest unit of matter.
  2. The size of an atom is very large as compared to the size of the nucleus of the atom.
  3. In an atom, the number of protons is equal to the number of electrons.
  4. Atoms are stable.
questions.explanation: An atom is the smallest unit of matter. The size of an atom is very large as compared to the size of the nucleus of the atom. In an atom, the number of protons is equal to the number of electrons. Atoms are stable particles.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is/are correct about {tex}\beta {/tex}-particles :

  1. They are electrons.
  2. They are deflected towards the positive electrode.
  3. They travel at the speed of sound.
  4. They are deflected towards the negative electrode.
questions.explanation: {tex}\beta {/tex}-particles are electrons. They are deflected towards the positive electrode.

questions.question: The electronic configuration of an element A is 2, 8, 6. How many electrons does it require to have a stable configuration?
questions.explanation: The number of electrons required to complete the octet represents the valency of an atom with electronegative character. The element with electronic configuration 2, 8, 6 will require 2 electrons to achieve a stable configuration.

questions.question: The number of valence electrons in Cl- ion is:
questions.explanation: The number of electrons present in the outermost shell of an atom is called the valence electrons. Chlorine atom contains 7 electrons in the outermost shell. Chlorine ion (Cl-) will have (7+1) electrons or 8 electrons in the outermost shell.

questions.question: Which element(s) have 7 electrons in their valence shell?
questions.explanation:
The atomic number of Chlorine (Cl) is 17. The electronic configuration is 2, 8, 7. So, 7 electrons are present in the valence shell of Chlorine.
Atomic number of Fluorine (F) is 9. Electronic configuration is 2, 7. So, 7 electrons are present in the valence shell of Fluorine.
Atomic number of Nitrogen (N) is 7. Electronic configuration is 2, 5. So, 5 electrons are present in the valence shell of Nitrogen.

questions.question: What is the physical state of water at 10oC?
questions.explanation: The physical state of water at 10oC is the liquid state. Below 0oC water in solid-state and above 100oC water in the vapour state.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Carbon (i) 2, 8, 1
(b) Sodium (ii) 2
(c) Argon (iii) 2, 8, 8
(d) Helium (iv) 2, 4
questions.explanation:
(a) Electronic configuration of Carbon  (iv) 2,4
 (b) Electronic configuration of Sodium  (i) 2,8,1
 (c) Electronic configuration of Argon  (iii) 2,8,8
 (d) Electronic configuration of Helium  (ii) 2

questions.question:
Which of the following elements have the same valency?

  1. An element with atomic number 12
  2. An element with atomic number 6
  3. An element with atomic number 4
  4. An element with atomic number 2
questions.explanation: The element with atomic number 12 will have an electronic configuration of 2, 8, 2. The element with atomic number 6 will have an electronic configuration of 2, 4. The element with atomic number 4 will have an electronic configuration of 2, 2. The element with atomic number 2 will have an electronic configuration of 2 (duplet). So. (a) and (c) will have the same valency.

questions.question: What is the actual mass of a proton?
questions.explanation: The mass of a proton is 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 Kg or 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 g. A proton is 1837 times heavier than an electron.

questions.question: What is name given to the number of protons in the nucleus of the atom?
questions.explanation: The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom is equal to its atomic number (Z). The sum of the number of protons and neutrons present in a nucleus is called the mass number (A).

questions.question: There are 14 protons and 13 neutrons in the nucleus of an element. What is its mass number?
questions.explanation:
The mass number (A) of an atom is equal to the sum of the number of protons and neutrons present in the nucleus of the atom.
So, the mass number (A) of the given element = number of protons + number of neutrons = 14 + 13 = 27 u

questions.question: The nitrogen atom has an atomic mass of 14 u and has 7 protons in its nucleus. How many neutrons does it have?
questions.explanation: Atomic mass (A) of Nitrogen is 14 u and number of protons (p+) in the nucleus is 7. So, number of neutrons (n0) = Mass number (A) – number of protons (p+) = 14 - 7 = 7

questions.question: An atomic number of an atom is defined as:

options.html: The number of neutrons they possess.
The number of protons they possess
The number of electrons they possess

questions.explanation: Atomic number (Z) is defined as the number of protons present in the nucleus of an atom.

questions.question: Which of the two will be chemically more reactive - an element X with atomic number 16 or an element Y with atomic number 17?

options.html: Both the elements X and Y are equally reactive.

questions.explanation: The electronic configuration of element X (Atomic number 16) is 2, 8, 6 and that of element Y (Atomic number 16) is 2, 8, 7. Element Y requires only one electron to complete the octet but X requires 2 electrons. So, Y will be more reactive.

questions.question: Write the number of neutrons in {tex}{}_{12}^{24}Mg{/tex}
questions.explanation: Magnesium (Mg) contains 12 protons (Atomic number is 12) and the mass number is 24. Number of neutrons = Mass number (A) – number of protons (Z) present in the nucleus. So, the number of neutrons = A - Z = 24 - 12 = 12.

questions.question: The atom of which element has twice as many electrons in its second shell as there are in the first shell:
questions.explanation: The electronic configuration of Carbon (C) is 2, 4. The first shell contains 2 electrons and the second shell contains 4 electrons. Hence, the number of electrons in its second shell is twice the number of electrons in its first shell.

questions.question: Which isotope of Hydrogen is known as protium?
questions.explanation: Hydrogen is present in three isotopic forms - protium, deuterium, and tritium. Hydrogen atom containing a single electron and a single proton without neutron is called as protium  {tex}\left( {_1^1H} \right){/tex}.

questions.question: The two isotopic forms of chlorine with masses 35 u and 37 u occur in the ratio of:
questions.explanation: Chlorine is found naturally in two isotopic forms having an atomic mass of 35 u and 37 u. The two isotopic forms occur in the ratio of 3:1. That makes the average atomic mass of chlorine 35.5 u.

questions.question: Find the incorrect pair:

options.html: An isotope of iodine - Cancer treatment
An isotope of cobalt - Cancer treatment
An isotope of Uranium - Fuel in a nuclear reactor
An isotope of Carbon – Radio carbon-dating technique

questions.explanation: An isotope of Uranium is used in a nuclear reactor. An isotope of cobalt is used in the treatment of cancer and an isotope of carbon is used for the carbon-dating technique. An isotope of iodine is not used in cancer treatment.

questions.question: Which radioactive isotope is used for the treatment of cancer?
questions.explanation: Cobalt isotope (Co-60) is used for the treatment of cancer. It is radioactive in nature. It is used for producing rays to kill the cancerous cells.

questions.question:
Which of the two statement is/are true?
Statement A: Nucleus of an atom is massive and electrically neutral in nature.
Statement B: Isotopes of an element are chemically similar.
questions.explanation:
The nucleus of an atom is massive but it is not electrically neutral in nature due to the presence of positively charged particles - protons.
Isotopes of an element are chemically similar due to the presence of the same number of valence electrons.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a characteristic of isotopes?

options.html: Isotopes differ in the number of neutrons.
Isotope are the different atoms of the same element.
The physical properties of isotopes are the same.
Isotopes of some elements are of radioactive nature.

questions.explanation: Isotopes are different atoms of the same element with the same atomic number but different atomic numbers. They differ in the number of neutrons. They are chemically similar but their physical properties are different.

questions.question: Which two of the following nuclei are isotopes of each other? {tex}{}_{90}^{231}Z{/tex}, {tex}{}_{91}^{230}Z{/tex}, {tex}{\text{ }}{}_{88}^{230}Z{/tex}, {tex}{\text{ }}{}_{90}^{233}Z{/tex}
questions.explanation: Isotopes are two or more atoms of the same element having same the atomic number but different mass numbers. In the given group of nuclei,  {tex}{}_{90}^{231}Z {/tex}, {tex}{ } _ { 90}^{ 233}Z{/tex} have equal atomic numbers (i.e. 90) but different mass numbers (231 and 233 respectively).

questions.question: Which of the following nuclei will be unstable?
questions.explanation: Carbon-14 or 14C is a radioactive isotope of carbon with an atomic nucleus containing 6 protons and 8 neutrons. It has an unstable nucleus.

tests.name: Structure Of The Atoms Test-04
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Structure Of The Atoms Test-04 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Example of isobars are:
questions.explanation: Isobars are two or more atoms of different elements having the same mass number but different atomic numbers. As all the given atoms belong to the same element, none of them are isobars.

questions.question: The atom which does not contain any neutron in the nucleus is:
questions.explanation:
• Protium - an isotope of Hydrogen - does not contain any neutron in its nucleus. The nucleus of protium contains only one proton.
  • Deuterium - another isotope of Hydrogen - contains 1 neutron in its nucleus.
  • Tritium - another isotope of Hydrogen - contains 2 neutrons.
  • The helium nucleus also contains 2 neutrons.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Deuterium (i) Radiocarbon Dating
(b) Carbon-14 (ii) Treatment of cancer
(c) Isotope of uranium (iii) Nuclear Reactors
(d) Cobalt-60 (iv) An isotope of Hydrogen
questions.explanation:
• Deuterium is an isotope of Hydrogen. 
  • Carbon-14 is a radioactive isotope used in radiocarbon dating.
  • An isotope of uranium is used as fuel in nuclear reactors. 
  • Cobalt isotope (Co–60) is used for the treatment of cancer. It is radioactive in nature. It is used for producing rays to kill the cancerous cells.

questions.question:
Isotopes of an element have:

  1. Same chemical properties
  2. Same atomic masses
  3. Different atomic numbers
  4. Atoms of the same element
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms with the same number of protons but a different number of neutrons. Since the atomic number is equal to the number of protons and the atomic mass is the sum of the number of protons and neutrons, it can also be said that isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same atomic number but a different mass number. They have the same chemical properties.

questions.question: How many electrons are present in the species He2+ ion?
questions.explanation: Helium (He) has two electrons in its atom. The cation (He2+) is formed through the loss of 2 electrons from the outer-most shell of the atom. Hence, the number of electrons in He2+ will be 0.

questions.question: In a species, the number of electrons is more than the number of protons. Predict its nature.
questions.explanation: An anion is formed by gaining electron(s) from other atoms. Hence, an anion contains more electrons than protons. On the other hand, a cation is formed through the loss of an electron(s). A neutral atom will have the same number of negatively-charged electrons as well as positively-charged protons.

questions.question:
Which of the two statements is/are true?
Statement A: The nature of cathode rays depends on the nature of gas taken in the discharge tube.
Statement B: Cathode rays rotate a light paddle wheel placed in their path.

options.html: Neither statement A nor statement B

questions.explanation: The nature of cathode rays does not depend on the nature of gas taken in the cathode tube. Cathode rays travel in a straight path and have energy that can rotate a light paddle wheel placed in their path.

questions.question: Valency of an element is 8; It means it has ________ electrons in its outermost shell.
questions.explanation: Valency is the combining capacity of an element. If an element has 8 valencies then it's outermost shell has 8 electrons or noble gas configuration.

questions.question: The order of the energy shells is:
questions.explanation: The energy of the shells increases from the inner-most to the outer-most shell. So the correct order of the energy of energy shells is K < L < M < N < O.

questions.question: Which of the following statements is not true about neutrons?

options.html: It is present in the nucleus of an atom.
It is highly unstable in nature.
It contributes to the mass of the atom.

questions.explanation: Neutron is a neutral sub-atomic particle present inside the nucleus of an atom. It contributes to the mass of the atom. Neutron is a stable particle.

questions.question: The sum of the first 15 multiples of 8 is
questions.explanation:
Multiples of 8 are 8, 16, 24, ...
Here, a = 8, d = 16 - 8 = 8 and n = 15
Now, Sn = {tex}\frac{n}{2}{/tex}[2a + (n - 1)d]
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} S15 = {tex}=\frac{{15}}{2}{/tex}[2 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 + (15 - 1)8]
{tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} S15 = {tex}\frac{{15}}{2}{/tex}[16 + 14 {tex}\times{/tex} 8]
= {tex}\frac{{15}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 128
= 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 64
= 960

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(1) Atomic number (A) Total number of neutrons and protons
(2) Atomic mass (B) Different atoms with same atomic masses
(3) Isotopes (C) Atoms of same element with same atomic numbers
(4) Isobars (D) Number of protons
questions.explanation:
Atomic number (Z) of a chemical element is the number of protons in the nucleus of that atom. It is a characteristic of the element and determines its place in the periodic table. The mass of an atom of a chemical element is approximately equivalent to the number of protons and neutrons in the atom (the mass number) or to the average number allowing for the relative abundance of the different isotopes. Isotopes are atoms with the same number of protons but different number of neutrons. Since the atomic number is equal to the number of protons and the atomic mass is the sum of the number of protons and neutrons, it can also be said that isotopes are atoms of the same element with the same atomic number but different mass number. Isobars are atoms (nuclides) of different chemical elements that have the same number of nucleons.

(1) Atomic number (D) equal to the number of protons
(2) Atomic mass (A) equal to the total number of neutrons and protons
(3) Isotopes (C) atoms of the same element with same atomic numbers
(4) Isobars (B) different atoms with same atomic masses

questions.question: The sum of the first n terms of an A.P. is given by
questions.explanation:
let S denote the sum of first n terms of an AP
a, a + d, a + 2d, a + 3d + ... a + (n - 1)d where a = 1st term, d = common difference, l = last term
Sn = {tex}\frac{n}{2}{/tex}(a + l) but l = a + (n - 1)d,
thus Sn = {tex}{n \over 2}{/tex} (a + a + (n - 1)d)
Therefore, sum of the first n terms of an A.P. is given by
Sn = {tex}\frac{n}{2}{/tex}[2a + (n - 1)d]

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Distribution of electrons (i) Electronic configuration
(b) In a neutral atom (ii) Gain or loss of electrons
(c) Ions (iii) Electrons are always equal to protons
(d) Molecule (iv) Stable
questions.explanation:
An atom is always electrically neutral; hence the number of protons is equal to the number of electrons. The distribution of electrons of an atom or a molecule (or other physical structure) in atomic or molecular orbitals is known as the electronic configuration. A molecule is an electrically neutral group of two or more atoms held together by chemical bonds. Molecules are distinguished from ions by their lack of electrical charge. An ion is a charged atom or molecule. It is formed when an atom or a group of atoms lose or gain electrons and get charged. The number of electrons in an ion is not equal to the number of protons.

(a) Distribution of electrons (i) Electronic configuration
(b) In a neutral atom (iii) Electrons are always equal to protons
(c) Ions (ii) Gain or loss of electrons
(d) Molecule (iv) Stable

questions.question:
In K+ and K-:

  1. Number of electrons is the same
  2. Number of protons is the same
  3. Number of neutrons are the same
  4. Number of shells are the same
questions.explanation: Different ions are formed by an atom through the gain or loss of electrons. Hence, the number of protons and neutrons remains the same.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Cell arises from the pre-existing cell was stated by
questions.explanation: Rudolf Virchow presented the idea in 1855 that every cell arises from another (pre-existing) cell. This was an addition to the cell theory that was proposed earlier by Matthias Jakob Schleiden and Theodor Schwann.

questions.question: Which of the following is known as the “physical basis of life”?
questions.explanation: According to Thomas Henry Huxley, the protoplasm is the physical basis of life. It is the living substance inside the cell that contains all the cell organelles. It is a thick fluid or jelly-like substance.

questions.question: The term protoplasm was coined by:
questions.explanation: The term protoplasm was coined by Johann Evangelist Purkinje in 1839. Protoplasm is the fluid medium inside a cell in which different cell organelles are present.

questions.question: Find the incorrect statement:

options.html: Plant cells are smaller than animal cells.
Cell division takes place by fission or budding in the prokaryotic cells.
Blue-green algae are also called cyanobacteria.
Cytosol acts as a store of vital chemicals such as amino acids, glucose, vitamins, ions, etc.

questions.explanation:
Plant cells are generally larger than animal cells. While the sizes can vary depending on the specific cell type and function, plant cells tend to range from 10 to 100 micrometers in diameter, whereas animal cells are typically
smaller, ranging from 10 to 30 micrometers.

questions.question: The longest cell in the human body is:
questions.explanation: The largest cell in the human body is the nerve cell. A nerve cell forms the functional unit of the nervous system in animals for control and coordination.

questions.question: Which cell organelle is found only in plants?
questions.explanation: Plastids are present only in plant cells. Chromoplasts and chloroplasts are coloured plastids whereas leucoplasts are colourless. Chloroplast contains chlorophyll which is essential for photosynthesis in plants. Plastids are absent in animal cells.

questions.question: Who proposed the Cell theory?
questions.explanation: The 'Cell theory' was proposed by Matthias Jakob Schleiden and Theodor Schwann. According to this theory, all living organisms are made up of cells. Rudolf Virchow also contributed to the Cell theory. He proposed that new cells arise from pre-existing cells.

questions.question: The main difference between an animal cell and a plant cell is:
questions.explanation: Plants cells contain chlorophyll which is an essential requirement for photosynthesis. Animal cells lack chlorophyll. They obtain their food from plants. So, a plant cell and an animal cell differ in their modes of nutrition.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response given below:

 (1) Largest cell  (A) Mycoplasma
 (2) Smallest cell  (B) Bacteria
 (3) Single cell  (C) Amoeba
 (4) Prokaryotic cell  (D) Ostrich egg
questions.explanation:
Mycoplasma is a genus of bacteria that lack a cell wall around their cell membrane. Without a cell wall, they are unaffected by many common antibiotics that target cell wall synthesis. Mycoplasma species are the smallest free-living organisms. Prokaryote is a unicellular organism that lacks a membrane-bound nucleus (karyon), mitochondria, or any other membrane-bound organelle. 

(1) Largest cell  (D) Ostrich egg
 (2) Smallest cell  (A) Mycoplasma
 (3) Single cell  (C) Amoeba
 (4) Prokaryotic cell  (B) Bacteria

questions.question:
Which out of the following is living?

  1. Plasma membrane
  2. Cell-wall
  3. Protoplasm
  4. Nucleus
questions.explanation: The plasma membrane, protoplasm, and nucleus are living matter of a cell. Cell-wall is a non-living part of the cell made up of cellulose.

questions.question:
Which of the following is incorrect for Cell theory?

  1. All organisms are made up of cells.
  2. All living organisms are made up of eukaryotic cells only.
  3. Cells arise from pre-existing cells.
  4. The outermost covering of all living cells is the cell wall.
questions.explanation:
Incorrect: All living organisms are made up of eukaryotic cells only. The outermost covering of all living cells is cell wall.
Correct: All organisms are made up of cells. Cells arise from pre-existing cells.
According to Cell theory given by Matthias Jakob Schleiden and Theodor Schwann, all living organisms are made up of cells. Rudolf Virchow had proposed that 'all cells arise from pre-existing cell'. The outermost covering of plant cells is the cell wall. The outermost covering of animal cells is the cell membrane. The cell wall is not present in animal cells. Eukaryotes represent a tiny minority of all living things.

questions.question: A number of chromosomes in a prokaryotic cell is:
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells do not contain a well-defined nucleus. These cells have only one chromosome scattered in the cytoplasm of the cell. Many important genes of prokaryotes are stored in separate circular DNA structures called plasmids.

questions.question: The only cell organelle seen in a prokaryotic cell is:
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are the only cell organelle seen in the prokaryotic cells. Ribosomes in prokaryotes are smaller than those in eukaryotes.

questions.question: Which types of ribosomes are found in eukaryotes?
questions.explanation: Eukaryotic cells contain 80S ribosomes, which are further divided into two unequal subunits designated small subunit (40S) and large subunit (60S) according to their sedimentation coefficients.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Prokaryotic cells (i) Complete cells
(b) Eukaryotic cells (ii) Removal of toxic substances
(c) Viruses (iii) The link between living and non-living
(d) Peroxisomes (iv) Nucleoid
questions.explanation:
• In a prokaryotic cell, the nuclear region is poorly defined due to the absence of a nuclear membrane. Such an undefined nuclear region containing only nucleic acids is called a nucleoid.
  • On the other hand, organisms with cells having a nuclear membrane are called eukaryotes. Eukaryotic cells are considered complete cells.
  • Viruses are the link between the living and the non-living because they lack membranes and do not show any characteristics of life until they enter the body of a host.
  • Peroxisomes are small, membrane-enclosed organelles that contain enzymes involved in a variety of metabolic reactions. They help in the removal of toxic substances from the cell. They are surrounded by only a single membrane.

tests.name: Fundamental Unit 0f Life Test-02

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question:
Eukaryotic cells have:

  1. Distinct nucleus
  2. Nucleolus
  3. Nuclear membrane
  4. Nucleoid
questions.explanation:
Eukaryotic cells have a distinct nucleus. a nucleolus and a nuclear membrane. The presence of a nucleus gives eukaryotes their name. 
Nucleoid is present in prokaryotic cells. It is the area of the cytoplasm that contains the prokaryote's single DNA molecule.

questions.question: The passage of water from a region of higher water concentration through a semi-permeable membrane to a region of lower water concentration is:
questions.explanation: The passage of water from a region of higher water concentration to a region of lower concentration through a semi-permeable membrane is called osmosis. The movement of water across the plasma membrane is affected by the amount of substance dissolved in water.

questions.question: Which of the following is the largest cell organelle in plants?
questions.explanation: Plastid is the largest cell organelle in a plant. It contains chlorophyll necessary for photosynthesis. Plastid is the site of manufacture and storage of important chemical compounds used by the plant cell.

questions.question: Which organelle is called the suicide bag of the cell?
questions.explanation: The lysosomes called the suicide bag of the cell as it contains enzymes for the hydrolysis of all cell components in case of injury. It plays a minor role in cell death. It was discovered and named by Belgian biologist Christian de Duve. De Duve had termed the organelles as the "suicide bags" or "suicide sacs" of the cells. He received the Nobel Prize in Medicine in 1974 for his discovery.

questions.question: _______ acts as a garbage disposal system of the cell.
questions.explanation: Lysosomes present in the cytoplasm of a cell contain very strong enzymes that can break down the organic wastes produced in the cell. They are called a garbage disposal system of the cell.

questions.question: Lysosomes are reservoirs of:
questions.explanation: Lysosomes are the reservoirs of hydrolytic enzymes. These enzymes help in the waste disposal of the cell. Hydrolytic enzymes can hydrolyze the complete cell in case of injury.

questions.question: Which cell organelle is called the digestive bag?
questions.explanation: The lysosomes called a digestive bag as it contains hydrolytic enzymes that help in the digestion of food and hydrolysis of cell constituents. It is also called a "suicide bag".

questions.question: A cell placed in a hypotonic solution will:
questions.explanation: When a cell is placed in a hypotonic solution, the cell swells up due to the endosmosis of water molecules from the region of higher concentration to the region of lower concentration.

questions.question: A solution having a higher concentration than that inside the cell is called:
questions.explanation: A solution having a higher concentration than that inside the cell is known as a hypertonic solution. If a cell is placed in such a solution, exo-osmosis will take place and the cell will shrink.

questions.question: Plasmolysis means:
questions.explanation: Plasmolysis is the shrinkage of cytoplasm due to the movement of water from the cell (loss of water) to the surrounding medium having lesser water concentration. The shrinkage of cytoplasm occurs due to ex-osmosis in a hypertonic medium. A hypertonic solution (outside the cell) is one that has a lesser concentration of water as compared to that inside the cell.

questions.question: In plant cells, ________ perform the function of centrioles.
questions.explanation: Plant cells lack centrosomes or spindle pole bodies which act as the microtubule-organising centers in animal cells. Centrioles are a very important part of centrosomes. The function of chromatid separation in plants is performed by polar cap during cell division.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Self-replicating organelle (i) Neuron
(b) The longest cell of the human body (ii) Chromatin
(c) Coloured Plastid (iii) Chromoplast
(d) The Threadlike structure inside the nucleus (iv) Mitochondria
questions.explanation:
• Mitochondria have their own DNA and ribosomes. They can self-replicate.
  • The longest cells in the human body are neurons. Neurons or nerve cells form the basic component of the nervous system. They have a typical structure.
  • Chromoplasts are coloured plastids. Leucoplasts are white or colourless plastids. Plastids are present only in plant cells. Plastids are similar to mitochondria in the external structure. Like the mitochondria, plastids have their own DNA and ribosomes.
  • Chromatin material is an entangled mass of thread-like structures inside the nucleus. The DNA is present as part of chromatin material which gets organized into chromosomes when the cell is about to divide.

questions.question:
The plasma membrane is called semi-permeable:

  1. It allows the entry of selective substances.
  2. It does not allow entry of any substance.
  3. It allows the exit of selective substances.
  4. It allows entry only.
questions.explanation: Cells are enclosed by a plasma membrane composed of proteins and lipids. It separates the contents of the cell from the external environment. It permits the entry of selective substances in the cell. It allows the exit of selective substances from the cell. The plasma membrane is therefore called a semi-permeable membrane.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the function of Golgi bodies?
questions.explanation: The functions of the Golgi apparatus include the storage, modification, and packaging of products in vesicles. The material synthesized near the ER is packaged and dispatched to various targets inside and outside the cell through the Golgi apparatus. It is also involved in the formation of lysosomes. It helps in the formation of cell plate during cell division but it is not involved in the protection of cells.

questions.question: Chlorophyll is present in:
questions.explanation: Plastids that contain chlorophyll are known as chloroplasts. Inside the chloroplast, chlorophyll is present in Thylakoid. Thylakoid is present inside the grana of the chloroplast of a plant cell. They are the site of the light-dependent reactions of photosynthesis.
