questions.question: Which organelle releases energy?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are called the power house of the cell because it contains enzymes for cellular respiration. During cellular respiration, energy is released which is stored in the form of ATP.

questions.question: What is the full form of ATP?
questions.explanation: The full form of ATP is adenosine triphosphate. ATP is known as the energy currency of the cell. The energy required for various chemical activities is released by mitochondria. They contain enzymes for cellular respiration.

questions.question: Which organelle is called the powerhouse of the cell?
questions.explanation: Mitochondria are called the powerhouses of the cell. The energy required for various chemical activities is released by mitochondria. They contain enzymes for cellular respiration. During cellular respiration, energy is released in the form of ATP (Adenosine triphosphate). The body uses energy stored in ATP.

questions.question: Which of the following imparts colours to flower to attract insects for pollination?
questions.explanation: Plant cells have plastids. Plastids with coloured pigments impart colours to various plant parts. The green colour is provided by chloroplast and other colours (except white) are imparted by chromoplast.

questions.question:
Statement A: The secreted proteins are packed inside the secretory vesicles which are pinched off from the Golgi apparatus.
Statement B: ER is absent in the red blood cells of mammals.
Which of the two statement(s) is/are true?

options.html: Neither statement A nor statement B
Both the statements - A and B

questions.explanation: Both statements are correct. Golgi apparatus consists of a system of membrane-bound vesicles. The proteins manufactured by the ribosomes are packed inside the vesicles. Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER) is absent in the red blood cells of mammals. Red Blood Cells in mammals anucleate when mature i.e. they do not contain a nucleus. This is so that the cell has maximum space for haemoglobin.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Cell-wall (i) Mitochondria
(b) Powerhouse of cell (ii) Ribosomes
(c) Photosynthesis (iii) Chloroplast
(d) Protein synthesis (iv) Non-living
questions.explanation:
Cell-wall is a non-living part of the cell. It is made up of cellulose. 
Mitochondria are called the powerhouses of the cell. The energy required for various chemical activities is released by mitochondria. They contain enzymes for cellular respiration.
Ribosomes are associated with the synthesis of proteins from amino acids. Proteins are required by a cell for functions such as repair or directing chemical processes.
Chloroplasts are found in leaves of plants. They make use of sunlight to perform a process called photosynthesis.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response:

(a) The living membrane surrounding cell (i) Stroma
(b) The single membrane surrounding the vacuole (ii) Plasma membrane
(c) Ground material of chloroplast (iii) Cristae
(d) Fold of inner membrane in mitochondria (iv) Tonoplast
questions.explanation:
Cells are enclosed by a plasma membrane composed of proteins and lipids. It separates the contents of the cell from the external environment. It permits the entry of selective substances in the cell. It allows the exit of selective substances from the cell. 
Tonoplast is the membrane that bounds the chief vacuole of a plant cell. It is also known as the vacuolar membrane. It separates the vacuolar contents from the cytoplasm of the cell.
Stroma is the ground material of chloroplast. It is the part of a tissue or an organ that has a connective and structural role. 
Each of the partial partitions in a mitochondrion formed by the infolding of the inner membrane is known as Cristae.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Lysosomes (i) Smooth endoplasmic reticulum
(b) Centriole (ii) Spindle formation
(c) Processing & packaging of cell secretions (iii) Suicidal bags
(d) Synthesis of lipids (iv) Golgi apparatus
questions.explanation:
• Lysosomes are popularly known as 'suicidal bags'. They contain enzymes for the hydrolysis of all cell components in case of damage. They play a role in cell death. 
  • Centrioles are a very important part of centrosomes or spindle pole bodies that act as the microtubule-organizing centers in animal cells. 
  • Golgi apparatus consists of a system of membrane-bound vesicles. The proteins manufactured by the ribosomes are packed inside the vesicles. 
  • Smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER) helps in the manufacture of fat molecules or lipids. Some of these lipids help in building the cell membrane (known as membrane biogenesis).

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Energy currency of the cell (i) ATP
(b) Organelle which releases oxygen (ii) RBC
(c) Human cells lacking a nucleus (iii) Chloroplast
(d) Synthesis of protein (iv) Ribosomes
questions.explanation:
• The energy required for various chemical activities is released by mitochondria. They contain enzymes for cellular respiration. During cellular respiration, energy is released in the form of ATP (Adenosine triphosphate). The body uses energy stored in ATP. Hence, ATP is called the energy currency of the cell. 
  • Chloroplasts are found in leaves of plants. They make use of sunlight to perform a process called photosynthesis. Chloroplasts are involved in the process of photosynthesis during which oxygen is produced and the net amount is given off. 
  • Red Blood Cells in mammals anucleate when mature i.e. they do not contain a nucleus. This is so that the cell has maximum space for haemoglobin. 
  • Ribosomes are associated with the synthesis of proteins from amino acids. Proteins are required by a cell for functions such as repair or directing chemical processes.

questions.question:
Function(s) of Endoplasmic Reticulum is/are:

  1. Synthesis of proteins
  2. Transfer of hereditary characters
  3. Synthesis of lipids
  4. Processing and packaging
questions.explanation: There are two types of Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER). The Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum (RER) has particles called ribosomes attached to its surface. Ribosomes are the sites of protein manufacture. The smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER) helps in the manufacture of fat molecules or lipids. Some of these proteins and lipids help in building the cell membrane (known as membrane biogenesis). Processing and packaging are done by the Golgi apparatus.

questions.question: Name the cell organelle in which centriole is present.
questions.explanation: In animal cells, centriole is present in the centrosome. Centrioles are a very important part of centrosomes. Centrosomes or spindle pole bodies act as the microtubule organizing centers in animal cells. They help in the movement of chromatids towards the opposite pole.

questions.question: Which structure is called the little nucleus?
questions.explanation: The nucleolus is present in the nucleus of the cell. It is called the little nucleus of the cell.

questions.question: The centriole is associated with:
questions.explanation: Centrioles are a very important part of centrosomes. Centrosomes or spindle pole bodies act as the microtubule organizing centers in animal cells. During cell division in animal cells, spindle formation takes place from the centriole. The spindle help in the movement of chromatids towards the opposite poles.

questions.question: Genes are located on:
questions.explanation: Chromosomes are composed of DNA and protein. Functional segments of DNA are called genes. Genes are functional units of heredity that determine the characters of organisms. In a cell that is not dividing, the DNA material is present as part of chromatin material. When a cell is about to divide, the chromatin material gets organized into chromosomes.

questions.question:
Identify the correct statement:

  1. The double membrane is present in the nucleus.
  2. A liver cell carries about 500-1000 mitochondria.
  3. Wood contains the largest amount of cellulose.
  4. All the statements are correct.
questions.explanation: The nucleus is a double membranous structure. It is surrounded by a double membrane - the inner nuclear membrane and the outer nuclear membrane. A liver cell contains about 500-1000 mitochondria. A large amount of cellulose is present in the wood of a plant.

tests.name: Fundamental Unit 0f Life Test-04
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Fundamental Unit Of Life Test-04 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Which of the following lacks nucleus?
questions.explanation: RBC is called as red blood corpuscles. Red Blood Cells in mammals anucleate when mature i.e. they do not contain a nucleus. This is so that the cell has maximum space for haemoglobin.

questions.question: Animal cell lacking nuclei would also lack in:
questions.explanation: An animal cell that does not contain nuclei will also lack chromosomes. Chromosomes are present inside the nucleus of a cell. Chromosomes contain information for the inheritance of features from parents to the next generation in the form of DNA (Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid) molecules.

questions.question: The number of chromosomes present in the frog is:
questions.explanation: There are 26 chromosomes (13 pairs) in the cells of a frog. These chromosomes are present inside the nucleus of a cell. Chromosomes contain information for the inheritance of features from parents to the next generation in the form of DNA (Deoxyribose Nucleic Acid) molecules.

questions.question: Ribosomes are the centre for:
questions.explanation: Protein synthesis involves the formation of amino acid chains according to information present on the DNA. The ribosomes, which are present in all active cells, are the sites for the manufacture of proteins. The proteins manufactured by the ribosomes are sent to other organelles in the cell. Some proteins function as enzymes and hormones.

questions.question: A cell has 10 chromosomes. After mitotic cell division, the number of chromosomes in the daughter cell will be:
questions.explanation: Mitosis is a part of the cell cycle where replicated chromosomes are separated into two new nuclei. During mitotic division, the number of chromosomes in the daughter cells remains the same. Therefore, a cell having 10 chromosomes will produce daughter cells that have 10 chromosomes each.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Genes (i) Gases
(b) Diffusion (ii) Loss of water by plant cells
(c) Osmosis (iii) Movement of water molecular
(d) Plasmolysis (iv) Hereditary units
questions.explanation:
• Genes are functional units of heredity that determine the characters of organisms.
  • Diffusion is the process of passage of fluid from a region of high concentration to a region of low concentration. It plays an important role in the gaseous exchange between the cells as well as the cell and its external environment. Water also obeys the law of diffusion.
  • The passage of water from a region of higher water concentration to a region of lower water concentration through a semi-permeable membrane is called osmosis. The movement of water across the plasma membrane is affected by the amount of substance dissolved in water.
  • Plasmolysis is a plant cell that refers to the contraction of protoplast as a result of the loss of water from the cell. The shrinkage of cytoplasm occurs due to exo-osmosis in a hypertonic medium. A hypertonic solution is one that has a lesser concentration of water as compared to that inside the cell. During the process, there is a higher external osmotic pressure and a net flow of water from the cell.

questions.question: Where are proteins synthesised inside the cells?
questions.explanation: The Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum (RER) has ribosomes attached to its surface. Ribosomes are the sites of protein manufacture. They are associated with the synthesis of proteins from amino acids.

questions.question: The energy currency of the cell is:
questions.explanation: Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is called as the energy currency of the cell. The energy produced by a cell during internal respiration is stored in the form of ATP molecules. ATP breaks into ADP and phosphate to produce energy during cellular processes.

questions.question: "Viruses are non-cellular organisms" - this statement is a:
questions.explanation: It is true that viruses are non-cellular organisms. Viruses do not contain cell membranes and other cellular organelles. Viruses are the link between the living and the non-living. They do not show any characteristics of life until they enter the body of a host. They then use the host cell machinery for reproduction.

questions.question: The technical term for the “Group of similar cells performing a specific function” is:
questions.explanation: A group of similar cells performing a specific function and having a common origin is called tissue. Tissues form organs to perform a particular function. Organs form organ systems.

questions.question: The number of lenses in a compound microscope is:
questions.explanation: The compound microscope contains two lenses that magnify the object. It uses a lens (called the objective lens) close to the object being viewed to collect light which focuses on a real image of the object inside the microscope. The image is then magnified by a second lens (called the eyepiece) that gives the observer an enlarged inverted virtual image of the object.

questions.question: Amoeba acquires its food through:
questions.explanation: Amoeba acquires its food by the process of endocytosis with the help of finger-like projections called pseudopodia (Pseudo means false; podia means feet). The flexibility of the plasma membrane enables amoeba to use pseudopodia to engulf food and other material from its environment.

questions.question:
Find the incorrect statement:

  1. Root tips are permanent tissue.
  2. Mycoplasma gallisepticum is the smallest cell among the unicellular organisms.
  3. Acetabularia measures nearly 10 cm in height.
  4. Tonoplast is the membrane surrounding the vacuole.
questions.explanation:
1. Root tips are replaced; they are meristematic tissue; and not permanent tissue.
  2. Mycoplasma gallisepticum is the smallest cell amongst the unicellular organisms.
  3. Acetabularia measures nearly 10 cm in height. 
  4. Tonoplast is the membrane that bounds the chief vacuole of a plant cell. It is also known as the 'vacuolar membrane'. It separates the vacuolar contents from the cytoplasm of the cell.

Hence, statement A is incorrect.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Robert Brown (i) Cell
(b) Purkinje (ii) Nucleus
(c) Robert Hooke (iii) Cell theory
(d) Schleiden (iv) Protoplasm
questions.explanation: While the nucleic part of the cell had been observed by Leeuwenhoek in 1682, it was Robert Brown who named it the “cell nucleus”. In 1839, Johann Evangelist Purkinje coined the term 'protoplasm' for the fluid substance of a cell. Robert Hooke observed the microscopic structure of the bark of a cork tree and in doing so, discovered and named the cell – the building block of life. The 'Cell theory' was proposed by Matthias Jakob Schleiden and Theodor Schwann.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response

(1) Large-sized vacuoles (A) Endo-osmosis
(2) Swelling of cells (B) Nucleus
(3) Shrinkage of cell content (C) Hypertonic solution
(4) Organelle that control all the activities (D) Plant cells
questions.explanation: Shrinkage of cell occurs due to exo-osmosis in a hypertonic medium. A hypertonic solution is one which has a lesser concentration of water as compared to that inside the cell. During exo-osmosis, there is a higher external osmotic pressure and a net flow of water from the cell. Swelling of cells occurs due to endo-osmosis. The inward flow of water into the cell containing an aqueous solution through a semi-permeable membrane is called as endo-osmosis. Nucleus is the organelle that control all the activities of a cell. Plant cells have large-sized vacuoles.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Cell-wall (i) Mitochondria
(b) The powerhouse of the cell (ii) Ribosomes
(c) Photosynthesis (iii) Chloroplast
(d) Protein synthesis (iv) Non-living
questions.explanation:
• Cell-wall is made up of cellulose - a non-living substance. Hence, a cell-wall is a non-living part of the cell. 
  • Mitochondria is known as the powerhouse of the cell. They contain enzymes for cellular respiration. During cellular respiration, energy is released in the form of ATP (Adenosine triphosphate). 
  • Photosynthesis is performed by chlorophyll pigments present in the Chloroplast (a plastid). 
  • Ribosomes are associated with the synthesis of proteins from amino acids. Proteins are required by a cell for functions such as repair or directing chemical processes.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Prokaryotic cells (i) The link between living and non-living
(b) Eukaryotic cells (ii) Nucleoid
(c) Viruses (iii) Removal of toxic substances
(d) Peroxisomes (iv) Complete cells
questions.explanation:
• In a prokaryotic cell, the nuclear region is poorly defined due to the absence of a nuclear membrane. Such an undefined nuclear region containing only nucleic acids is called a nucleoid.
  • The organisms with cells having a nuclear membrane are called eukaryotes. Eukaryotic cells are considered complete cells. 
  • Viruses are the link between the living and the non-living because they lack any membranes and do not show any characteristics of life until they enter the body of a host.
  • Peroxisomes are small, membrane-enclosed organelles that contain enzymes involved in a variety of metabolic reactions. They play a key role in the removal of toxic substances from the cell. They are surrounded by only a single membrane.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Genes (i) Loss of water by plant cells
(b) Diffusion (ii) Gases
(c) Osmosis (iii) Hereditary units
(d) Plasmolysis (iv) Movement of water molecules
questions.explanation:
• Genes are functional units of heredity that determine the characters of organisms.
  • Diffusion is the process of passage of fluid from a region of high concentration to a region of low concentration. It plays an important role in the gaseous exchange between the cells as well as the cell and its external environment. Water also obeys the law of diffusion.
  • The passage of water from a region of higher water concentration to a region of lower water concentration through a semi-permeable membrane is called osmosis. The movement of water across the plasma membrane is affected by the amount of substance dissolved in water.
  • Plasmolysis refers to the contraction of protoplast as a result of the loss of water from the cell. The shrinkage of cytoplasm occurs due to exo-osmosis in a hypertonic medium. A hypertonic solution is one that has a lesser concentration of water as compared to that inside the cell. During the process, there is a higher external osmotic pressure and a net flow of water from the cell.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Robert Brown (i) Nucleus
(b) Purkinje (ii) Cell
(c) Robert Hooke (iii) Protoplasm
(d) Schleiden (iv) Cell theory
questions.explanation:
• While the nucleic part of the cell had been observed by Leeuwenhoek in 1682, it was Robert Brown who named it the “cell nucleus”.
  • In 1839, Johann Evangelist Purkinje coined the term 'protoplasm' for the fluid substance of a cell.
  • Robert Hooke observed the microscopic structure of the bark of a cork tree and in doing so, discovered and named the cell – the building block of life.
  • The 'Cell theory' was proposed by Matthias Jakob Schleiden and Theodor Schwann.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Largest cell (i) Mycoplasma
(b) Smallest cell (ii) Bacteria
(c) Single-cell (iii) Amoeba
(d) Prokaryotic cell (iv) Ostrich egg
questions.explanation: A prokaryote is a unicellular organism like bacteria that lacks a membrane-bound nucleus (karyon), mitochondria, or any other membrane-bound organelle. The word prokaryote comes from the Greek language - pro is "before" and karyon is nut or kernel. Mycoplasma is a genus of bacteria that lack a cell wall around their cell membrane. Without a cell wall, they are unaffected by common antibiotics that target cell wall synthesis. Mycoplasma species are the smallest free-living organisms. Ostrich egg is the largest cell.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) The living membrane surrounding cell (i) Stroma
(b) The single membrane surrounding the vacuole (ii) Plasma membrane
(c) Ground material of chloroplast (iii) Cristae
(d) Fold of inner membrane in mitochondria (iv) Tonoplast
questions.explanation:
• Cells are enclosed by a plasma membrane composed of proteins and lipids. It separates the contents of the cell from the external environment.
  • Tonoplast is the membrane that bounds the chief vacuole of a plant cell. It is also known as the 'vacuolar membrane'. It separates the vacuolar contents from the cytoplasm of the cell.
  • Stroma is the part of a tissue or organ that has a connective and structural role.
  • Each of the partial partitions in a mitochondrion formed by the infolding of the inner membrane is known as Cristae.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Lysosomes (i) Golgi apparatus
(b) Centriole (ii) Suicidal bags
(c) Processing & packaging of cell secretions (iii)Smooth endoplasmic reticulum
(d) Synthesis of lipids (iv) Spindle formation
questions.explanation:
• Lysosomes are popularly known as 'suicidal bags'. They play a role in cell death.
  • The main function of the centriole is to help with cell division in animal cells. The centrioles help in the formation of the spindle fibers that separate the chromosomes during cell division (mitosis).
  • The Golgi apparatus is responsible for transporting, modifying, and packaging proteins and lipids into vesicles for delivery to targeted destinations.
  • The smooth ER is involved in the synthesis of lipids.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

 (1) Large sized vacuoles  (A) Plant cells
 (2) Swelling of cells  (B) Hypertonic solution
 (3) Shrinkage of cell content  (C) Endo-osmosis
 (4) Organelle that control all the activities  (D) Nucleus
questions.explanation:
Plant cells have large-sized vacuoles. Swelling of cells happens when the concentration of solutes inside the cell is greater than the concentration of solutes outside the cell. The water enters the cell thorugh Endosmosis (absorption). The shrinkage of cytoplasm occurs due to exo-osmosis in a hypertonic medium.

 (1) Large-sized vacuoles  (A) Plant cells
 (2) Swelling of cells  (C) Endo-smosis
 (3) Shrinkage of cell content  (B) Hypertonic solution
 (4) Organelle that control all the activities  (D) Nucleus

.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

 (1) Energy currency of the cell  (A) Mitochondria
 (2) Organelle which releases oxygen  (B) Ribosomes
(3) Human cells lacking nucleus  (C) RBC
(4) Synthesis of protein  (D) ATP
questions.explanation:
The energy required for various chemical activities is released by mitochondria. They contain enzymes for cellular respiration. During cellular respiration, energy is released in the form of ATP (Adenosine triphosphate). The body uses energy stored in ATP. Hence, ATP is called the energy currency of the cell. Red Blood Cells in mammals anucleate when mature i.e. they do not contain a nucleus. This is so that the cell has maximum space for haemoglobin. Ribosomes are associated with the synthesis of proteins from amino acids. Proteins are required by a cell for functions such as repair or directing chemical processes.

 (1) Energy currency of the cell  (D) ATP
 (2) Organelle which releases oxygen  (A) Mitochondria
 (3) Human cells lacking nucleus  (C) RBC
 (4) Synthesis of protein  (B) Ribosomes

questions.question:
The characteristics of eukaryotic cells are:

  1. Distinct nucleus
  2. Nucleolus
  3. Nuclear membrane
  4. Nucleoid
questions.explanation: Eukaryotic cells (Eu means true; karyon means membrane) have a distinct nucleus, nucleolus, and a nuclear membrane. Nucleoid is a part of prokaryotic cells.

questions.question:
Which of the following is/are living parts?

  1. Plasma membrane
  2. Cell wall
  3. Protoplasm
  4. Nucleus
questions.explanation: The plasma membrane, protoplasm, and nucleus are living parts of a cell. Cell-wall is made up of cellulose and is a non-living part of a plant cell.

questions.question:
The function of Endoplasmic Reticulum is/are:

  1. Synthesis of proteins
  2. Transfer of hereditary characters
  3. Synthesis of lipids
  4. Processing and packaging
questions.explanation: There are two types of Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER). The Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum (RER) has ribosomes attached to its surface. Ribosomes are the sites of protein manufacture. The smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER) helps in the manufacture of fat molecules or lipids. Some of these proteins and lipids help in building the cell membrane (known as membrane biogenesis). The Endoplasmic Reticulum also serves other general functions including the folding of protein molecules in sacs called cisternae and the transport of synthesized proteins in vesicles to the Golgi apparatus.

questions.question:
The plasma membrane is called semi-permeable:

  1. It allows the entry of selective substance.
  2. It does not allow entry of any substance.
  3. It allows the exit of selective substances.
  4. It allows entry only.
questions.explanation: The plasma membrane is called semi-permeable because it is selectively permeable and regulates the entry and exit of substances. It allows entry of selective substances into the cell and allows exit of selective substances from the cell.

questions.question:
Which of the following statement is/are correct:

  1. Chlorophyll is found in the chloroplast.
  2. The nucleus is surrounded by a single membrane called the nuclear membrane.
  3. Plastids are coloured.
  4. Chloroplast contains DNA.
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll is found in the chloroplast (green plastids). Chloroplast contains DNA. In plants, DNA or deoxyribonucleic acid is contained within the membrane-bound cell structures of the nucleus, mitochondria and chloroplasts. All plastids are not coloured. Chromoplasts and chloroplasts are coloured plastids whereas leucoplasts are colourless. The nucleus is surrounded by a double membrane - the inner nuclear membrane and the outer nuclear membrane.

questions.question:
Which of the following is incorrect for cell theory:

  1. All organisms are made up of cells.
  2. All living organisms are made up of eukaryotic the cell only.
  3. Cells arise from pre-existing cells.
  4. The outermost covering of all living cells is the cell wall.
questions.explanation: B and D are incorrect. According to cell theory, all organisms are made up of cells and cells arise from pre-existing cells.

questions.question: Complex tissue consists of:

options.html: Different types of cells carrying out the same function
Same types of cells having the same origin and carry the same function
Different types of cells having the same origin and carry the same function.
Different types of cells carrying out the different function

questions.explanation: Complex tissue consists of different types of cells carrying out the same function. They help in the transportation of organic material, water, and minerals. In plants, xylem and phloem are examples of complex tissues.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(a) Skin (i) Epithelial tissue
(b) Bone (ii) Parenchyma
(c) Bark of tree (iii) Connective tissue
(d) Vascular bundle (iv) Permanent tissue
questions.explanation:
(a) Skin (i) Epithelial tissue
(b) Bone (iii) Connective tissue
(c) Bark of tree (ii) Parenchyma 
(d) Vascular bundle (iv) Permanent tissue

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.
(1) Meristematic tissue
(2) Epidermal tissue
(3) Connective tissue
(4) Nervous tissue

(A) Outer surface of plants
(B) Conduct message
(C) Blood and bones
(D) Divides rapidly
questions.explanation: Meristematic tissue divides rapidly. Epidermal tissue are the outer surface of plants. Blood and bones are connective tissues. Through nervous tissues messages are conducted.

questions.question: The non-living cell of phloem is:
questions.explanation: Sieve tubes are specialized phloem cells that lack a nucleus and most other cellular organelles when mature, making them non-living in terms of their function.

questions.question: Which of the following plant tissues do not possess living protoplasm at maturity?
questions.explanation: Sclerenchyma is permanent tissue present in different parts of plants. They are thick-walled and do not possess living protoplasm at maturity because these are dead.

questions.question: Phloem in plants performs the function of:
questions.explanation: Phloem in plants is responsible for the conduction of food prepared in leaves to all other parts of plants. Sieve tubes and companion cells are mainly responsible for the transport of food in the phloem.

questions.question:
Where is the apical meristem found?

  1. Root tip 
  2. Shoot tip
questions.explanation: Apical Meristematic tissues are found in root tips, shoot tips to increase the length of root, stem.

questions.question: In desert plants, the rate of water loss gets reduced due to the presence of:
questions.explanation: In desert plants, the rate of water loss gets reduced due to the presence of a cuticle on the leaf's surface. Water is mostly lost in the form of water vapour during transpiration.

questions.question:
Find the incorrect statement:

  1. Cambium has the apical meristem.
  2. In monocot stems, the intercalary meristem is located at the base of the internodes.
  3. Totipotancy exists in Meristematic cells.
  4. Parenchyma contains isodiametric cells.
questions.explanation: Totipotancy is the ability of plant parts to develop the whole plant. In monocot stems, the intercalary meristem is located at the base of internodes and parenchyma contains isodiametric cells but cambium has no apical meristem.

questions.question: Collenchyma differs from sclerenchyma in:
questions.explanation: Collenchyma consists of cells having thick-walled cells only on the corner but sclerenchyma has thick-walled cells devoid of cytoplasm. So, collenchyma differs from sclerenchyma in retaining cytoplasm at maturity.

questions.question: How many types of elements together make up the xylem tissue?
questions.explanation: Xylem tissue consists of tracheids, vessels, xylem parenchyma and xylem fibres. The cells have thick walls, and many of them are dead cells. Tracheids and vessels are tubular structures. This allows them to transport water and minerals vertically. The parenchyma stores food and helps in the sideways conduction of water. Fibres are mainly supportive in function.

questions.question: Latex of which of the following plants yield chewing gum?
questions.explanation: Latex of Achras sapota plants yields chewing gum. Latex is obtained by making small cuts in the stem to secrete juice like latex.

questions.question: Grass stem elongates by the activity of:
questions.explanation: Grass belongs to the monocots group of angiosperms in which secondary growth does not take place. The elongation of a stem takes place by the activity of the intercalary meristem.

questions.question: Cork is impervious to water because it has deposition of ______.
questions.explanation: Cork forms the outer protective dead tissue of epidermis in dicots. Cork is impervious to water because it has deposition of suberin and cells are compactly packed.

questions.question: The cell walls of collenchyma tissues are unevenly thickened due to
questions.explanation: The collenchyma is the typical supporting tissue of the primary plant body and growing plant parts, though it is kept with unaltered structure and function even in outgrown organs like stems, petioles, laminae or roots. The walls of collenchyma cells are strengthened by the deposit of cellulose and the coating with pectin. These strengthenings are often restricted to single parts or edges of the cell.

tests.name: Tissues Test-02
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Tissues Test-02 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.

  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Which of the following is the role of the epidermis in plants?

options.html: Formation of insulation stationary air layer with the help of hair.

questions.explanation: The epidermis in plants provides protection, regulation of transpiration, and formation of insulation stationary air layer with the help of hair on the surface of it.

questions.question: Which tissue makes up the husk of coconut?
questions.explanation: The husk of the coconut is made up of sclerenchyma tissue. Sclerenchyma is simple permanent tissue that provides mechanical support to parts of the plant.

questions.question: Name the regions in which parenchyma tissue present.
questions.explanation: Parenchyma tissue is present in all parts of plants that include cortex, flower, stem, leaves, and pith. These tissues are thin-walled and living.

questions.question: Parenchyma, Collenchyma, and sclerenchyma are:
questions.explanation: Parenchyma, collenchyma, and sclerenchyma are simple permanent tissue present in different parts of plants. Parenchyma and collenchyma are living but sclerenchymas are dead cells.

questions.question: Which type of meristem is responsible for growth in length of the root?
questions.explanation: Apical meristem is present on the root and shoot tips that are responsible for growth in length of root and stem.

questions.question:
Identify the incorrect statement:

  1. The common name of xylem is bust.
  2. sclerenchyma is used for making ropes
  3. Companion cell is attached to the lateral side of the sieve tube
  4. Cambium is a lateral meristem.
questions.explanation: The common name of xylem is wood.

questions.question: Which chemical is deposited on the cell wall of sclerenchyma?
questions.explanation: The chemical deposited on the wall of sclerenchyma is lignin. This deposition of lignin on the wall makes these tissues impervious to water and air.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response

(a) Aerenchyma (i) Permanent tissue
(b) Parenchyma (ii) Thin-walled packing cells
(c) Chlorenchyma (iii) Buoyancy
(d) Cork (iv) Photosynthesis
questions.explanation:
• Aerenchyma provides buoyancy.
  • Parenchyma cells are thin-walled.
  • Chlorenchyma is responsible for photosynthesis in green plants.
  • Cork is a permanent tissue.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response

(a) Collenchyma (i) Grit cells
(b) Sclereids (ii) Conduction
(c) Sclerenchyma (iii) Lignin deposition
(d) Xylem and phloem (iv) Flexibility in plants
questions.explanation:
• The tissue which is responsible for flexibility in plants is collenchyma. 
  • Sclereids are also called grit cells.
  • Mature sclerenchyma cells are dead cells that have heavily thickened walls containing lignin. 
  • Xylem distributes water and phloem distributes food throughout the plant's body.

questions.question:
Which of the following activities are due to voluntary muscles?

  1. Jumping of frog
  2. Pumping of the heart
  3. Writing with hand
  4. Movement of chocolate in your intestine
questions.explanation:
1. Jumping of the frog is the activity of voluntary muscles as it is controlled by skeletal muscles.
  2. Pumping of heart involuntary, i.e., it is controlled by cardiac muscles.
  3. Writing with the hand is voluntary muscle action.
  4. Movement of chocolate in your intestine is involuntary muscles.

So, (a) and (c) statements are correct.

questions.question:
Primary growth in plants occurs by:

  1. Apical meristem
  2. Intercalary meristem
  3. Lateral meristem
  4. Vertical meristem
questions.explanation:
Primary growth in plants occurs by both the apical meristem and intercalary meristem.
Apical meristem is present on root apex, stem apex, leaf buds, and flower buds. They are responsible for the growth in length, i.e. primary growth. Intercalary meristem act along with other types of meristem to increase the length of the leaves and stems. They are mainly found at the base of leaves and nodes and internodes.
Lateral meristem is present along the side of the stem. They are responsible for growth in girth, i.e. secondary growth. Vertical meristem is also responsible for secondary growth.
So, In (a) and (b) Primary growth occur.

questions.question: Find out the incorrect sentence:

options.html: Apical and intercalary meristems are permanent tissues.
Meristematic tissues, in its early stage, lack vacuoles.
collenchymatous tissues are irregularly thickened at corners.

questions.explanation:
• Parenchyma serves as a packing tissue in plants therefore they have intercellular spaces.
  • Collenchymatous tissues are mechanical tissues in the plants and are characterized by the deposition of cellulose at the corners of the cell, which leads to localized thickenings of the cell wall.
  • Apical and intercalary meristem bring primary growth (increase in height) and secondary growth (increase in diameter) respectively and are classified under meristematic tissues.
  • Meristematic tissue is dividing units of the plants and contains dense cytoplasm and large nucleus with few or no vacuoles at all.

So, statements A, B, and D are a correct statement and statement C is an incorrect statement.

questions.question: White fibers of connective tissue are made up of:
questions.explanation: White fibers of connective tissue are made up of collagen fibre. These fibers are responsible for the contractile nature of connective tissues.

questions.question: Smooth muscles occur in:
questions.explanation: There are three kinds of muscle found in animals. Smooth muscle, skeletal muscles, and cardiac muscles. Smooth muscles are present in the uterus, artery, and veins.

questions.question: Ciliated epithelium is found in
questions.explanation: Ciliated columnar epithelium moves mucus and other substance via cilia as found in the upper respiratory tract, the fallopian tubes, the uterus, and the central part of the spinal cord. They are the primary target of infection for “common cold viruses” such as coronaviruses, influenza virus and rhinoviruses.

  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Bone consists of cells called:
questions.explanation: Bone consists of cells called Osteocytes. Bone is a specialized connective tissue. Osteocytes are present between the lamellae in fluid-filled spaces called lacunae.

questions.question: What is the main function of RBC?

options.html: Helps in the transport of CO
Helps in the transport of oxygen and CO<sub>2</sub>
Helps in the transport of CO<sub>2</sub>
Helps in the transport of oxygen

questions.explanation: The primary function of red blood cells, or erythrocytes, is to carry oxygen from the lungs to the body tissues and carbon dioxide as a waste product, away from the tissues and back to the lungs.

questions.question: Name the part of neuron which carries impulses from cyton.
questions.explanation: Nervous tissue consists of nerve cells or neurons. Dendrite present on the cell body receives a stimulus and cyton converts stimulus into nerve impulses and carries impulses through the axon.

questions.question: Which of the following muscle gets fatigued very soon?
questions.explanation: Skeletal muscles get fatigued very soon. Skeletal muscles are responsible for movement in different parts of the body.

questions.question: Which type of WBC is most abundant in lymph?
questions.explanation: Lymphocytes of white blood cells are most abundant in the lymph. Lymph is the extracellular fluid that contains WBC but RBC in it.

questions.question: Name the tissue responsible for the movement of our body.
questions.explanation: Movement in our body is mainly caused by muscular tissue. Skeletal muscular tissues are joined with bones that cause movement due to contractile nature.

questions.question: Fibers are absent in:
questions.explanation: Fibers are absent in blood since blood flows through very thin capillaries, fibers are absent in it. But the liver, kidney, and heart contain fibers.

questions.question: A sprain is caused by excessive pulling of:
questions.explanation: A sprain is caused by excessive pulling of ligaments joined with muscle cells.

questions.question: Haversian canal is present in:
questions.explanation: Haversian canal is present in bone. Haversian canals are present in the center of osteocytes surrounded by coiled lamellae called lacunae.

questions.question: The efferent part of the neuron is:
questions.explanation: Axon is the part of the neuron that transfers the nerve impulse away from neuron cells. Axon is called efferent part or neuron.

questions.question: Fibrinogen is absent in:
questions.explanation: Fibrinogen is present in blood that is responsible for the clotting of blood in case of injury. The serum does not contain fibrinogen.

questions.question: Haversian canal is present in:
questions.explanation: Haversian canal is present in bone. Haversian canals are present in the center of osteocytes surrounded by coiled lamellae called lacunae.

questions.question: Cartilage is not found in:
questions.explanation: Cartilage is the flexible connective tissue that provides support to the different parts along with flexibility. It is present on nose tips, external ear, and larynx but absent in kidney.

questions.question: Name the connective tissue with a fluid matrix.
questions.explanation: Blood is the connective tissue with a fluid matrix. Blood consists of formed cells and plasma as a matrix.

questions.question: Which type of tissue is present in the lining of the kidney tubule?
questions.explanation: The kidney of human beings is made up of a large number of the nephron. Nephrons are made up of long tubules. These tubules are made up of epithelial tissue for the absorption of water.

tests.instructionHtml:
This is Tissues Test-04 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Which of the following tissue is known as packaging tissue?
questions.explanation: Alveolar tissue is known as packaging tissue. These tissues are responsible for storing materials not required by our body at a particular time like glycogen.

questions.question: Heart muscles are:
questions.explanation: Heart muscles are called cardiac muscles. These muscles are involuntary, striated, and uninucleate. Cardiac tissue pumps blood from birth till death.

questions.question: Squamous epithelium is found in:
questions.explanation: Squamous epithelium tissue is found in alveoli of lung and other parts of plants where regular contraction and relaxation takes place.

questions.question:
Statement A: Stratified squamous epithelium occurs in the buccal cavity, Pharynx, etc.
Statement B: Germinal epithelium produces male and female sex cells.
Which of the statement is true?
questions.explanation:
Stratified squamous epithelium occurs in areas where there is regular wear and tear occur in the buccal cavity, pharynx, skin, etc.
The germinal epithelium produces the egg cells.  Produce the sex hormones estrogen and progesterone. Once females mature sexually, an egg cell is produced each month and released during ovulation. Germinal epithelium produces male and female sex cells.
Statement A and B are true statements.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response

(a) Adipose tissue (i) Subcutaneous layer
(b) Stratified squamous epithelium (ii) Skeletal muscles
(c) Striated muscles (iii) Skin
(d) Surface of joints (iv) Cartilage
questions.explanation:
• Adipose tissue works as a subcutaneous layer.
  • Stratified squamous epithelium is present in the skin.
  • Striated muscle present in the skeletal muscle. skeletal muscle is striated; its long, thin, multinucleated fibers are crossed with a regular pattern of fine red and white lines, giving the muscle a distinctive appearance.
  • The surface of joints in cartilage.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response

(a) Tendons (i) Osteocytes
(b) Ligaments (ii) Yellow fibres
(c) Cartilage (iii) White fibres
(d) Bones (iv) Chondrocytes
questions.explanation:
• Tendons are white fibrous tissue.
  • Ligaments are the modification of yellow elastic tissue with some collagen fibres.
  • Chondrocytes are the only cells found in healthy cartilage.
  • Osteocyte is the most abundant cell type of bone.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response

(a) RBC (i) Alternate light & dark bands
(b) Striated muscles (ii) Neuron
(c) Smooth muscles (iii) Uninucleate
(d) Axon (iv) Transport oxygen
questions.explanation:
• The primary function of red blood cells is to transport oxygen from the lungs to the cells of the body.
  • Striated muscles contain alternate light and dark bands.
  • Smooth muscles are composed of uninucleate cells. 
  • An axon is a long, slender projection of a nerve cell or neuron.

questions.question:
The function of areolar tissue is:

  1. It supports internal organs
  2. It stores fat
  3. It helps in repair of tissues
  4. It protects internal organs
questions.explanation:
Areolar Tissue:  It consists of a matrix, several types of cell, collagen, and elastin fibres. They are found between the skin and the muscles around blood vessels and nerves and in the bone marrow.
The function of areolar tissue is

  1. It fills the space inside the organ.
  2. It supports the internal organ.
  3. It helps in the repair of the tissues.

questions.question: Name a basic tissue present in plants but absent in animals.
questions.explanation:
The animal body is made of four different types of tissues.

  1. Epithelial Tissue
  2. Muscle Tissue
  3. Connective Tissue
  4. Nerve Tissue

Plant tissues are basically divided into two: Meristematic tissue and Permanent tissue.
So, Meristematic tissue is present in plant cells but absent in animal cells.

questions.question:
Statement A: Collagen occurs in yellow elastic fibre.
Statement B: Sclereids form a gritty part of ripe fruits.
Which of the two statements is/are true?
questions.explanation:
• A collagen is actually a group of proteins found naturally in the body. Elastic or yellow cartilage contains elastic fiber networks and collagen fibers.
  • Sclereids from the gritty part of most of the ripe fruits and contribute hardness to the seed coat and nutshells.

Statement A and B are true statements.

questions.question:
Statement A: Cuboidal epithelium is located in salivary glands.
Statement B: Collenchymas are lignified elongated dead cells.
Which of the two statements is/are true?
questions.explanation: Cuboidal epithelium is located in salivary glands. Collenchyma is simple permanent tissue thickened at the corner of the cells but they are not lignified elongated and dead cells.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response

(a) Parenchyma (i) Conduct prepared food
(b) Phloem (ii) Muscular tissue
(c) Stomach (iii) An organ
(d) heart (iv) Storage of food in plants
questions.explanation:
• Parenchyma is a thing that stores food in plants.
  • Phloem conduct prepared food in plants.
  • The stomach is an organ.
  • The heart is a muscular tissue.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(1) Root tip (A) Fluid connective tissue
(2) Cell (B) Consist of meristematic tissue
(3) Blood (C) Grow & Reproduce
(4) Living organisms (D) Structural & functional unit of life
questions.explanation:
• The root tip consists of meristematic tissue.
  • The cell is a structural and functional unit of life.
  • Blood is a fluid connective tissue.
  • Living organisms grow and reproduce.

questions.question:
Upper and lower layer of cells in leaves

  1. Protect the inner parts
  2. Synthesise food
  3. Regulate the water content
  4. Exchange the gases
questions.explanation:
1. Leaves have an outer layer that protects them. This outermost layer is called the cuticle. It is generally waxy to protect the leaf and prevent water loss.
  2. The network of veins supports the leaf and transport water and carbohydrates.
  3. Guard Cells surrounding stomata that control the rate of gas and water exchange. Stomata opening between guard cells for gas and water exchange.

So, (a), (c) and (d) are a function of upper and lower layers of cells in leaves.

questions.question: The cells that do not lose their nucleus at maturity are:
questions.explanation: Among these four only companion cells have a nucleus. The other three cells do not have a nucleus in maturation. Companion cells are the cell that does not lose their nucleus at maturity.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Skin (i) Connective tissue
(b) Bone (ii) Permanent tissue
(c) Bark of tree (iii) Parenchyma
(d) Vascular bundle (iv) Epithelial tissue
questions.explanation:
• Skin is made up of epidermal cells.
  • Bone is connective tissue.
  • The barks of tissue are made up of parenchyma.
  • Vascular bundles are permanent tissues.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Meristematic tissue (i) Blood and bones
(b) Epidermal tissue (ii) Divides rapidly
(c) Connective tissue (iii) Conducts message
(d) Nervous tissue (iv) The outer surface of plants
questions.explanation:
• Meristematic tissue is composed of rapidly dividing the undifferentiated mass of cells.
  • The epidermis is the outermost layer of the plant body.
  • Blood and bones are types of connective tissues.
  • Nervous tissue functions to conduct messages throughout the body.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Parenchyma (i) Muscular tissue
(b) Phloem (ii) Conduct prepared food
(c) Stomach (iii) Storage of food in plants
(d) heart (iv) An organ
questions.explanation:
• Parenchyma is concerned with the storage of food in the plants.
  • Phloem conduct foods to all part of plants.
  • The stomach is an organ.
  • The heart is muscular tissue.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Root tip (i) Consist of meristematic tissue
(b) Cell (ii) Fluid connective tissue
(c) Blood (iii) The structural and functional unit of life
(d) Living organisms (iv) Grow and Reproduce
questions.explanation:
• The root tip consists of meristematic tissue.
  • The cell is a structural and functional unit of life.
  • Blood is fluid connective tissue.
  • Living organisms grow and reproduce.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Adipose tissue (i) Skin
(b) Stratified squamous epithelium (ii) Cartilage
(c) Striated muscles (iii) Skeletal muscles
(d) Surface of joints (iv) Subcutaneous layer
questions.explanation:
• Adipose tissue is present in the subcutaneous layer.
  • Stratified squamous epithelium is present in the skin.
  • Striated muscles are skeletal muscles.
  • The main component of the joint surface is a special tissue called hyaline cartilage.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(a) Tendons (i) Chondrocytes
(b) Ligaments (ii) White fibres
(c) Cartilage (iii) Osteocytes
(d) Bones (iv) Yellow fibres
questions.explanation:
• Tendons are white fibers.
  • Ligaments are yellow fibers.
  • Chondrocytes are the only cells found in healthy cartilage. 
  • Bones contain osteocytes.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Aerenchyma (i) Permanent tissue
(b) Parenchyma (ii) Thin-walled packing cells
(c) Chlorenchyma (iii) Buoyancy
(d) Cork (iv) Photosynthesis
questions.explanation:
• In hydrophytes such as water hyacinth, hydrilla, etc., large air cavities are present in parenchyma to give buoyancy to plant, such type of parenchyma is called aerenchyma.  
  • Parenchyma is thin-walled cells found in soft parts of the plant.
  • Chlorenchyma is responsible for photosynthesis.
  • Cork is a permanent tissue.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Collenchyma (i) Flexibility in plants
(b) Sclereids (ii) Lignin deposition
(c) Sclerenchyma (iii) Grit cells
(d) Xylem & phloem (iv) Conduction
questions.explanation:
• The tissue which is responsible for flexibility in plants is Collenchyma. 
  • Sclereids are called grit cells. 
  • Lignin deposition takes place in sclerenchyma.
  • Xylem and phloem involved in the conduction of water and the food.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) RBC (i) Alternate light & dark bands
(b) Striated muscles (ii) Neuron
(c) Smooth muscles (iii) Uninucleate
(d) Axon (iv) Transport oxygen
questions.explanation:
• RBC transport oxygen in the body through blood.
  • Striated muscles have alternate light & dark bands. 
  • Smooth muscles are composed of uninucleate cells.
  • Axon is a long, slender projection of a neuron.

questions.question:
Which of the following activities involves voluntary muscles-

  1. Jumping of frog
  2. Pumping of the heart
  3. Writing with hand
  4. Movement of food in the stomach
questions.explanation: Jumping of frog and writing with hand, both the action done willingly. Hence done by voluntary muscles.

questions.question:
The function of the upper and lower layer of cells in leaves is/are

  1. Protect the inner parts
  2. Synthesize food
  3. Regulate the water content
  4. Exchange the gases
questions.explanation: Protection of inner parts of leaves, exchange of gases, and regulation of water is done by the upper and lower layers of cells while photosynthesis is done in the mesophyll.

questions.question:
Which of the following types of meristems constitutes in the primary growth of the plant?

  1. Apical meristem
  2. Intercalary meristem
  3. Lateral meristem
  4. Vertical meristem
questions.explanation: Primary growth in plants takes place due to the division in the apical meristem (growth of root and shoot) and intercalary meristem (branching).

questions.question:
Which of the following does not lose their nucleus at maturity?
A. Red blood cells
B. Vessel 
C. Companion cells
D.Sieve tube cells
questions.explanation: Companion cells are  metabolically active and sieve tube elements are dependent on these cells they do not loose nucleus at maturity. RBCs , vessels and sieve tube cells loose their nucleus at maturity.

questions.question:
Find out the correct sentence:

  1. Parenchymatous tissues have intercellular spaces.
  2. Collenchymatous tissues are irregularly thickened at corners.
  3. Apical and intercalary meristems are permanent tissues.
  4. Meristematic tissues, in its early stage, lack vacuoles.
questions.explanation: Meristematic tissue takes up a specific role and loses their ability to divide and then form a permanent tissue.

questions.question:
The function of areolar tissue is

  1. It supports internal organs
  2. It stores fat
  3. It helps in the repair of tissues
  4. It protects internal organs
questions.explanation: Areolar tissue fills the spaces inside the organ, supports internal organs, and repair the tissues.

This is Diversity in Living Organisms Test-01 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: In which phylum, nervous system develops for the first time?
questions.explanation: The nervous system is responsible for control and coordination in animals. The nervous system develops for the first time in phylum Coelenterates. A nerve net consists of interconnected neurons lacking a brain. While organisms with bilateral body symmetry are normally associated with a central nervous system, organisms with radial symmetry are associated with nerve nets. The nerve net is the simplest form of a nervous system found in multicellular organisms. Coelenterates are the first two phyla to possess nervous systems that differentiate during development and communicate by synaptic conduction. Most research on the evolution of nervous tissue concerning nerve nets has been conducted using cnidarians. The nervous systems of coelenterates allow for sensation, contraction, locomotion, and hunting/feeding behaviors. Coelenterates and bilaterians share common neurophysiological mechanisms.

questions.question: Pinna is a characteristic feature of:
questions.explanation: Pinna is the external opening of ears. Pinna is a characteristic feature of Mammalia. The pinna is the visible part of the ear that resides outside of the head. The purpose of the pinna is to collect sound. It does so by acting as a funnel, amplifying the sound and directing it to the ear canal. In mammals like cats and dogs, it is obviously highly directional, but even in humans, the pinna functions as a directional sound collector. The "sweet spot" for which the pinna best collects sound is called the acoustic axis or the acoustic fovea. In various species, the pinna can also signal mood and radiate heat.

questions.question: 5- kingdom classification was proposed by:
questions.explanation: The five kingdoms of classification was proposed by R. Whittaker. These kingdoms were Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae, and Animalia.R.H. Whittaker (1969) proposed a Five Kingdom Classification. The kingdoms defined by him were named Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae, and Animalia. The main criteria for classification used by him include cell structure, thallus organization, mode of nutrition, reproduction, and phylogenetic relationships.

questions.question: Name the group of organisms that have dicondylic skull.
questions.explanation: The skull has two occipital condyles is called dicondylic skull. The members of class amphibians and mammals possess dicondylic skull. Skull has two occipital condyles, with the help of these two condyles skull is connected by the first vertebra of the vertebral column i.e, Atlas.

questions.question: In Whittaker’s classification, unicellular organisms are grouped under ________.
questions.explanation: The Whittaker’s classification includes five-kingdom in which unicellular organisms having Eukaryotic cells are placed in the kingdom Protista. Protists are eukaryotic organisms that cannot be classified as a plant, animal, or fungus. They are mostly unicellular, but some, like algae, are multicellular. Kelp, or 'seaweed,' is a large multicellular Protista that provides food, shelter, and oxygen for numerous underwater ecosystems. Even though kelp resembles a plant, it is not classified into Kingdom Plantae because it lacks the cellular complexity of plant cells. Protists can be heterotrophic, which means they obtain the energy they need to live by consuming other organisms. Or, they can be autotrophic, which means they obtain energy from the environment through photosynthesis, the process of capturing light energy, and storing it in carbohydrates.

questions.question: Polymorphism is exhibited by:
questions.explanation:
Polymorphism is the occurrence of different forms of types of individuals among the members of a single species. It is shown by species Physalia. 
Coelenterate animals may show a number of zooids. They are of different forms. They take up different functions. These are called polymorphisms. This phenomenon is called polymorphism. (Such a colony is called 'Polymorphic' colony). Polymorphism denotes a division of labor among the zooids of the individual. Polymorphism is one of the
Coelenterates animal characteristic feature. A polymorphic colony contains many individuals called zooids. They are mainly two types.1. Medusae 2. Polyps.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(a) Monera (i) Unicellular, eukaryotic
(b) Protista (ii) Multicellular, autotrophic cell wall
(c) Fungi (iii) Multicellular, heterotrophic, cell wall
(d) Plantae (iv) Prokaryotic
questions.explanation:
• Monera means single was a kingdom that contained unicellular organisms with a prokaryotic cell organization (having no nuclear membrane), such as bacteria. They are single-celled organisms with no true nuclear membrane in a prokaryotic organism.
  • Protists are eukaryotic organisms that cannot be classified as a plant, animal, or fungus. They are mostly unicellular, but some, like algae, are multicellular. Kelp, or 'seaweed,' is a large multicellular protist that provides food, shelter, and oxygen for numerous underwater ecosystems.
  • A fungus is any member of the group of eukaryotic organisms that includes microorganisms such as yeasts and moulds as well as the more familiar mushrooms. These organisms are classified as a kingdom Fungi, which is separate from the other eukaryotic life kingdoms of plants and animals A characteristic that places fungi in a different kingdom from plants, bacteria, and some protists is chitin in their cell walls. Fungi are multicellular and heterotrophs in nature.
  • Plants are mainly multicellular, predominantly photosynthetic eukaryotes of the kingdom Plantae. The term is today generally limited to the green plants. This includes the flowering plants and other gymnosperms, ferns, hornworts, mosses, and green algae, and excludes the red and blue algae. Green plants have cell walls containing cellulose and obtain most of their energy from sunlight by photosynthesis by primary chloroplasts, derived from endosymbiosis with cyanobacteria.

questions.question:
Which of the following is not correctly matched

  1. Father of taxonomy - Mendel
  2. Five kingdom classification - R.H. Whittaker
  3. Binomial nomenclature-Darwin
  4. Two kingdom classification - Linnaeus
questions.explanation:
• Carl Linnaeus. Carl Linnaeus, also known as Carl von Linné or Carolus Linnaeus, is often called the Father of Taxonomy. His system for naming, ranking, and classifying organisms is still in wide use today (with many changes).
  • The formal introduction of the Binomial system of naming species is credited to Carl Linnaeus, effectively beginning with his work Species Plantarum. Linnean system of binomial nomenclature, the scientific way to name living things with a generic (genus) and specific (species) name.
  • Gregor Mendel, who is known as the "father of modern genetics", gained posthumous recognition as the founder of the modern science of genetics.
  • Charles Robert Darwin was an English naturalist, geologist, and biologist best known for his contributions to the science of evolution.

questions.question: Choanocytes are characteristic of:
questions.explanation: Choanocytes are cells that line the interior of asconoid, syconoid, and leuconoid body type. They are characteristic of the Porifera kingdom. Choanocytes (also known as "collar cells") are cells that line the interior of asconoid, syconoid, and leuconoid body type sponges that contain a central flagellum, or undulipodia, surrounded by a collar of microvilli which are connected by a thin membrane. They make up Choanoderm, a type of cell layer found in sponges.

questions.question: Give one term for the branch of biological science concerned with grouping and naming organisms.
questions.explanation: The branch of biological science involves identifying, grouping, and naming of organisms is called taxonomy. Taxonomy is a branch of science that encompasses the description, identification, nomenclature, and classification of organisms. Alpha taxonomy, the description, and basic classification of new species, subspecies, and other taxa. Carl Linnaeus is considered as Father of Taxonomy.

questions.question: In which kingdom will you place an organism which is single-celled, eukaryotic, and photosynthetic?
questions.explanation: The organism which is single-celled; eukaryotic, and photosynthetic are placed in the kingdom Protista. Protists are eukaryotic organisms that cannot be classified as a plant, animal, or fungus. They are mostly unicellular, but some, like algae, are multicellular. Kelp, or 'seaweed,' is a large multicellular protist that provides food, shelter, and oxygen for numerous underwater ecosystems. Even though kelp resembles a plant, it is not classified into Kingdom Plantae because it lacks the cellular complexity of plant cells. Protists can be heterotrophic, which means they obtain the energy they need to live by consuming other organisms. Or, they can be autotrophic, which means they obtain energy from the environment through photosynthesis, the process of capturing light energy, and storing it in carbohydrates.

questions.question: Find the incorrect pair:
questions.explanation: Leech belongs to Phylum Annelida, Octopus belongs to phylum Mollusca, and liver fluke belongs to phylum Chordate but Starfish belongs to Phylum Echinodermata. Starfish or sea stars are star-shaped echinoderms belonging to the class Asteroidea. Starfish are marine invertebrates. They typically have a central disc and five arms, though some species have a larger number of arms. The aboral or upper surface may be smooth, granular or spiny, and is covered with overlapping plates.

questions.question:
Find the incorrect pair:

  1. Class Amphibia - Dry and non-glandular skin with feathers.
  2. Class Reptilia - Dry and non-glandular skin with scales.
  3. Class Mammalia - Glandular skin with hairs
  4. Class Aves - Dry and non-glandular skin with feathers.
questions.explanation:
Class Reptilia have organisms having dry and non-glandular skin with scales. Mammals have glandular skin with hairs. Aves have dry and non-glandular skin with feathers. Amphibians have glandular skin without feathers.
Amphibians are ectothermic, tetrapod vertebrates of the class Amphibia. They inhabit a wide variety of habitats, with most species living within terrestrial.

questions.question: Name the plant structure, which helps in attaching the bryophytes to the substratum?
questions.explanation: Bryophytes do not have a well-defined stem, root, and leaves. Bryophytes get attached to substratum with the help of rhizoid, which is a fibrous structure. Rhizoids are protuberances that extend from the lower epidermal cells of bryophytes. They are similar in structure and function to the root hairs of vascular land plants. Similar structures are formed by some fungi Rhizoids may be unicellular or multicellular. Rhizoids are multicellular organs composed of multiple tissues that collectively carry out a common function.

questions.question: Which of the following is the dicot plant?
questions.explanation: The angiosperm plants in which seeds contain two dicotyledons are called as dicot plant. Brassica Campestris (mustard) is a dicot plant out of the given plants. Brassica is a genus of plants in the mustard family. The members of the genus are informally known as cruciferous vegetables, cabbages, or mustard plants. The genus Brassica is known for its important agricultural and horticultural crops and includes a number of weeds,  both of wild taxa and escapees from cultivation. Brassica species and varieties commonly used for food nutritional values.
