questions.question:
Identify the incorrect statement.

  1. The spring force always pushes a body.
  2. The weight of a body always acts towards the centre of the Earth.
  3. The tension of a string always pulls a body.
  4. Frictional force always acts parallel to the surface of contact.
questions.explanation: The spring force is the force exerted by a compressed or stretched spring upon any object that is attached to it. An object that compresses or stretches a spring is always acted upon by a force that restores the object to its rest or equilibrium position. So, the spring force does not always push a body.

questions.question: How much force acts on the body whose momentum is constant (i.e. = constant)?
questions.explanation: Force is the rate of change of momentum. So, momentum is constant when force is zero.

questions.question: How much force acts on the body whose momentum is constant (i.e. = constant)?
questions.explanation: Force is the rate of change of momentum. So, momentum is constant when force is zero.

questions.question: The principle of conservation of linear momentum states that linear momentum of a system:

options.html: Can be changed only if external forces act
Can be changed if only internal forces act

questions.explanation: The linear momentum of a system remains constant. It may get transferred to the bodies in the system itself but the total momentum remains the same.

questions.question: The action and reaction forces referred in the third law

options.html: Need not be equal in magnitude but must have the same direction.
Must act on the same object.

questions.explanation: Newton's Third Law of Motion states: 'To every action there is an equal and opposite reaction'. It must be remembered that action and reaction always act on different objects. Action of one object generates reaction in other.

questions.question:
Statement A: Gravitational force is the weakest force in nature.
Statement B: Electrostatic force is an example of non - contact forces.
Which of the above two statements is true?

options.html: Neither statement A nor B is true

questions.explanation: Gravitational force is a very weak force as it depends upon the mass of the body and distance between two objects. The electrostatic force is a non-contact force as it acts even if there is no contact between the objects.

questions.question: Ayush hits a cricket ball, it then rolls off a level ground after covering a short distance, the ball comes to rest. The ball slows and then stops because:

options.html: Velocity is proportional to the force exerted on the ball.
There is no unbalanced force on the ball so the ball would want to come to rest.
There is a force on the ball opposing the motion.
Ayush did not hit the ball hard enough.

questions.explanation: The ball moving on the level ground comes to rest after covering a short distance due to frictional force acting on it. So, the ball slows and then stops because there is a force acting on the ball by the floor thus, opposing the motion.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(1) Action and reaction (A) No acceleration
(2) Magnitude of force (B) Newton’s second law
(3) Momentum (C) Act on different objects
(4) Resultant of balanced force (D) Vector quantity
questions.explanation:
• According to Newton's third law in every interaction, there is a pair of forces acting on the two different interacting objects.
  • Newton's second law states that the acceleration of an object as produced by a net force is directly proportional to the magnitude of the net force, in the same direction as the net force, and inversely proportional to the mass of the object.
  • Momentum is a vector quantity. For a particle with mass, the momentum equals mass times velocity, and velocity is a vector quantity while mass is a scalar quantity. A scalar multiplied by a vector is a vector.
  • The resultant of a balanced force has no acceleration.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(1) Cart pulled by a horse (A) Friction
(2) Backward jerk (B) Newton’s III law of motion
(3) Sole of shoe worn out (C) Law of conservation of momentum
(4) Recoiling of gun (D) Law of inertia
questions.explanation:
• Cart is pulled by horse due to Newton's third law of motion.
  • Backward jerk is felt on an account of the law of inertia. 
  • Soles of shoes are worn out due to the force of friction while walking.
  • Recoiling of guns after a fire occurs with respect to the law of conservation of momentum.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(1) Cause of acceleration (A) Mass
(2) Quantitative measure of inertia (B) Increases friction
(3) powder applied on carom board (C) Decreases friction
(4) Tyres of vehicles made Rough (D) Force
questions.explanation:
• Any body is accelerated due to force acting on it respecting the first law of motion.
  • Mass is the quantitative measure of inertia.
  • Powder reduces the friction on the carom board.
  • Tyres of vehicles are made rough to increase the friction between road and vehicle.

questions.question:
When a force is exerted on an object, it can change its:

  1. State
  2. Position
  3. Shape
  4. Direction
questions.explanation: The force causes a change in the state of rest, motion, direction, position, or even the shape of a body.

questions.question: It is advised to tie any luggage kept on the roof of a bus with a rope to avoid the luggage from falling down because of:
questions.explanation: The luggage kept on the roof of a bus is tied with a rope to avoid the luggage from falling down due to the inertia of rest, motion, and direction.

questions.question:
An object moving along a straight path with accelerated motion. The correct statement about the object-

  1. Its velocity keeps changing
  2. Its speed keeps changing
  3. A force is always acting on it
  4. Its mass keeps on changing
questions.explanation: Since the body is accelerating it means that a force acting on the body is changing the velocity of the body. Since there is no change in direction the speed is also changing. And mass always remains constant.

questions.question: The passenger feels a jerk forward when a bus moving at a high speed suddenly stops. This happens due to:

options.html: the gravitational pull of the moon
the gravitational pull of the Earth

questions.explanation: When a moving bus at high speed suddenly stops, the passenger feels jerk in forwarding direction due to inertia of motion, which tends to keep the moving passenger to continue moving.

questions.question: Weight of a body due to the Earth's gravity acts:

options.html: towards the centre of the earth

questions.explanation: The weight of the body is the measure of force by which the object is attracted to the center of the earth. It always acts vertically downwards.

tests.name: Forces and Laws of Motions  Test-02
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Forces and Laws of Motions Test-02 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Some of the leaves may get detached from a tree if we vigorously shake its branch because of:
questions.explanation: Newton’s first law of motion states that a body at rest will remain at rest, and a body in motion will continue in motion in a straight line with a uniform speed unless it is compelled by an external force to change its state of rest or of uniform motion. All objects resist a change in their state of motion. The tendency of undisturbed objects to stay at rest or to keep moving with the same velocity is called inertia. This is why the first law of motion is also known as the law of inertia. Hence, if a branch of a tree is vigorously shaken, some of the leaves may get detached from the tree due to the inertia of rest. However, the leaves tend to remain in rest but branches come in motion.

questions.question: Inertia is the property of a body by virtue of which the body is:

options.html: unable to change by itself the state of rest
unable to change itself the direction of motion
unable to change itself the state of motion

questions.explanation: Inertia is the property of a body by virtue of which the body is unable to change the state of rest or state of motion and direction as inertia is the tendency to oppose the change in state or direction.

questions.question: Which has more inertia?
questions.explanation: The inertia of the body is proportional to the mass of the body. ₹ 5 coin is heavier than ₹ 5 note and ₹ 1 coin. So, the inertia of ₹ 5 coins more than others.

questions.question: A high jumper runs for a while before taking high jump so that the inertia of ________ help him in taking a long jump.
questions.explanation: A high jumper runs for a while before taking high jump so that the inertia of motion helps him in taking a higher jump.

questions.question: If P and Q are two bodies with masses 10 kg and 5 kg respectively, then which body has more inertia?

options.html: both bodies P and Q have no equal inertia
both bodies P and Q have equal inertia

questions.explanation: The inertia of the body is proportional to the mass of the body. So, body P with masses 10 kg will have more inertia in comparison to body Q of mass 5 kg.

questions.question:
In a football and stone of same size, the inertia of

  1. Football is greater
  2. Football is lesser
  3. Stone is greater
  4. Stone is lesser
questions.explanation: The football being hollow has lesser mass when compared to stone and inertia is directly proportional to the mass of the object. Therefore stone has greater inertia.

questions.question:
Dust particles come out of a hanging carpet when we strike it with a stick because-

  1. Carpet starts moving forward and backward due to inertia of motion
  2. Dust particles remain at rest
  3. Dust particles start moving forward due to inertia of motion
  4. Carpet remains at rest
questions.explanation: Dust can be removed from a hanging carpet by shaking it or beating it with a stick. Initially, both the carpet and the dust are at rest. When the carpet is shaken or beaten with a stick, the carpets are set into motion while the dust remains in the state of rest due to the inertia of rest. Thus the dust falls down due to the pull of gravity.

questions.question: A force of 7 kg-wt acts on a body of mass 10 kg. calculate the acceleration produced.
questions.explanation:
Force = 7 kg-wt = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 70 N, mass = 10 kg. therefore, acceleration produced in the body = {tex}F\over a{/tex} = {tex}70\over 10{/tex}
= 7 ms-2.

questions.question: The physical quantity which is equal to the rate of change of momentum is:
questions.explanation:
Rate of change of momentum=(final momentum-initial momentum)/time taken
= {tex}(mv-mu)\over t{/tex}
= {tex}m(v-u)\over t{/tex}
= ma
= F

questions.question: Which of the following physical quantity is associated with Newton’s second law of motion?
questions.explanation: Newton’s second law of motion states that the rate of change in momentum is equal to force applied. Momentum is the product of mass and velocity. So, mass, velocity, and time all are associated with Newton’s second law of motion.

questions.question: State, whether the following statement is true or false “force, can be measured using the first law of motion”
questions.explanation: The force can be measured by newton’s second law of motion. The first law of motion states that object in rest or uniform motion tends to remain in its state.

questions.question: “A body moves with a constant speed of 5m/s. This shows that no unbalanced forces are acting on the body” is a:
questions.explanation: Since the body is moving with constant speed this means that there is no acceleration. Thus the forces acting are balanced. Hence there are no unbalanced forces acting on the body.

questions.question: Same amount of force is acting on a car and bus. If the ratio of the mass of the bus and the car is 50:1, calculate the ratio of the acceleration produced in the car to the acceleration produced in the bus.
questions.explanation: Let mass of bus and car are 50x and 1x respectively. Force acting on both objects is same. So, ratio of acceleration produced = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{F}}{1 x}: \frac{F}{50 x}{/tex} = 1 : 50

questions.question: A force of 100N applied on an object accelerates it with an acceleration of 2ms-2. How much force is required to produce the same acceleration when an identical object of same mass is tied with the first object?
questions.explanation:
Force = 100N, acceleration = 2ms-2, Mass = {tex}force \over acceleration{/tex} = {tex}100\over 2{/tex} = 50 kg. when mass is doubled, mass = 100 kg, required acceleration = 2ms-2, than force required = m {tex} \times {/tex} a
= 100 {tex}\times {/tex} 2
= 200N

questions.question: Which would require a greater force - accelerating a body A of mass 2kg at 5ms-2 or a body B of mass 5kg at 2ms-2?
questions.explanation:
Mass of body A = 2Kg, acceleration = 5 m/s2, than force
= m {tex} \times {/tex} a
= 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 5 = 10N.
Mass of body B = 5kg, acceleration= 2 m/s2. Force
= {tex}5 \times 2{/tex}
= 10 N.
so, both require equal force.

tests.name: Forces and Laws of Motions  Test-03
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Forces and Laws of Motions Test-03 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: The essential characteristic of equilibrium is:
questions.explanation: At equilibrium, the acceleration is equal to zero. So, there is no net change in the acceleration of the moving body.

questions.question: To every action there is an:
questions.explanation: To every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. This is the newton’s third law of motion.

questions.question: “Forces always occur in pairs”. From which law can we conclude the above statement?
questions.explanation: The force always occurs in pairs. According to the third law of motion, there is an equal and opposite reaction. So two forces, action and reaction act in pairs on two different bodies always.

questions.question: Frictional force:

options.html: always in the opposite direction of motion
always opposes relative motion between the surfaces of contact
always in the direction of motion
always opposes the motion of a body

questions.explanation: Friction is a contact force always acting in the direction opposite to that of motion.

questions.question: ‘Any two forces which are equal and opposite form an action and reaction pair’ The above statement is:
questions.explanation: The two forces acting on the same body, which are in equal and opposite direction form action and reaction pair. This is newton’s thirds law of motion.

questions.question: “Action and reaction are equal and opposite but even then they do not cancel each other” the above statement is
questions.explanation: Action and reaction are equal and opposite but even they do not cancel each other because they act in the opposite direction and on two different bodies. Hence the statement is correct.

questions.question: Find the incorrect statement about Newton’s law of action and reaction. Action and reaction:
questions.explanation: Newton’s third law of motion explains action and reaction. Action and reaction act simultaneously, are equal in magnitude. They act in different directions and acts on different bodies.

questions.question:
Which is incorrect statement about action and reaction referred to Newton’s third law of motion?

  1. They are not equal.
  2. They are opposite.
  3. They act on same object.
  4. They act simultaneously.
questions.explanation: Action and reaction are equal and opposite and act on two different bodies simultaneously.

questions.question: Which of the following is not used to reduce friction

options.html: making scratches on the contact surfaces

questions.explanation: To reduce the friction, boiling between contact surfaces can be done, besides we can use ball bearing or grease between contact surfaces. Making scratches between contact surfaces will make the surface rough and so, will increase the friction.

questions.question: What is the momentum of an object of mass {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}m, moving with a velocity 2v?
questions.explanation: The momentum of a body is equal to product of mass and velocity. Momentum = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}m {tex}\times{/tex} 2v = mv.

questions.question: Internal forces are forces which bodies exert on each other when the bodies are part of the system is
questions.explanation: Internal forces are those forces which are exerted on each other when the bodies are part of the system. The forces acting between the atoms of molecules that keep them together are an example of internal force.

questions.question: According to the law of conservation of momentum
questions.explanation:
m1u1 + m2u2 = m1v1 + m2v2
When two object colloid. The Law of conservation of momentum states that total momentum before the collision is equal to the total momentum after a collision if no external force acts on it.

questions.question: A bullet of mass 10 g is fired from a rifle. The bullet tales 0.003 second to move through its barrel and leaves it with a velocity of 300 m/s. What is the force exerted on the bullet by the rifle?
questions.explanation:
Mass of bullet = 10 g = {tex}\frac{10}{1000}{/tex} kg.
initial velocity (u) = 0 and final velocity (v) = 300 m/s.
time (t) = 0.003 s
F= {tex}\frac{(mv - mu)}{t}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(0.01 \times 300 - 0.01 \times 0) }{0.003}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(3- 0)}{0.003}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{0.003}{/tex} 
= 1000 N

questions.question: A force of 5 N gives a mass m1 an acceleration of 10ms-2 and mass m2 an acceleration of 20 ms-2. What acceleration would if give it both the masses were tied together?
questions.explanation:
m1 = {tex}\frac{5N}{10 ms^{-2}}{/tex} = 0.5 kg, m2 = {tex}\frac{5N}{20 ms^{-2}}{/tex} = 0.25 kg 
Total mass = 0.5 + 0.25 = 0.75 kg.
Acceleration produced when both masses are tied together = {tex}\frac{5 N}{0.75 kg}{/tex} kg
= 6.67 ms-2.

questions.question:
Statement A: Rocket contains fuel as well as oxygen to burn its fuel in its body.
Statement B: A jet plane takes oxygen from the atmosphere to burn its fuel.
Which statement is true?

options.html: Neither statement A nor statement B is true

questions.explanation: The rocket contains fuel as well as oxygen to burn its fuel as combustion takes place only in the presence of oxygen. A jet plane is filled with hydrogen only and it takes oxygen from the atmosphere to burn its fuel. So, both statements are true.

tests.name: Forces and Laws of Motions  Test-04
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Forces and Laws of Motions Test-04 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question:
Inflated balloon lying on the surface of a floor moves forward when pierced with a pin.
The above-mentioned phenomena is due to
questions.explanation: Inflated balloon lying on the surface of a floor moves forward when pierced with a pin due to Newton’s third law of motion. The air coming out of the balloon forces the balloon in the opposite direction.

questions.question: The change in the momentum of a body in 0.01 seconds is 10 kg ms-1. The force acting on this body is
questions.explanation: The force acting on a body is equal to rate of change of momentum. Change of momentum = 10 Kg/s, time taken = 0.01 second. Force = {tex}\frac{10}{0.01}{/tex} = 1000 N.

questions.question:
Statement A: Rocket can propel its self in a vacuum.
Statement B: Newton’s laws are universal.
Which of the two statements is true?
questions.explanation: The rocket moves forward according to newton’s third law of motion. The gas and other substance coming out from the tail push the rocket in the forward direction. Newton’s laws are universal.

questions.question: A ball is dropped from a height of 10m. The ball is embedded in sand of 1m and stops.

options.html: Both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.
Neither K.E nor momentum is conserved.

questions.explanation: When the body is dropped from a height, the potential energy decreases, and kinetic energy increases. After being fallen on the sand potential energy becomes zero and kinetic energy becomes maximum. Thus, a ball dropped from a height will conserve only kinetic energy.

questions.question: Two bodies of masses 1kg and 5kg are dropped gently from the top of a tower. At a point, 50cm from the ground both the bodies will have the same:
questions.explanation: The velocity of the body falling towards depends upon gravitational force acting on it. So, both the body of masses 1 kg and 5 kg will have the same velocity at a particular height and fall at the same time at the ground.

questions.question:
Statement A: Calculus, a new branch of mathematics was stated by Newton.
Statement B: If mass remains the same, doubling the force will reduce the acceleration to half.
Which of the above two statements is true?
questions.explanation: The branch of mathematics, called Calculus was stated by Newton. Force is the product of mass and acceleration. So, acceleration = {tex}force \over mass{/tex}. Doubling of force will double the acceleration if the mass is kept the same.

questions.question: If an object experiences a net zero unbalanced force then the body:
questions.explanation: If an object experiences a net-zero unbalanced force, then the body moves with constant velocity. Zero unbalanced forces produce no acceleration in the body and the body continues to move with the same velocity.

questions.question:
Statement A: A passenger falls forward when a bus suddenly starts moving in the forward direction.
Statement B: A gun recoils backward with a small speed than the bullet moving forward.
Which of the following statements is/are true?

options.html: both statements A and B are true
neither statement A nor Statement B is true

questions.explanation: A passenger falls backward when a bus suddenly starts moving in the forward direction due to the inertia of rest. A gun recoils backward with a small speed than the bullet moving forward due to the law of conservation of momentum.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(1) Inertia (A) Product of mass and velocity
(2) Friction (B) Mass of the object
(3) Momentum (C) Rate of change of momentum
(4) Force (D) Necessary evil
questions.explanation:
• Inertia depends on the mass of object.
  • Friction is a necessary evil because neither movement of bodies not holding anybody would have been possible without friction.
  • Momentum can be given as the product of mass and velocity.
  • Force can be defined as the rate of change of momentum.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(1) Newton’s first law of motion (A) The acceleration produced is directly proportional to the product of mass & force applied
(2) Newton’s second law of motion (B) When no force is exerted on an object it stays at rest or it moves in a straight light with constant speed
(3) Newton’s third law of motion (C) To every action, there is an equal & opposite reaction
(4) Galileo’s law of inertia (D) An object at rest or in uniform motion will remain at rest or uniform motion unless an unbalanced force is applied on it
questions.explanation:
• Newton's First Law of Motion: Any object remains in the state of rest or in uniform motion along a straight line until it is compelled to change the state by applying the external force.
  • Newton's Second Law of Motion: The rate of change of momentum is directly proportional to the force applied in the direction of the force.
  • Newton's Third Law of Motion: There is an equal and opposite reaction for every action.
  • Galileo Galilei: Galileo first of all said that objects move with a constant speed when no forces act on them. This means if an object is moving on a frictionless path and no other force is acting upon it, the object would be moving forever. That is there is no unbalanced force working on the object.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(1) Force (A) Newton
(2) Momentum (B) Acceleration
(3) Unbalanced force (C) Kg m/s
(4) Principle on rocket works (D) Newton’s third law of motion
questions.explanation:
Units of-

  • force- Newton
  • momentum- kg m/s
  • acceleration is caused due to unbalanced force acting on the body.
  • rocket works on the principle of Newton's third law of motion

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(1) Friction (A) when one object rolls over another
(2) Limiting friction (B) Force just sufficient to move the object
(3) Sliding friction (iii)(C) Force which opposes motion
(4) Rolling friction (D) Force sufficient to slide one object over another
questions.explanation:
• Frictional force is a contact force that opposes the motion of a body.
  • The force which is just enough to bring about change in state and tend a body to motion is called limiting force of friction.
  • The force of friction which is just sufficient to make a body slide over any surface is called sliding friction.
  • rolling friction acts upon when a body rolls over any surface.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(1) Unbalanced force (A) Applying grease
(2) Unchanging tendency (B) Change in state
(3) Method of reducing friction (C) Force
(4) Rate of change of momentum (D) Inertia
questions.explanation:
• An unbalanced force cause change in the state of motion i.e. acceleration in a body.
  • Inertia has an approach to remain conserved.
  • Grease reduces friction which may lead to wear and tear of machine parts.
  • Force can be defined as the rate of change of momentum.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.
 

Column I Column II
(i) F (a) {tex}m \times v{/tex}
(ii) P (b) Momentum after the collision are equal
(iii) {tex}\Delta P{/tex} (c) {tex}\frac{{{P_2} - {P_1}}}{t}{/tex}
(iv) Momentum before the collision (d) {tex}m \times a{/tex}
questions.explanation:
1. Force can be defined as the product of mass and acceleration i.e. F = m {tex}\times{/tex} a.
  2. Momentum(p) defined as the product of mass and velocity i.e. p = m {tex}\times{/tex} v.
  3. Change in momentum can be given as- {tex}\Delta{/tex}p = {tex}\frac {p_2 - p_1}{t}{/tex}​​​​​
  4. The law of conservation of momentum,  momentum before the collision is equal to momentum after the collision.

questions.question:
Which is correct about frictional force & gravitational force?

  1. Frictional force always produces retardation while gravitational force produces both retardation and acceleration
  2. Both produce retardation
  3. The frictional force acts when two surfaces are in contact
  4. Gravitational force acts when the object is at some height
questions.explanation:
Statement (B) is wrong as when you drop a ball from a height gravity provides acceleration.
Also, the frictional force is a retarding force while gravitational force may be retarding or accelerating. The frictional force is a contact force whereas gravitational force acts from distance like from height.

questions.question: The S. I unit of G is ________.
questions.explanation: Universal gravitational constant is represented by ‘G’. S.I. unit of universal gravitational constant is Nm2/kg2. As G = {tex}Fx{r^2}\over Mm{/tex}.

questions.question: Inverse-square law means F {tex}\propto{/tex} ________.
questions.explanation: The force acting on two bodies is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. Therefore, F {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}1\over {r^2}{/tex}.

questions.question: What is ratio of the force of gravitation between two mases m1 and m2 kept at a distance R on the Earth and on the moon?
questions.explanation: Force of gravitation is equal to the product of masses divided by the square of the distance between them. So, the force of gravitation between two bodies will be the same irrespective of their place. The ratio will be 1:1.

questions.question: The value of G depends on:
questions.explanation: The value of universal gravitational constant (G) is 6.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11Nm2/Kg2 which is constant and independent of mass, the distance between the bodies, and other factors.

questions.question: Two masses m and M are kept at a distance r. The ratio of the force exerted on m due to M and that of M due to m is equal to
questions.explanation: Gravitational force between two bodies F ={tex}G{\text{ }}Mm/{r^2}{/tex}. The value of force exerted on m due to M will be equal to that of M due to m. so, the ratio will be 1;1.

questions.question:
Which of the two statements is true?
Statement A: The force of gravitation may be attractive or repulsive.
Statement B: Inverse-square law means {tex}F\alpha \frac{1}{r^{2}}{/tex}.
questions.explanation: The force of attraction is always attractive. The law of the inverse square means that the force by which two objects attract each other is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

questions.question:
According universal law of Gravitation

  1. Force is directly proportional to the product of masses of two bodies.
  2. Force is inversely proportional to the square of distance between two bodies.
  3. Force is directly proportional to the square of distance between two bodies.
  4. Force is inversely proportional to the product of masses of two bodies.
questions.explanation: According to the universal law of gravitation the force is directly proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

questions.question: The value of G one-sixth on moon as compared to that on the earth. The above statement is
questions.explanation: The value of G, a universal gravitational constant remains the same everywhere. So, the value of G is equal at both earth and moon. Hence, the above statement is false.

questions.question: What is the force of gravitation between two point masses of 1kg and 2kg kept 1 m apart?
questions.explanation:
Gravitational force = {tex}G\frac{m_1 \times m_2}{d^2}{/tex}
= 6.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{2}{1^2}{/tex}
= 13.34 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-11 N

questions.question: Newton’s law of gravitation is valid in
questions.explanation: Newton’s law of gravitation or universal law of gravitation is valid everywhere, in the laboratory, on the earth's surface, or on other solar system.

questions.question: How does the force of gravitation between two objects change when the distance between them is reduced to half?
questions.explanation: Gravitational force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. Hence when the distance is reduced to half the gravitational force will increase four times.

questions.question:
Which of the following statement are incorrect-

  1. The value of acceleration due to gravity is the same at all places.
  2. Mass is constant at all places.
  3. Weight is constant at all places.
  4. The weight of an object can be zero.
questions.explanation: Acceleration due to gravity does not remain the same everywhere and so the weight also because 'g' is variable with the radius and mass of the body.

questions.question: What happens to the force between two objects if the masses of both objects are doubled?
questions.explanation: As the gravitational force between two bodies is directly proportional to the product of their masses. If the mass of both objects is doubled. The value of force becomes four times.

questions.question: The distance between two particles is decreased. The gravitational force between them
questions.explanation: The gravitational force between two bodies is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. So, when the distance between the two bodies is decreased the gravitational force between them will increase.

questions.question: If Ge is the value of universal gravitational constant at the Earth and Gm is the value of universal gravitational constant on the moon then
questions.explanation: As Universal gravitational constant remain same throughout the universe. The value of Gm and Ge will be same. So, Ge = Gm.

tests.name: Gravitation  Test-02
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Gravitation Test-02 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Which of the following phenomena is due to gravitational force?
questions.explanation: Gravitational force acts between two bodies to attract the bodies towards each other. Tides in the ocean, movement of satellite and rainfall all are due to gravitational force.

questions.question:
Statement A: Polar radius is 6357km and the equatorial radius is 6378km.
Statement B: At a given place the weight of an object is equal to the mass of the object.
Which of the two statements is true?
questions.explanation:
The radius of the earth at the poles is 6357 km, the radius at the equator is 6378 km.
The force by which earth attracts an object towards it is called the weight of the object, which is the product of mass (m) of the object and acceleration due to gravity (g). Mass is the measurement of inertia. So, at a given place the weight of an object is not equal to the mass of the object.
So, Statement A is true and B is false.

questions.question: Gravitation force is a
questions.explanation: Gravitational force is a weak force. This force exists between each and every object but cannot be felt until the mass of the bodies is not extremely high like planets.

questions.question:
Statement A: The Earth attracts the moon with a force greater than the force with which the moon attracts the Earth.
Statement B: The mass and weight of a body become 0 at the center of the Earth.
Which of the two statements is true?

options.html: both the statements A and B

questions.explanation: Earth and moon attract each other with equal gravitational force. The mass of a body never becomes 0. Hence both statements are incorrect.

questions.question:
Force of gravitation

  1. Directly proportional to the product of masses of two objects.
  2. Inversely proportional to the product of masses of two objects.
  3. Directly proportional to the square of distance between two objects.
  4. Inversely proportional to the square of distance between two objects.
questions.explanation:
F = {tex}G\frac{Mm}{r^2}{/tex} 
Here, m and M are masses of any two bodies respectively and r is the distance between them.
Law of Gravitation states that force of attraction by which an object attracts other object is directly proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of distance between them.

questions.question:
Which of the following is an application of Earth’s gravitation

  1. It holds atmosphere around our globe.
  2. It holds us firmly on the surface of the Earth.
  3. It is responsible for motion of moon.
  4. It is responsible for sea tides due to the moon.
questions.explanation: Gravitational force although invisible or rarely felt but it holds all the bodies and earth atmosphere firmly. It is also responsible for the revolution of the moon around the earth. Gravity is also the cause of tides.

questions.question: Suppose a planet starts shrinking, the value of g starts
questions.explanation: Value of acceleration due to gravity g = {tex}\frac{GM}{r^2}{/tex}. Suppose a planet starts shrinking, the value of ‘g’ will increase accordingly. As the radius is decreasing then g will increase proportionally.

questions.question: Two bodies of mass M and m are kept on the surface of the Earth. What is the ratio of acceleration due to gravity on the two bodies?
questions.explanation: Acceleration due to gravity is independent of the mass of the object. Two bodies having different mass kept on earth will have equal acceleration due to gravity as g = {tex}\frac{GM}{r^2}{/tex}.

questions.question: The value of g at the mount everest is
questions.explanation: The value of 'g' at the mount everest is < g (acceleration due to gravity on earth surface) because with increase in height, gravitational force of earth decreases.

questions.question: A ball thrown up vertically returns to the ground after 10 second. Find the velocity with which it was thrown up? (g = 10 m/s2)
questions.explanation:
Time taken to reach at highest point = {tex}\frac{10}2{/tex} = 5s. Value of g = 10 m/s2. Final velocity (v) = 0 at heigth point.
V= u + gt
0 = u + (-10)5
0 = u - 50
u = 50 m/s

questions.question: As we move above the surface of the earth, the value of g
questions.explanation: As we move above the surface of the earth, the value of g, acceleration due to gravity decreases as g = {tex}\frac{GM}{r^2}{/tex}. With increase in height, r also increases.

questions.question: Drop an iron nail and a sheet of paper from the roof of your house. Which of the two will reach the ground first?

options.html: neither sheet of paper nor iron nail

questions.explanation: Although the force by which iron nail and a sheet of paper are attracted towards the earth is equal. The more air friction is faced by a sheet of paper due to the larger size. So, an iron nail will reach the ground first.

questions.question:
Find the incorrect statement?

  1. The value of g increases with depth.
  2. The value of g is more at poles and less at the equator.
  3. The value of g at the surface of the Earth is 9.8 m/s2.
  4. The value of g decreases with height.
questions.explanation: The value of g depends upon the mass of earth and the distance between the centre of the object and earth. As depth increases, the value of acceleration due to gravity falls.

questions.question:
Which of the following statement is incorrect?

  1. When a body is thrown vertically upward the value of acceleration due to gravity is negative.
  2. When a body is thrown vertically upward its final velocity becomes zero.
  3. ​​​​​​​When a body is dropped freely from a height its initial velocity is zero.
  4. ​​​​​​​None of these
questions.explanation: When a body is dropped freely from a height its initial velocity is zero and thrown vertically upward its final velocity becomes zero and acceleration due to gravity is negative in case of the body thrown vertically upward.

questions.question: “When an apple is thrown up earth attracts the apple but the apple does not attract the earth” The above-written statement is:
questions.explanation: The gravitational force between earth and apple is equal but attraction takes place towards the body of heavier mass. So, apples are attracted by earth but the earth is not attracted by apple.

tests.name: Gravitation  Test-03
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Gravitation Test-03 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.

  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: What is the direction of ‘g’ when the body is falling down?
questions.explanation: When a body is thrown upwards, the direction of ‘g ‘ is negative that slows down the speed of the body. When the body is falling downward, the direction of ‘g’ is the downward direction and is positive.

questions.question: A man throws a ball with a velocity of 20m/s. After what time will the ball come back to his hands? (Take g = 10m/s2)
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity ,u= 20 m/s. final velocity= 0 , g = 10m/s2 .
V = u + gt
0= 20 + (-10)t 
-10t = 20
t = {tex}\frac{20}{10}{/tex} = 2s. time taken to fall in downward direction = 2s.
Hence total time taken = 2s + 2s = 4s.

questions.question: The equation of motion for free fall can be used in case of any free-fall is:
questions.explanation: The equation of motion for free fall can be used in case of falling of body only due to gravitation of earth without any other force. So, this statement is not true.

questions.question: What is the weight of the body whose mass is 100 kg on the surface of the earth:
questions.explanation:
Mass of the body (m) = 100 kg, acceleration due to gravity (g) = 9.8 m/s2.
Weight (w) = m {tex}\times{/tex} g
= 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8
= 980 N

questions.question: The weight of an object is least at some region of the earth. What will be the colour of bears found in that region?
questions.explanation: The weight of the body is the measure of force by which the earth attracts the body. Acceleration due to gravity is less in case of poles and colour of bears found in these regions is white, called a polar bear.

questions.question: An object weighs 10 N when measured on the surface of the Earth. What would be its weight when measured on the surface of the moon?
questions.explanation: Acceleration due to gravity (g) on moon is {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex}th of the acceleration of gravity (g) on earth. So, weight of body of 10N on the surface of moon will be {tex}\frac{1}{6}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 10 N = 1.67 N.

questions.question: The weight of a body can be expressed in
questions.explanation: Weight of the body is equal to product of mass and acceleration due to gravity. W = kg {tex}\times{/tex} m/s2 = N (Newton).

questions.question: 1 kg wt = ?
questions.explanation:
One kilogram weight is that gravitational force which acts on a body of mass 1 kg.
g = 9.8 m/s2
1 kg wt =  9.8 N
This means that the weight of 1 kg mass is 9.8 N.

questions.question:
Statement A: When an object is thrown up then its speed at any position while going up is the same as the speed while coming down.
Statement B: A can’t have zero weight.
Which of the two statements is true?
questions.explanation: When an object is thrown up its speed decreases with height as gravitational force acting on it is negative. After attaining maximum height objects start falling downward with increasing speed as the gravitational force acting is positive.

questions.question: Calculate the mass of the earth given radius of the Earth is 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m.
questions.explanation:
g = {tex}\frac{G M}{r^{2}}{/tex} 
M = {tex}\frac{g r^{2}}{G}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{9.8 \times\left(6.4 \times 10^{6}\right)^{2}}{6.67 \times 10^{-11}}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1027 m = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 Km

questions.question: If the weight of Harsh is 150N. What is his mass? (g = 10 m/s2)
questions.explanation:
Weight of a body (W) = Mass {tex}\times{/tex} acceleration due to gravity.
Here, W = 150 N and g = 10 m/s2.
So, Mass = {tex}\frac wg=\;\frac{150N}{10\;m/s^2}{/tex} = 15
Mass = 15 Kg.

questions.question:
Statement A: A person will weight more in Ooty than in Delhi.
Statement B: Mass is measured by pan balance and weight is measured by spring balance.
Which of the two statements is true?
questions.explanation: The value of g decreases as we move at a higher altitude, so ‘g’ is less at Ooty than Delhi. Weight will be less in Ooty than Delhi. Mass is measured by pan of beam balance and weight is measured by spring balance.

questions.question:
Statement A: Mass of a body is a fundamental property.
Statement B: Weight is a fundamental property.
Which of the two statements is true?
questions.explanation: Mass of a body always remains the same. Mass is independent of other factors. Hence mass is a fundamental property. On the other hand weight of the body is the force by which it is attracted by the earth that depends upon the value of ‘g’. So, weight is not a fundamental factor.

questions.question: A body has a weight W on the surface of Earth. What is its weight on a planet which has mass 10 times that of the Earth and a radius of 4 times that of the earth?
questions.explanation:
Weight of earth = W, mass on earth = {tex}\frac{W}{g}{/tex}.
Mass of planet = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} m,
Gravity of planet = {tex}\frac{10m}{4^2}{/tex}.
Weight on the planet ={tex}16 \times \frac W{10}{/tex}= {tex}\frac 85{/tex}W.

questions.question: If a block of mass weighed 1 N at equator, What would be its weight at poles?
questions.explanation: The value of ‘g’ is slightly less at poles in comparison to that of equator due to closeness to centre of the earth. The weight of the body is equal to product of mass and acceleration due to gravity. Hence, the weight will be more on poles than the equator.

tests.name: Gravitation  Test-04
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Gravitation Test-04 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question:
Two objects of different masses are dropped freely from a height near the surface of the Earth. They have:-

  1. Different velocities at any instant
  2. Same velocities at any instant
  3. Different accelerations
  4. Same acceleration
questions.explanation: Acceleration and velocity remain the same when a body falls freely towards the earth irrespective of the distance traveled or yet to travel.

questions.question:
A stone falling towards earth

  1. Is attracted by earth.
  2. Attracts the earth.
  3. Both stone and earth attract each other.
  4. No one attract each other.
questions.explanation: Everybody is attracted by earth and attracts the earth towards it. But the movement occurs where the force of attraction is higher. Body with higher mass will have more force of attraction i.e. the earth.

questions.question: What will be the pressure when a force of 200 N is exerted on area of 5 m2
questions.explanation:
Here, Force = 200N.
Area = 5m2.
Pressure = {tex}\frac{force}{area}=\;\frac{200N}{15\;m^2}{/tex} = 40 Pa

questions.question:
In which of the following pressure is increased

  1. The strap of bag is made wider
  2. Knife with a sharp edge
  3. Shoes with flat sole
  4. Nail with a finely pointed tip
questions.explanation:
The pressure is inversely proportional to the area of contact.
Smaller the area of an object in contact with a body, the higher will be the pressure exerted by it.

questions.question: What is the buoyant force?

options.html: An upward force on an object unimmersed in a liquid
A downward force on an object immersed in a liquid

questions.explanation: The upward force experienced by the body when partially or completely immersed in the fluid.

questions.question:
Find the incorrect statement.

  1. Mass is a vector quantity.
  2. That planet will have large value of g which has large value of mass and small value of radius.
  3. Air resistance may make the acceleration different.
  4. When a body is thrown up, the acceleration due to gravity is directed upwards.
questions.explanation: The value of g depends upon the mass of the planet and radius, acceleration due to gravity does not change due to air friction. The body moving in an upward direction will have gravitational force in a downward direction.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(a) F (i) {tex}\frac{{GM}}{{{R^2}}}{/tex}
(b) g (ii) {tex}\frac{{{\text{Density of substance}}}}{{{\text{Density of water}}}}{/tex}
(c) Relative density (iii) {tex}\frac{{{\text{Force}}}}{{{\text{Area}}}}{/tex}
(d) Pressure (iv) {tex}\frac{{G.M.m}}{{{r^2}}}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
The correct order of the given match is (a) - (iv), (b) - (i), (c) - (iii), (d) - (ii).

  1. F = {tex}\frac{{G.M.m}}{{{r^2}}}{/tex}
  2. g = {tex}\frac{{GM}}{{{R^2}}}{/tex}
  3. Relative density = {tex}\frac{{{\text{Density of substance}}}}{{{\text{Density of water}}}}{/tex}
  4. Pressure = {tex}\frac{{{\text{Force}}}}{{{\text{Area}}}}{/tex}

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(a) Fluid (i) Gravitation force, earth attracts an object
(b) Mass (ii) Gases
(c) Weight (iii) Inversely proportional to square of distance
(d) Newton’s law of gravitation (iv) Quantity of matter
questions.explanation:
1. Fluid: Gasses and liquids are all fluids because the molecules that make them up are in constant motion.
  2. Mass: The mass of a body is the quantity of matter it contains.
  3. Weight: The weight of an object is the force with which it is attracted towards the earth.
  4. Newton’s law of gravitation: Law of Gravitation states that force of attraction by which an object attracts other objects is directly proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(a) Acceleration due to gravity depends (i) 9.8N
(b) Gravitational constant (ii) {tex}6.6734 \times {10^{ - 11}}N{m^2}/K{g^2}{/tex}
(c) Free fall of the object (iii) Latitude of the place
(d) One kg wt. (iv) Influence of earth’s gravitational pull
questions.explanation:
1. Acceleration due to gravity depends on the latitude of the place.
  2. The accepted value of G is 6.673 {tex}\times{/tex} 10−11 Nm2 kg−2
  3. When an object falls from any height under the influence of gravitational force only, it is known as free fall.
  4. One kg wt = 9.8 N

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(a) Archimedes principle (i) One-sixth of weight of an object on earth
(b) Weight of object on moon (ii) Upward force
(c) Strap of school bag made wider (iii) To Reduce pressure on the shoulder
(d) Buoyant force (iv) Used in designing ships & submarines
questions.explanation:
1. Archimede's principle is also used in designing ships and submarines. The floating of a big ship is based on the Archimedes' principle.
  2. The weight of the object on the moon is one-sixth of weight of an object on earth.
  3. The strap of the school bag made wider to Reduce pressure on the shoulder.
  4. Buoyancy is the upward force exerted by fluids over the surface area of contact of an object which is immersed in fluids.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(a) Universal law of gravitation (i) Motion of moon around the earth
(b) Gravitation force between the earth and an object (ii) Pressure
(c) Thrust per unit area (iii) Weight
(d) Mass per unit volume (iv) density
questions.explanation:
1. Universal law of gravitation applied for the motion of moon around the earth.
  2. The gravitational force between a mass and the Earth is the object's weight.
  3. The force acting (thrust) per unit area is called pressure.
  4. The mass per unit volume of a substance is called density.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(a) Value of G (i) Newton
(b) Object floats over water (ii) Density is less than water
(c) S.I unit of pressure (iii) Always constant
(d) S.I unit of weight (iv) Pascal
questions.explanation:
1. Value of G is always constant.
  2. An object floats over water if its density is less than water.
  3. S.I unit of pressure is Pascal.
  4. S.I unit of weight is Newton.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(a) Thrust (i) 1.63 m/s2 
(b) Ge (ii) Force perpendicular to the surface
(c) gm (iii) Gravitational attraction of sun
(d) Motion of planets in fixed orbit (iv) 9.8 m/s2
questions.explanation:
1. The force applied on a surface in a direction perpendicular or normal to the surface is called thrust.
  2. g = 9.8 m /s2
  3. gm = 1.63m/s2
  4. The Sun's gravity pulls on the planets, just as Earth's gravity pulls down anything that is not held up by some other force and keeps you and me on the ground.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(a) Density (i) No unit
(b) Relative density (ii) Nm2/Kg2 
(c) Acceleration due to gravity (iii) m/s2 
(d) Gravitational contrast (iv) Kg/m3
questions.explanation:
1. The SI unit of Density is kilogram per cubic metre (kg/m3) and the cgs unit of gram per cubic centimetre (g/cm3)
  2. Relative density of a substance = Density of the substance/Density of water at 4°C As relative density is the ratio of densities, it has no units.
  3. The acceleration which is gained by an object because of the gravitational force is called its acceleration due to gravity. Its SI unit is m/s2.
  4. The SI unit of G is Nm2kg−2.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(a) Mass is measured by (i) Beam balance
(b) Weight is measured by (ii) Lactometer
(c) Purity of milk (iii) Spring balance
(d) Density of liquid (iv) Hydrometer
questions.explanation:
1. Mass is measured by a beam balance.
  2. Weight is measured by a spring balance.
  3. A lactometer is an instrument that is used to check for the purity of milk by measuring its density.
  4. A hydrometer or areometer is an instrument that measures the specific gravity (relative density) of liquids.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Work done by centripetal force is
questions.explanation:
When a body moves on a circular path. The force that acts on the revolving body towards the center is called the centripetal force. As the force acts as the right angle to the direction of motion, so work done is 0.
W = FScos{tex}\theta{/tex}
Here, force and displacement are perpendicular to each other, so {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90° So, cos90° = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} W = 0 J.

questions.question: For what value of {tex}\theta{/tex} is work done maximum?
questions.explanation:
Work done when a body moves at an angle to the direction of force is W = F {tex}\times{/tex} S cos {tex}\theta{/tex}.
W = F {tex}\times{/tex} S cos {tex}\theta{/tex} ( cos 0o = 1); So, W = F {tex}\times{/tex} S means maximum work is done.

questions.question: 1 kwh =?
questions.explanation:
1 kWh = 1 kW {tex}\times{/tex} 1 h
Or, 1 kWh = 1000 joule {tex}\times{/tex} 3600 s
Or, 1 kWh = 3600000 joule
Or, 1 kWh = 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 joule

questions.question: 1 W. S = ________?
questions.explanation: 1 Watt = 1 Joule per second and a 1 Joule = 1 Watt times one second.

questions.question: A certain household has consumed 250 units of energy during a month. How much energy is this in joules?
questions.explanation:
1 unit (Kw-H) = 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 joule 
Therefore, 250 units = 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 joule    
 = 900 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 Joule.
= 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 Joule.

questions.question: 1 meV (mega electron volt) is equal to
questions.explanation:
An electron volt is a unit of energy. One electron volt is equal to 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 joule.
One mega electron = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 Joule {tex}\times{/tex} 1000000 = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-13 J

questions.question: 9800 joule of energy was spent to raise a mass of 50 kg, the mass was raised to a height of (take g = 9.8 m/s2)
questions.explanation:
P.E = 9800 j, mass = 50 kg, g = 9.8 m/s2 
Potential energy = mgh,
9800= 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 9.8 {tex}\times{/tex} h
9800 = 490 {tex}\times{/tex} h
h = {tex}\frac{9800}{490}{/tex} = 20 m

questions.question: A machine does 2000 joule of work in 400 seconds. What is the power of machine?
questions.explanation:
Power ={tex}\frac{\;work\;done\;}{\;time}{/tex}
work done = 2000 joule, time = 400 s.
power = {tex}\frac{2000}{400}{/tex} = 5 j/s or 5w.

questions.question: Calculate the time taken by a 150 w bulb of consuming 3000 joule of energy.
questions.explanation:
Power of bulb = 150 w, work done = 3000 joule,
Time taken= {tex}\frac{work\;done}{power}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3000}{150}{/tex} = 20 s.

questions.question: What is the work done to be done to increase the velocity of a car from 30 km h-1 to 60 km h-1 if the mass of the car is 1500kg?
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity (u) = 30 km/h = {tex}30\;\times\frac5{18}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{25}3{/tex} m/s.
final velocity (v) = 60 = {tex}\frac{50}3{/tex} m/s.
Mass = 1500 kg.
Work done = change in kinetic energy = {tex}\frac12{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex} m(v2  – u2) = {tex}\frac12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1500 [(16.67)2 - (8.37)2] = 750 {tex}\times{/tex} (277.8889 - 70.0569) =  155874 j.

questions.question: What is the force required to lift a block of mass 2m?
questions.explanation:
Force is the product of mass and acceleration due to gravity.
Mass = 2m and acceleration due to gravity = g m/s2. 
Force = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} m {tex}\times{/tex} g = 2 mg.

questions.question: A body of mass 20 kg climbs up 20 steps of the stairs in 20 seconds. If each step is 20 cm high. What is the power used in climbing the stairs (g = 10 m/s2)
questions.explanation:
Mass = 20 kg, height = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{20}{100}{/tex} m = 4m, time taken = 20 s.
Power = {tex}\frac{work\;done}{\;time}{/tex}
Power = {tex}\frac{mgh}t=\;\frac{20\;\times10\;\times4}{20}{/tex} watt = 40 w.

questions.question: The angle between force and displacement is 2 Q. for what value of Q is work done zero?
questions.explanation:
Work done = W = F {tex}\times{/tex} S cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
(cos 900 = 0) 
So, W = 0
So, Q = 45o.

questions.question: Two persons P and Q do same amount of work. The person Q does the work in tp seconds and person Q in tq seconds. Find the ratio of power delivered by them.
questions.explanation:
Power = {tex}\frac{Work\;done\;}{\;time\;taken}{/tex} 
Pp = {tex}\frac W{t_q}{/tex} 
PQ = {tex}\frac W{t_p}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{P_p}{P_q}\;=\;\frac{\displaystyle\frac W{t_q}}{\displaystyle\frac w{t_p}}=\;\frac{t_p}{t_q}{/tex}

questions.question:
Statement A: Work is said to be done when a camel is carrying a load on its back.
Statement B: Work is said to be done when a sailboat is moving due to wind energy.
Which of the above statement is true?
questions.explanation: Work is said to be done when there is a displacement of the body with respect to moving body. No work is done when a camel is carrying a load on its back. But work is done when a sailboat is moving due to wind energy. So, statement B is a true statement.

tests.name: Work and Energy  Test-02
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Work and Energy Test-02 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question:
Statement A: Any physical or mental activity is termed as work done in physics.
Statement B: Work is said to be +ve work done when the body is displaced in the direction of applied force.
Which of the two statements is true?
questions.explanation:
In general, work represents any physical or mental activity. But work has a precise meaning in physics. Work is said to be done when a net force acts on a body and the body undergoes displacement. So statement A is false.
Work done by a force is positive if the applied force has a component in the direction of the displacement. So, statement B is true.

questions.question: The work done is given by,
questions.explanation:
The simplest formula for mechanical work (in Physics) is W = Fs.
Here, the force is constant and parallel to displacement and the body moves in a steady direction. W is work, F is force and s is displacement. The result is in Joules.

questions.question: An electric lamp of 100w is used for 5 hours per day calculate the units of energy consumed by a lamp in one day is:
questions.explanation:
Power of lamp = 100w = {tex}\frac{100}{1000}{/tex} = 0.1Kw, time = 5 hrs.
Electrical energy = power {tex}\times{/tex} time  E = 0.1kw {tex}\times{/tex} 5h = 0.5 kw - h. = 0.5 unit.
E = 0.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 = 0.5 units.

questions.question: In which of the following activities the work is said to be done?

options.html: Khushi is pushing a wall of a house but fails to do so

questions.explanation: No work is done in reading a book, pulling the wall of the house, and walking on a level road with book on his head as there is no change in the position of an object on which force is applied. Stretching string causes displacement after applying force, so it is work.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(a) Work (i) Watt
(b) Power (ii) Newton
(c) Energy (iii) Kilowatt-hour
(d) Force (iv) Joule
questions.explanation:
• Work is defined as a force acting upon an object to cause a displacement. The unit of work is Joule.
  • The rate of doing work is called power. The SI unit of power is the watt.
  • Energy is the capacity of doing work. a large quantity of energy is expressed in kilowatt-hour and is written as kWh.
  • The unit of force is Newton.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(a) W (i) Nm
(b) 1 KW (ii) 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 106J
(c) 1 KWh (iii) 1000 W
(d) 1 HP (iv) 746 W
questions.explanation:
• The SI unit of work is joule and is denoted as ‘J’, which is named after an English physicist James Prescott Joule. The 1 joule of work done is equal to 1N {tex}\times{/tex} 1 m.  Or, 1 joule = Nm
  • 1 kilowatt = 1000 watt
  • 1 kWh = 1 kW {tex}\times{/tex} 1h
    Or, 1kWh = 1000 joule {tex}\times{/tex} 3600 s
    Or, 1 kWh = 3600000 joule
    Or, 1kWh = 3.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 joule
  • 1 HP = 746 W

questions.question:
Which of the following is a unit of energy

  1. Newton metre
  2. Joule
  3. Kilowatt-hours
  4. Kilowatt
questions.explanation:
Joule is a unit of energy. A large quantity of energy is expressed in kilowatt-hour and is written as kWh.
Energy is capable of doing work, therefore, the SI unit of energy is the same as of work.
So, 1 joule = Nm is also a unit of energy.

questions.question:
What is necessary to do the work

  1. A force is acted on the object
  2. Object is displaced
  3. A force is acted on the object it is not displaced
  4. All of these
questions.explanation:
When force is exerted on an object and object is displaced, work is said to be done.
Work = Force {tex}\times{/tex} Displacement
An essential condition for work to be done is

  1. A force should act on an object.
  2. The object must be displaced.

questions.question: K. E of a body is always:
questions.explanation: The kinetic energy of a body is K.E ={tex}\frac{1}{2} {\text{ }}m{v^2}{/tex}. Kinetic energy depends upon the mass of the body and velocity. Since mass and velocity never be negative, kinetic energy is always positive.

questions.question: If the momentum of a body is doubled, the K. E becomes:
questions.explanation:
The momentum of an object with mass m and traveling at a velocity v is given by M = m.v and the kinetic energy is given by K.E. = {tex}\frac12{/tex} mv2
The momentum of the object can be made double by either increasing its mass to twice the initial mass with the velocity remaining the same or by increasing the velocity to twice the initial value.
Assuming the mass of the object is not altered and only the velocity is increased to twice the initial value, the kinetic energy of the object is increased to {tex}\frac12{/tex}m (2v)2 = 4 ({tex}\frac12{/tex}) mv2, or its changes of four-time the initial value.
So, If the momentum of an object is doubled by increasing its velocity, its kinetic energy becomes 4 times the initial value.

questions.question: In a watch P. E of wound spring is converted into:
questions.explanation: A wound-up watch spring has elastic potential energy due to its wound upstate. As the spring unwinds, the PE stored in it changes into kinetic energy which does work in moving the hands of the watch.

questions.question: A body is thrown vertically upwards, during its rise the P. E ________ and K.E ________.
questions.explanation: When a body is thrown vertically upwards, during its upward movement the potential energy is increase due to increase in height and kinetic energy decreases due to decrease in velocity.

questions.question: The point of maximum K. E in case of oscillating pendulum is ________ position.

options.html: Mid print of mean and extreme

questions.explanation: Potential energy is maximum at the extremes. When it again starts its motion towards the mean position then the potential energy is converted into kinetic energy. Thus at the mean position, the kinetic energy is maximum but potential energy is minimum. At the extreme position, the P.E is maximum but the K.E is minimum.

questions.question: In which of the following mentioned activities, the P. E is converted into K.E.
questions.explanation: The pendulum shows the principal of conservation of energy in action. Gravitational potential energy is converted to kinetic energy and back, over and over again, as the pendulum swings.

questions.question: The gravitational P. E depends on
questions.explanation: The gravitational potential energy of an object depends on its mass, how high it is above the ground and the strength of the gravitational field. P.E = mgh.

tests.instructionHtml:
This is Work and Energy Test-03 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
