  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Which one is not a bacterial disease?
questions.explanation: Cholera, tuberculosis, and anthrax are bacterial diseases. Cholera is caused by bacterium Vibrio cholerae. A type of bacteria called mycobacterium tuberculosis causes Tuberculosis. Anthrax is an infection caused by the bacterium Bacillus anthracis. It can occur in four forms: skin, inhalation, intestinal, and injection. Influenza is a viral disease that shows a very high fever, vomiting, and body ache.

questions.question: Diarrhoea is caused by:
questions.explanation: Diarrhea is caused by bacteria E.coli, protozoa Entamoeba histolytica and virus Rotavirus. So, diarrhea is caused by bacteria, virus as well as protozoa.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Protozoa (i) Chicken Pox
(b) Virus (ii) Elephantiasis
(c) Bacteria (iii) Tuberculosis
(d) Worms (iv) Malaria
questions.explanation:
Matched responses are given below.

(a) Protozoa (iv) Malaria
(b) Virus (i) Chicken Pox
(c) Bacteria (iii) Tuberculosis
(d) Worms (ii) Elephantiasis

Malaria is caused by a protozoan microbe (plasmodium). A female Anopheles mosquito can be a carrier of such microbes. Chickenpox, also known as varicella, is a highly contagious disease caused by the Varicella zoster virus (VZV). Tuberculosis (TB) is a serious infection caused by bacteria which affects the lungs and sometimes other parts of the body such as the bones, the joints, and the kidneys. Elephantiasis is a chronic disease caused by a species of worms.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response:

(a) Air-borne disease (i) Gonorrhoea
(b) Water borne disease (ii) Cholera
(c) Carriers (iii) Pneumonia
(d) Physical contact (iv) Rabies
questions.explanation:
Pneumonia is an air-borne disease; it spreads through the air and targets the lungs. Cholera is a water-borne disease. Rabies spreads through animal bite. Gonorrhea is a sexually transmitted bacterial infection; it spreads through sexual contact.

(a) Air-borne disease (iii) Pneumonia
(b) Waterborne disease (ii) Cholera
(c) Carriers (iv) Rabies
(d) Physical contact (i) Gonorrhea

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response:

(a) Sleeping sickness (i) Ascaris lumbricoides
(b) Kala-azar (ii) Trypanosoma
(c) Roundworm found in the human intestine (iii) Staphylococci
(d) Acne (iv) Leishmania
questions.explanation:
The correct order of match is given as:
(a) - (ii), (b) - (iv), (c) - (i), (d) - (iii)

  • Sleeping sickness, also called human African trypanosomiasis, is a widespread tropical disease. It is spread by the bite of an infected tsetse fly, a species native to Africa.
  • Leishmania is a genus of trypanosomes (protozoan microbe) that are responsible for the disease leishmaniasis. Visceral leishmaniasis (VL), also known as kala-azar, black fever, and Dumdum fever, is the most severe form of leishmaniasis.
  • Ascaris is a genus of parasitic nematode worms known as the "small intestinal roundworms".
  • Acne is caused by staphylococci - a type of spherical bacteria commonly found on the skin.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) AIDS (i) Swelling and pain
(b) Inflammation (ii) Streptomycin
(c) Vaccination (iii) Develops immune system
(d) Antibiotic (iv) Weak immune system
questions.explanation:
The correct order of match is given as:

(a) AIDS (iv) Weak immune system
(b) Inflammation (i) Swelling and pain
(c) Vaccination (iii) Develops the immune system
(d) Antibiotic (ii) Streptomycin

The incidence of AIDS results in a weak immune system. HIV damages the functions of the immune system. Inflammation is a localized physical condition in which the affected part of the body becomes reddened, swollen, hot, and often painful, especially as a reaction to an injury or an infection. Vaccination is a treatment with a vaccine to produce immunity against a disease. Thus, vaccination develops the immune system. Streptomycin is an antibiotic used to treat a number of bacterial infections.

questions.question: What is the full form of BCG?
questions.explanation: BCG stands for Bacillus Calmette Guerin. This vaccine prevents persons from tuberculosis (TB). The BCG vaccine was first used medically in 1921. It was developed in 1908 by Albert Calmette, a French physician and bacteriologist, and his assistant and later colleague, Camille Guérin, a veterinarian, who worked at the Institut Pasteur de Lille (Lille, France).

questions.question:
Find the incorrect pair:

  1. Polio – Tuberculosis
  2. Hepatitis B – Hepatitis
  3. DTP – Diphtheria, tetanus, pertussis and influenza type B
  4. BCG – TB
questions.explanation: Hepatitis B vaccine is given for protection against Hepatitis disease. DPT is a vaccine given for Diptheria, Pertussis, and Tetanus, and influenza type B. BCG stands for Bacillus Calmette Guerin. BCG vaccine is given for protection against tuberculosis. Polio and tuberculosis are two different diseases. Therefore, Polio – Tuberculosis is the incorrect pair.

questions.question: Who discovered small pox vaccine?
questions.explanation: Vaccine for smallpox was discovered by Edward Jenner. Smallpox is a viral disease. The vaccine of smallpox includes suspension of dead or killed virus that develops antibodies within the body. Robert Koch was the first pathologist who established that diseases are mainly caused by pathogens. Disease-causing microbes are called pathogens. Robert Brown discovered the nucleus. Rudolf Virchow made contributions to the cell theory.

questions.question: Out of all the macromolecules found in cells, the most diverse ones, both physically as well as chemically, are:
questions.explanation: Carbohydrates, proteins, fats, etc are the macromolecules found in cells. These macromolecules perform different functions. Proteins are the most diverse macromolecules, both physically as well as chemically. Proteins form different kinds of products within the body.

questions.question: Iron is present in:
questions.explanation: Iron is present in Myoglobin muscle fibers. Myoglobin (symbol Mb or MB) is an iron- and oxygen-binding protein found in the muscle tissue of vertebrates in general and in almost all mammals. Myoglobin muscles help in the contraction and relaxation of muscles for movement.

questions.question: Fat-soluble vitamins are:
questions.explanation: Vitamin A, D, E, and K are fat-soluble vitamins whereas vitamin B and C are water-soluble vitamins. Vitamins are classified as either water-soluble or fat-soluble. There are 13 vitamins that the body needs. Vitamins such as vitamins A, D, E, and K are the 4 fat-soluble vitamins. There are 9 water-soluble vitamins (8 B vitamins and vitamin C). Water-soluble vitamins dissolve easily in water and, in general, are readily excreted from the body.

questions.question: When was the Pulse Polio Immunization Programme launched in India?
questions.explanation: The pulse polio immunization programme was launched in India in 1995-1996 following the Global Polio Eradication Initiative of the World Health Organization (WHO). Pulse Polio is an immunization campaign established by the government of India to eliminate poliomyelitis (polio) in India by vaccinating all children under the age of five years against the poliovirus. The campaign fights poliomyelitis through a large-scale pulse vaccination programme and monitoring of polio cases.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) OPV (i) Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus
(b) DPT (ii) Tetanus
(c) BCG (iii) Tuberculosis
(iv) TT (iv) Polio
questions.explanation:
Vaccine   Disease(s) Explanation
(a) OPV (iv) Polio OPV are Oral Polio Vaccines. OPV is the predominant vaccine used in the campaign to eradicate polio.
(b) DPT (i) Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus DPT refers to a class of combination vaccines against three infectious diseases in humans: Diphtheria, Pertussis (whooping cough), and Tetanus.
(c) BCG (iii) Tuberculosis Bacillus Calmette–Guérin (BCG) vaccine is a vaccine primarily used against Tuberculosis. It is given to infants for protection against Tuberculosis (TB).
(d) TT (ii) Tetanus Tetanus toxoid is a vaccine for prevention against tetanus.

questions.question:
AIDS can be transmitted by:

  1. Sexual contact
  2. Handshake
  3. Hugs
  4. Breast feeding
questions.explanation: AIDS can be transmitted from one partner to another through sexual contact. It can also be transmitted through blood to blood contact with infected people or from an infected mother to her baby during pregnancy or through breast-feeding. It is not transmitted through casual physical contacts like handshakes or hugs.

tests.name: Why Do We Fall Ill Test-04
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Why Do We Fall Ill Test-04 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question:
Find the incorrect statement:

  1. 24th June is the World TB Day.
  2. 25th April is the World Malaria Day.
  3. December 1 is the World AIDS Day.
  4. TAB – vaccine provides immunity for 3 years.
questions.explanation: World Tuberculosis Day is observed on March 24 each year; it is designed to build public awareness about the global epidemic of tuberculosis (TB) worldwide and the status of TB prevention and care efforts. World AIDS Day, designated on 1 December every year since 1988, is dedicated to raising awareness of the AIDS pandemic caused by the spread of HIV. World Malaria Day(WMD) is an international observance commemorated every year on 25 April and recognizes global efforts to control malaria. TAB vaccine (typhoid - paratyphoid A and B vaccine) provides immunity for 3 years.

questions.question: Which of the following condition(s) is/are essential for good health?

options.html: Complete physical fitness, free from any disease.

questions.explanation: Good health is the condition of complete physical fitness, free from disease, perfect mental health, and perfect social well-being. Health is a state of being well enough to function well physically, mentally, and socially. It depends on the surroundings or the environment. Living in a clean environment under good economic conditions, and social equality and harmony are important for individual health.

questions.question: Mineral deposition in bones and teeth is due to:
questions.explanation: Vitamins have diverse biochemical functions. Some, such as vitamin D, have hormone-like functions as regulators of mineral metabolism. Mineral deposition in bones and teeth is due to vitamin D. Vitamin D helps in the absorption of minerals present in milk and other food items.

questions.question: Name the enzyme present in tears which prevents eye infections.
questions.explanation: The enzyme present in tears is the Lysozyme enzyme. It is an antimicrobial enzyme that prevents eye infection by creating a physiological barrier. Bacterial cell walls contain a layer of peptidoglycan, which is the specific site that Lysozyme targets.

questions.question: The chemical name of vitamin K is:
questions.explanation: The chemical name of vitamin K is Phylloquinone that helps in blood clotting during injury. Vitamin K (Phylloquinone) is a fat-soluble vitamin that can be obtained from dairy and plant sources. Phylloquinone is often called vitamin K1.

questions.question: Vitamin K helps in:
questions.explanation: Vitamin K helps in blood coagulation. During an injury, loss of blood can be prevented through the clotting of blood. The German-speaking scientists who isolated and described vitamin K (in addition to naming it as such) did so because the vitamin is intimately involved in the coagulation of blood following wounding (from the German word - Koagulation).

questions.question: Niacin helps in the formation of:
questions.explanation: Niacin is also known as nicotinic acid. It is an organic compound with the formula C6H5NO2. Niacin (Vitamin B3) helps in the formation of NAD (Nicotine Amide Diphosphate). NAD is the energy-storing molecules present in the cell.

questions.question: The commonest anti-malarial drug is obtained from:
questions.explanation: The most common anti-malarial drug is obtained from the bark of the cinchona tree. The bark contains the alkaloid quinine along with several other alkaloids effective against malaria.

questions.question: Blood capillaries can easily rupture due to the deficiency of:
questions.explanation: Blood capillaries can easily rupture due to the deficiency of Vitamin C. Bleeding gums are due to the deficiency of vitamin C (Ascorbic acid).

questions.question:
Find the Incorrect statement:

  1. Malnutrition and under-nutrition are similar.
  2. Lactating mothers need more proteins.
  3. Egg is a good source of vitamins, minerals and proteins.
  4. Soyabean is one of the richest sources of proteins.
questions.explanation: Eggs are a good source of Vitamins, minerals and proteins. Soyabean is one of the richest sources of proteins. Lactating mothers need more proteins. Malnutrition is the absence of nutrients from food. Under-nutrition is the absence of a balanced diet. Under-nutrition is a deficiency of calories, or of one or more essential nutrients. Under-nutrition may develop because people cannot obtain or prepare food, have a disorder that makes eating or absorbing food difficult, or have a greatly increased need for calories.

questions.question: A doctor advised a patient to increase the intake of a particular vitamin in his diet to improve his vision in dim light. Name the vitamin.
questions.explanation: A vitamin is an organic compound and a vital nutrient that an organism requires in limited amounts. Vitamin A is required for proper vision. It includes the compounds retinal, retinol, and four known carotenoids. So, the particular vitamin that has been advised by the doctor in the given case to improve the vision of the patient in dim light is vitamin A.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Vector (i) Viral disease
(b) Rabies (ii) AIDS
(c) Long lasting effect (iii) Cigarette smoking
(d) HIV (iv) Female Anopheles
questions.explanation:
The correct order of the match is given below.

(a) Vector (iv) Female Anopheles
(b) Rabies (i) Viral disease
(c) Long-lasting effect (iii) Cigarette smoking
(d) HIV (ii) AIDS

Animals that carry infecting agents from a sick person to another potential host are called vectors. The most common vectors are mosquitoes. Female Anopheles mosquito is a vector of plasmodium that causes malaria. Rabies is a viral disease that causes inflammation of the brain in humans and other mammals. Cigarette smoking has long-lasting effects. A virus (HIV) causes AIDS (Acquired Immuno-Deficiency Syndrome).

questions.question:
Choose the correct statement:

  1. High blood pressure is caused by excessive weight and lack of exercise.
  2. Cancers can be caused by genetic abnormalities.
  3. Peptic ulcers are caused by eating acidic food.
  4. Acne is not caused by staphylococci.
questions.explanation: Peptic ulcers are caused by a bacterium - Helicobacter pylori (H. pylori). Acne is caused by a bacterium - Staphylococci. Hence, C and D are incorrect. Eating acidic/spicy food may increase the odds of an ulcer. High blood pressure can be caused by excessive weight and lack of exercise. Some cancers are caused by genetic abnormalities. Hence, A and B are correct.

questions.question:
Which of the following is not an antibiotic?

  1. Penicillin
  2. Insulin
  3. Streptomycin
  4. BCG
questions.explanation: Penicillin is one of the first, and still one of the most widely used antibiotic agents, derived from the Penicillium mold. It works by indirectly bursting bacterial cell walls. Streptomycin is an antibiotic used to treat a number of bacterial infections. Insulin is a hormone produced by the pancreas that allows the body to use sugar (glucose) from carbohydrates in the food and to store glucose for future use. Bacillus Calmette–Guérin (BCG) is a vaccine primarily used against Tuberculosis. Insulin and BCG are not antibiotics.

questions.question:
Which of the following statement(s) is/are incorrect?

  1. Infectious diseases are caused by micro-organisms.
  2. Antibiotics work against viral infections.
  3. Vaccines develop immunity in the children.
  4. Female anopheles is a causal organism of malaria.
questions.explanation: B and D are incorrect. A and C are correct statements. Antibiotics are the chemicals that kill or stop the growth of certain kinds of microbes like bacteria. Antibiotics do not work against viral infections because viruses have very few biochemical mechanisms of their own. Viruses enter host cells and use the machinery of the host cell for their life processes. The causal organism of malaria is plasmodium. Female anopheles is a vector of malaria. Animals that carry infecting agents from a sick person to another potential host are called vectors.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct responses:

(a) Hepatitis (i) Brain
(b) Japanese encephalitis (ii) Skin
(c) Dengue (iii) Platelets
(d) Fungal diseases (iv) Liver
questions.explanation:
The correct order of match is (a) - (iv), (b) - (i),(c) - (iii), (d) - (ii).

  • Hepatitis targets the liver.
  • Japanese encephalitis targets the brain.
  • Dengue leads to a drop in platelets.
  • Fungal diseases affect the skin.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response:

(a) Genetic disease (i) Diabetes
(b) Deficiency disease (ii) Haemophilia
(c) Metabolic disorder (iii) AIDS
(d) Infectious disease (iv) Goitre
questions.explanation:
• Haemophilia is a mostly inherited genetic disorder that impairs the body's ability to make blood clots. Clotting is a process needed to stop bleeding. This results in people bleeding longer after an injury, easy bruising, and an increased risk of bleeding inside joints or the brain.
  • Goiter (swelling in the neck resulting from an enlarged thyroid gland) is a deficiency disease. It is caused by the deficiency of iodine.
  • Diabetes is a group of metabolic disorders in which a person has high blood sugar levels over a prolonged period.
  • AIDS is an infectious disease caused by a virus.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response:

(a) Protozoa (i) Elephantiasis
(b) Virus (ii) Chicken Pox
(c) Bacteria (iii) Malaria
(d) Worms (iv) Tuberculosis
questions.explanation:
Matched responses are as follows:

(a) Protozoa (iii) Malaria
(b) Virus (ii) Chicken Pox
(c) Bacteria (iv) Tuberculosis
(d) Worms (i) Elephantiasis

Malaria is caused by a protozoan microbe (plasmodium). A female Anopheles mosquito can be a carrier of such microbes. Tuberculosis (TB) is a serious infection caused by bacteria which affects the lungs and sometimes other parts of the body such as the bones, the joints, and the kidneys. Elephantiasis is a chronic disease caused by a species of worms. Chickenpox, also known as varicella, is a highly contagious disease caused by the varicella-zoster virus (VZV).

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Air-borne disease (i) Pneumonia
(b) Waterborne disease (ii) Rabies
(c) Carriers (iii) Cholera
(d) Sexual contact (iv) Gonorrhoea
questions.explanation:
Matched responses are as follows:

(a) Air-borne disease (i) Pneumonia
(b) Water-borne disease (ii) Cholera
(c) Carriers (iii) Rabies
(d) Sexual contact (iv) Gonorrhea
  • Pneumonia is an air-borne disease; it spreads through the air and targets the lungs.
  • Cholera is a water-borne disease.
  • Rabies spreads through an animal bite.
  • Gonorrhea is a sexually transmitted bacterial infection; it spreads through sexual contact.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response:

(a) Acute disease (i) Long-lasting
(b) Chronic disease (ii) Blocks biochemical pathways
(c) Vaccine (iii) Suspension of dead or weak disease-causing microbes
(d) Antibiotics (iv) Short duration
questions.explanation:
Matched responses area as follows:

(a) Acute disease (iv) Short duration
(b) Chronic disease (i) Long-lasting
(c) Vaccine (iii) Suspension of dead or weak disease-causing microbes
(d) Antibiotics (ii) Blocks biochemical pathways

Diseases are classified as acute or chronic, depending on their duration. Acute diseases such as common cold last only for a short duration. Chronic diseases last for a long time. A vaccine is a biological preparation that provides active acquired immunity to a particular disease. A vaccine typically contains an agent that resembles a disease-causing microorganism and is often made from weakened or killed forms of the microbe. Antibiotics block the biochemical pathways that are used by microbes for the synthesis of substances/respiration, without affecting the biochemical pathways of human beings.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Sleeping sickness (i) Ascaris lumbricoides
(b) Kala-azar (ii) Trypanosoma
(c) Roundworm found in the human intestine (iii) Staphylococci
(d) Acne (iv) Leishmania
questions.explanation:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Sleeping sickness (ii) Trypanosoma
(b) Kala-azar (iv) Leishmania
(c) Roundworm found in the human intestine (i) Ascaris lumbricoides
(d) Acne (iii) Staphylococci

Sleeping sickness, also called human African trypanosomiasis, is a widespread tropical disease. It is spread by the bite of an infected tsetse fly, a species native to Africa. Leishmania is a genus of trypanosomes (protozoan microbe) that are responsible for the disease leishmaniasis. Visceral leishmaniasis (VL), also known as kala-azar, black fever, and Dumdum fever, is the most severe form of leishmaniasis. Ascaris is a genus of parasitic nematode worms known as the "small intestinal roundworms". Acne is caused by staphylococci - a type of spherical bacteria commonly found on the skin.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) AIDS (i) Streptomycin
(b) Inflammation (ii) Swelling and pain
(c) Vaccination (iii) Weak immune system
(d) Injection for tuberculosis (iv) Develops the immune system
questions.explanation:
Matched responses are as follows:

(a) AIDS (iii) Weak immune system
(b) Inflammation (ii) Swelling and pain
(c) Vaccination (iv) Develops the immune system
(d) Injection for tuberculosis (i) Streptomycin

The incidence of AIDS results in a weak immune system. HIV damages the functions of the immune system. Inflammation is a localized physical condition in which part of the body becomes reddened, swollen, hot, and often painful, especially as a reaction to injury or infection. Vaccination is a treatment with a vaccine to produce immunity against a disease. Thus, vaccination develops the immune system. Streptomycin is an antibiotic used to treat a number of bacterial infections. These also include tuberculosis.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) OPV (i) Polio
(b) DPT (ii) Tuberculosis
(c) BCG (iii) Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus
(d) TT (iv) Tetanus
questions.explanation:
Matched responses are as follows:

(a) OPV (i) Polio
(b) DPT (iii) Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus
(c) BCG (ii) Tuberculosis
(d) TT (iv) Tetanus
  • OPV are Oral Polio Vaccines. They are the predominant vaccine used in the fight to eradicate polio.
  • DPT refers to a class of combination vaccines against three infectious diseases in humans: diphtheria, pertussis (whooping cough), and tetanus.
  • Bacillus Calmette–Guérin (BCG) vaccine is a vaccine primarily used against Tuberculosis. It is given to infants for protection against Tuberculosis (TB).
  • Tetanus toxoid (TT) is a vaccine to prevent tetanus.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response:

(a) Vector (i) Viral disease
(b) Rabies (ii) AIDS
(c) Long-lasting effect (iii) Cigarette smoking
(d) HIV (iv) Female Anopheles
questions.explanation:
Matched responses are as follows:

(a) Vector (iv) Female Anopheles
(b) Rabies (i) Viral disease
(c) Long-lasting effect (iii) Cigarette smoking
(d) HIV (ii) AIDS
  • Animals that carry infecting agents from a sick person to another potential host are called vectors. The most common vectors are mosquitoes. Female Anopheles mosquito is a vector of plasmodium that causes malaria.
  • Rabies is a viral disease that causes inflammation of the brain in humans and other mammals.
  • Cigarette smoking has long-lasting effects.
  • HIV causes AIDS (Acquired Immuno-Deficiency Syndrome).

questions.question:
Choose the correct statement:

  1. Peptic ulcers are caused by eating acidic food.
  2. Cancers can be caused by genetic abnormalities.
  3. High blood pressure is caused by excessive weight and lack of exercise.
  4. Acne is not caused by staphylococci.
questions.explanation:
• Peptic ulcer is caused by a bacterium - Helicobacter pylori. 
  • Acne is caused by Staphylococci.

Hence, C and D are incorrect statements.

  • High blood pressure can be caused by excessive weight and lack of exercise.
  • Some cancers are caused by genetic abnormalities. 

Hence, A and D are correct statements.

questions.question:
AIDS can be transmitted by:

  1. Sexual contact
  2. Handshake
  3. Hugs
  4. Breast-feeding
questions.explanation: AIDS can be transmitted through sexual contact from one partner to another. It can also be transmitted through blood to blood contact with infected people or from an infected mother to her baby during pregnancy or through breast-feeding. It is not transmitted through casual physical contacts like handshakes or hugs.

questions.question:
Which of the following is important for good health?

  1. Living in a clean space.
  2. Living in a large furnished house.
  3. Social equality & harmony.
  4. Balanced diet.
questions.explanation: Health is a state of being well enough to function well physically, mentally, and socially. It depends upon the surroundings or the environment. Living in a clean environment under good economic conditions, and social equality and harmony are important for individual health. Good economic conditions are needed so that an individual can work and earn, and get food for nutrition. Living in a large and well-furnished house is not important for individual health.

questions.question:
Which of the following is/are not antibiotic(s)?

  1. Penicillin
  2. Insulin
  3. Streptomycin
  4. BCG
questions.explanation: Penicillin is one of the first, and still one of the most widely used antibiotic agents, derived from the Penicillium mold. It works by indirectly bursting bacterial cell walls. Streptomycin is an antibiotic used to treat a number of bacterial infections. Insulin is a hormone produced by the pancreas that allows the body to use sugar (glucose) from carbohydrates in the food and to store glucose for future use. Bacillus Calmette–Guérin (BCG) is a vaccine primarily used against Tuberculosis.

questions.question:
Which of the following constitute the different states of health?

  1. Physical well-being
  2. Social well-being
  3. Economic well-being
  4. Mental well-being
questions.explanation: Health is a state of being well enough to function well physically, mentally, and socially.

questions.question:
Which of the following statement is incorrect?

  1. Infectious diseases are caused by micro-organisms.
  2. Antibiotics work against viral infections.
  3. Vaccines develop immunity in children.
  4. Female anopheles is a causal organism of malaria.
questions.explanation: B and D are incorrect. Antibiotics are the chemicals that kill or stop the growth of certain kinds of microbes like bacteria. Antibiotics do not work against viral infections because viruses have very few biochemical mechanisms of their own. Viruses enter host cells and use the machinery of the host cell for their life processes. The causal organism of malaria is plasmodium. Female anopheles is a vector of malaria. Animals that carry infecting agents from a sick person to another potential host are called vectors.

questions.question: Which of the following factor does not influence the movement of air?

options.html: Topography over which the wind passes.
Vaporization and condensation of water vapors

questions.explanation:
As the air rises, it expands and cools. This cooling causes the water vapour in the air to condense in the form of tiny droplets. Vaporization can be defined as the process in which the liquid state changes into the vapor state. Hence, the Vaporization and condensation of water vapors do not influence the movement of air.
The effects of topography on the climate of any given region are powerful. Mountain ranges create barriers that alter wind and precipitation patterns. Topographical features such as amplify winds. Rotation and revolution of the earth also affect the movement of air.

questions.question: Which of the following planet have an atmosphere?
questions.explanation: The envelope of air surrounding the surface of planets is called atmosphere. Earth, Venus, and Mars all these planets have their own atmosphere.

questions.question: The major component of the atmosphere in Venus and Mars planet is-
questions.explanation: The atmosphere of Venus and Mars planet consists of mainly carbon dioxide gas. Due to the presence of a higher amount of carbon dioxide gas temperature increases very high during the day and low during the night.

questions.question: What are the two forms of oxygen found in the atmosphere-
questions.explanation: In the atmosphere, oxygen is found in the form of ozone (O3) and oxygen (O2). Oxygen is found in lower layers and ozone in the ozonosphere.

questions.question: Name two biologically important compounds that contain both oxygen and nitrogen.
questions.explanation: Biologically important compounds that contain both oxygen and nitrogen are protein and nucleic acid. These compounds contain oxygen and nitrogen besides carbon and hydrogen.

questions.question: If there were no atmosphere surrounding the earth, then the temperature of the earth will be

options.html: Increases during day decreases during night

questions.explanation: The atmosphere plays a very important role in maintaining the temperature of the earth. If there were no atmosphere surrounding the earth, then the temperature of the earth increases during the day and decrease during the night.

questions.question: The major part of freshwater found in:
questions.explanation: Major part of freshwater is located on mountain tops and glaciers in the form of ice caps and glacier i.e., in the frozen form. Major portion of water found on earth is salty and found in the ocean and seas.

questions.question: Rise in temperature of water due to addition of hot water into it is known as
questions.explanation: Rise is temperature of water due to addition of hot water into it is known as thermal pollution. Thermal pollution causes death of aquatic organisms.

questions.question: Percentage of total water found as fresh water is ________.
questions.explanation: The percentage of total water found as freshwater is only 2.5%. Out of this percent of the water, most are in the form of ice caps and glaciers. The major portion of water is salty and found in the ocean and sea.

questions.question: Which of the following has the smallest soil particles?
questions.explanation: Soil is a mixture of sand particles, humus, and other organic substances along with microbes. Different soil has particles of different sizes. Clay soil has the smallest soil particles and thus its water holding capacity is high.

questions.question: Soil erosion can be prevented by _____
questions.explanation: Soil erosion can be prevented by afforestation and reforestation. Reforestation is the restoration of forests, which have been destroyed by deforestation, while afforestation is planting of new forests. Plants hold soil with their roots and thus prevent erosion.

questions.question: Wind causes weathering of rocks through
questions.explanation: The process of breaking down large rock particles into small soil particles is called weathering. Wind causes weathering of rocks through abrasion on its surface i.e., friction due to air pressure.

questions.question: The kind of soil water most useful to plants is
questions.explanation: Plants require water for all physiological processes. Water is present in the soil as hygroscopic, gravitational, and capillary water but water available for plant for absorption is capillary water.

questions.question: Which of the following factors promote soil erosion?

options.html: Tillage, scraping and falling of tree

questions.explanation:
1. Tillage is the agricultural preparation of soil by mechanical agitation of various types, such as digging, stirring, and overturning. 
  2. Scraping is a small amount of something that has been obtained by scraping it from a surface.
  3. Falling of trees means deforestation promote soil erosion.

questions.question: Top-soil contains the following:
questions.explanation: Topsoil is the uppermost fertile soil layer. Topsoil contains humus, living organisms, and soil particles.

  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(1) Prevention of soil erosion (A) Cholera
(2) Water borne disease (B) Top soil
(3) Mineral in soil (C) Weathering of parent rock
(4) Most fertile layer (D) Afforestation
questions.explanation:
1. Soil cover and reforestation is a mechanism to protect soil from water loss. Like the protection of an umbrella, soil cover and reforestation protects the soil and the microbes within from the impact of sun heat, rain and wind. It stops the soil surface from sealing, and reduces the amount of precious rainwater that runs off.
  2. Water borne disease is cholera. The bacteria cause cholera.
  3. Soil minerals form the basis of soil. They are produced from rocks (parent material) through the processes of weathering and natural erosion.
  4. Topsoil is the top most layer of soil and is the most fertile because it contains the organic, nutrient-rich materials.

questions.question:
Which of the following increases soil erosion

  1. Raising forests
  2. Overgrazing by animals
  3. Step farming
  4. Deforestation
questions.explanation: Overgrazing deforestation causes loosening of soil particles of top soil which enhances soil erosion.

questions.question:
Weathering of rocks occurs by-

  1. Lichens
  2. Frost
  3. Fossil fuels
  4. Fungi
questions.explanation:
• Lichen releases hydrolytic enzymes which break the rocks into small particles.
  • Frost is the ice crystal that gets deposited on the rocks which help in the contraction of rocks due to which cracks formed on it.

questions.question: Which of the following is a non-biodegradable pollutant?
questions.explanation: Those substances that can be broken down into simpler forms by the action of microbes are called biodegradable substances, leaves, fruits, and vegetables and mud are biodegradable substances. Plastic cannot be broken into simpler forms so, it is an example of non-biodegradable pollutants.

questions.question: Which of the following is the cause of water pollution?
questions.explanation: Water pollution is the addition of a harmful substance in water that changes its chemical, physical and biological properties. Water pollution is caused by all of them i.e., eutrophication, pesticide toxicity and bio magnifications.

questions.question: Which one is not correct for the use of a large amount of fertilizers and pesticides?

options.html: They turn the fields barren after some time.
They affect the useful component from the soil.

questions.explanation: The use of a large amount of fertilizers and pesticides turns the fields barren after some time, they affect the useful components of the soil and destroy the soil fertility. Fertilizers and pesticides are not eco-friendly.

questions.question: The major cause of ozone depletion is
questions.explanation: The major cause of ozone layer depletion includes CFCs (Chlorofluro carbons) that are released from fridges and air conditioners. This gas breaks the ozone (O3) into free oxygen.

questions.question:
Find the correct statement :

  1. Water hyacinth can be exploited to control water pollution.
  2. Corbett National park is in the state of U. P.
  3. Forest do not check flood and soil erosion.
  4. Coal is the common source of energy in Indian Villages.
questions.explanation: Water hyacinth can be exploited to control water pollution but reduce the oxygen in the water. Corbett National park is in the state of Uttarakhand. Forest check flood and soil erosion. Cow dung cakes and woods are the common sources of energy in villages.

questions.question: What is ODS?
questions.explanation: Ozone depleting substances (ODS) are those substances that deplete the ozone layer. These substances includes chlorofluorocarbons other gases.

questions.question: Toxic chemical released by paper industry is
questions.explanation: The toxic chemical released by the paper industry is Mercury. Mercury is used to decolorize the paper and make it smoother.

questions.question: Long exposure of UV  light to man cause-
questions.explanation: Direct  exposure of UV light to man causes skin cancer. Depletion of the ozone layer allows ultraviolet rays to enter the earth surface which in turn may cause skin cancer to human beings. Exposure to UV rays can cause premature aging of the skin and signs of sun damage such as wrinkles, leathery skin, liver spots, actinic keratosis, and solar elastosis. UV rays can also cause eye problems.

questions.question: Lichens are sensitive to which component of air pollution?
questions.explanation: Lichens are used as an indicator of pollution. Lichens do not grow in the area having very high pollution. Lichens are sensitive to Sulphur dioxide gas present in polluted air.

questions.question:
Statement A: Ozone hole was first noticed over Antarctica in 1985.
Statement B: Xenobiotic are man-made substances which can easily be degraded by natural process.
Which of the two statements is/are true?

options.html: Both A and B are false

questions.explanation: Ozone hole was first noticed over Antarctica in 1985. Through this hole, ultraviolet rays reach to earth. Xenobiotic or man-made substances may be biotic or abiotic.

questions.question:
Match the type of pollution with the correct example.

(1) Water Pollution (A) Hot water
(2) Air pollution (B) Acid rain
(3) Soil Pollution (C) Eutrophication
(4) Thermal pollution (D) Soil erosion
questions.explanation:
• Water pollution is caused by the mixing of organic wastes, a process called eutrophication.
  • Water pollution is mainly caused by acid rain due to excessive air pollution.
  • Soil pollution is caused by the removal of topsoil called soil erosion.
  • Thermal pollution is caused by the heating of water through sunlight and geothermal energy.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(1) Inexhaustible (A) Minerals
(2) Exhaustible (B) Winds
(3) Clean fuel (C) CNG
(4) Hot air (D) Solar energy
questions.explanation:
• Inexhaustible sources are those which can be used again and again and can not be destroyed like solar energy, wind energy, etc.
  • Exhaustible sources are those which can be replenished again and again.
  • CNG is clean fuel as it not produces any harmful gases.
  • Air becomes hotter due to solar energy.

tests.name: Natural Resources Test-03
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Natural Resources Test-03 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(1) Water Pollution (A) Hot water
(2) Air pollution (B) Acid rain
(3) Soil Pollution (C) Eutrophication
(4) Thermal pollution (D) Soil erosion
questions.explanation:
• Water pollution is due to eutrophication i.e., mixing of organic wastes.
  • Air pollution causes acid rain due to increased concentration of poisonous gases like carbon dioxide, sulphur dioxide, etc.
  • Soil gets polluted as the topsoil is removed day by day due to increased uses of fertilizers in the agricultural fields.
  • Water gets polluted due to heating of it by sunlight.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(1) Photosynthesis (A) Rain
(2) Greenhouse effect (B) Trapping of solar energy
(3) Ozone depletion (C) Fix carbon dioxide
(4) Precipitation (D) Chloro-fluoro carbons
questions.explanation:
1. Plants produce oxygen during the process of photosynthesis by using carbon dioxide, sunlight, and water to create energy.
  2. The greenhouse effect refers to the change in the steady-state temperature of a planet or moon by the presence of an atmosphere containing gas that absorbs and emits infrared radiation. Greenhouse gases trap heat within the surface-troposphere system.
  3. Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) and other halogenated ozone depleting substances (ODS) are mainly responsible for man-made chemical ozone depletion.
  4. As the water in the clouds gets too heavy, the water falls back to the earth.  This is called precipitation.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(1) Greenhouse gas (A) Industrial effluent
(2) Acid rain (B) Methane
(3) Contamination of water (C) Nitrogenous fertilizers
(4) Industrial fixation (D) Oxides of sulphur and nitrogen
questions.explanation:
1. Greenhouse gases, which include water vapour, carbon dioxide and methane, warm the atmosphere by efficiently absorbing thermal infrared radiation emitted by the earth’s surface, by the atmosphere itself, and by clouds.
  2. Acid rain is caused by a chemical reaction that begins when compounds like sulfur dioxide and nitrogen oxides are released into the air.
  3. Water pollution signifies contamination of water bodies, which makes their water unfit for drinking and other purposes. There are basically five primary sources of water pollution―domestic sewage, agricultural runoff, industrial effluents, wastewater from septic tanks, and stormwater runoff.
  4. Nitrogen fixation, any natural or industrial process that causes free nitrogen (N2), which is a relatively inert gas plentiful in air, to combine chemically with other elements to form more-reactive nitrogen compounds such as ammonia, nitrates, or nitrites.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(1) Smog (A) Producers
(2) Biotic components (B) Coal
(3) Protective blanket (C) Ozone layer
(4) Fossil fuel (D) Smoke and fog
questions.explanation:
1. Smog is a mixture of fog and smoke. Formation of smog is a visible indication of air pollution.
  2. Living things constitute the biotic component of the biosphere. Means producers are  Biotic components .
  3. The ozone layer or ozone shield is a region of earth's stratosphere that absorbs most of the Sun's ultraviolet (UV) radiation. So, ozone layer act as protective blanket.
  4. Coal, petrol are fossil fuel.

questions.question:
Water pollution is

  1. Addition of undesirable substances into water bodies.
  2. Removal of desirable substances from water.
  3. Change of temperature.
  4. Addition of desirable substance into water.
questions.explanation:
The term water-pollution is used to cover the following effects:

  • The addition of undesirable substances to water-bodies: The fertilizers and pesticides used in farming or any poisonous substances, like mercury salts which are used by paper-industries are water pollutants. These could also be disease-causing organisms, like the bacteria which cause cholera.
  • The removal of desirable substances from water-bodies: Live animals and plants present in water uses dissolved oxygen. Any change that reduces the amount of dissolved oxygen would adversely affect these aquatic organisms. Other nutrients could also deplete from the water bodies.
  • A change in temperature: The eggs and larvae of various aquatic animals are susceptible to temperature changes. The aquatic organisms are used to a certain range of temperature in the water bodies where they live, and a sudden marked change in this temperature would be dangerous for them or affect their breeding.

So, A, B, and C are correct statements.

questions.question:
Which of the following is not correct for the use of large amount of fertilizers and pesticides

  1. They are eco-friendly.
  2. They turn the fields barren after some time.
  3. They adversely affect the component of soil
  4. They destroy the soil fertility.
questions.explanation: Fertilizers and pesticides are not eco-friendly as their excessive use causes the killing of lady bugs and flower bugs, which naturally protect the crops. When these pesticides mix with water and go to the river, it makes the river water not only unfit but dangerous for human beings and animals.

questions.question:
Which of the following method is used to control pollution?

  1. Installation of gobar gas plant
  2. Dumping of non-biodegradable waste in low lying areas
  3.  Use of coal & petrol
  4. Conversion of organic waste and fecal material into compost
questions.explanation:
1. Installation of gobar gas plant and availability of cooking gas, the slurry can improve living standards in rural areas, reduces dependency on LPG cylinders, reduces time wastage in collecting firewood, reduce pollution, produce good quality enriched biofertilizer to improve soil fertility and to promote organic farming.
  2. The dumping of non-biodegradable waste in low lying areas is causing land pollution.
  3. Use coal and petrol causes air pollution.
  4. Conversion of organic waste and fecal material into compost is used to control pollution.

questions.question: In which of the following state, water is found during the water cycle?
questions.explanation: During the water cycle, water exists in all three states, solid, liquid, and Gas. Due to evaporation and transpiration, liquid water changes into water vapour. On cooling, water vapour converts into solid state during cloud formation.

questions.question: Which step is not involved in carbon cycle-
questions.explanation: The process by which water is lost in the form of water vapour from the aerial part of the plant is called transpiration. Transpiration is not involved in the carbon cycle.

questions.question: Name the bacterium capable of nitrogen fixation which resides in the root nodules of legumes.
questions.explanation: Rhizobium leguminosarum bacterium is capable of nitrogen fixation which resides in the root nodules of legumes plants that convert atmospheric nitrogen into nitrate and nitrite.

questions.question: Both power and manure are provided by
questions.explanation: Both power and manure are provided by biogas plants. Anaerobic respiration of animal wastes and other organic material produce methane gas that is used as excellent fuel and outlets from biogas plant is used as manure.

questions.question: Nitrobacter changes
questions.explanation: Nitrobacterbacterium changes Nitrite into Nitrate which can be dissolved in water and can be absorbed by roots of plants. Nitrite and nitrate are forms of nitrogen.

questions.question: Amount of rainfall in the wet zones of India is
questions.explanation: The amount of rainfall in the wet zones of India is more than 200 cm. These areas receive higher rainfall and have lots of vegetation.

questions.question: Which organism cannot fix atmospheric nitrogen?
questions.explanation: Rhizobium, Nostoc, and Azatobacter are responsible for fixing nitrogen. They convert atmospheric nitrogen into nitrate and nitrite that can be absorbed by plants.

questions.question:
Match the following with correct response.

(1) Nitrogen fixation (A) Bacteria and fungi
(2) Ammonification (B) Pseudomonas
(3) Nitrification (C) Nitrosomonas
(4) Denitrification (D) Rhizobium
questions.explanation:
1. Nitrogen Fixation: Molecular nitrogen is converted into nitrates and nitrites by free living bacteria like Rhizobium present in the root nodules of legumes.
  2. Ammonification: When an animal or plant dies they release wastes from their bodies, nitrogen is released in the organic form. This organic nitrogen is converted into ammonium by fungi and bacteria through the process ammonification.
  3. Nitrification: It is the process which converts ammonia or ammonium to nitrite and then nitrite to nitrate that the plants can take in as nutrients. The bacteria Nitrosomonas and Nitrococcus convert the ammonia into nitrite and then Nitrobacter converts nitrite to nitrate by the process of oxidation.
  4. Denitrification: Anaerobic bacteria Pseudomonas and Clostridium are responsible for the process of denitrification.

questions.question:
Which of the following step is involved in the water cycle

  1. Evaporation
  2. Transpiration
  3. Precipitation
  4. Condensation
questions.explanation:
1. During part of the water cycle, the sun heats up liquid water and changes it to a gas by the process of evaporation. Water that evaporates from Earth’s oceans, lakes, rivers, and moist soil rises up into the atmosphere.
  2. The process of evaporation from plants is called transpiration.
  3. As the water in the clouds gets too heavy, the waterfalls back to the earth.  This is called precipitation.
  4. As water (in the form of gas) rises higher in the atmosphere, it starts to cool and become a liquid again. This process is called condensation. When a large amount of water vapor condenses, it results in the formation of clouds.

questions.question: The basic requirement of all life forms are
questions.explanation: All form of life requires food, water, favourable temperature to sustain life. Food provides energy for carrying out various metabolic activities. Water and temperature provide optimal conditions inside the body.

questions.question: Of these the non-conventional source of energy is
questions.explanation: The sun is an example of a non-conventional source of energy that can be used to produce heat energy; electrical energy etc. coal, petroleum, and wood are conventional sources of energy.

questions.question:
Find the incorrect statement

  1. Chinkara deer is an endangered species
  2. DDT is a non – biodegradable pollutant.
  3. Jaldapara sanctuary is situated in west Bengal
  4. Red Data Book provides information regarding threatened species of plants and animals
questions.explanation: DDT is a non-biodegradable pollutant. Red data book provides information regarding threatened species of plants and animals. Jaldpara sanctuary is situated in West Bengal. Chinkara deer is not an endangered species.

questions.question: Find the incorrect pair
questions.explanation: Bandipur Sanctuary is situated in Karnataka. Anamalai Sanctuary in Tamil Nadu. Sariska sanctuary in Rajasthan but Periyar sanctuary is in Kerela.

questions.question: Biosphere consist of:
questions.explanation: The life-supporting zone of the Earth where the atmosphere, the hydrosphere, and the lithosphere interact and make life possible, is known as the biosphere.

questions.question:
Statement A: 1% reduction of ozone in ozonosphere allows 2% more high energy ultraviolet radiations to reach earth
Statement B: Endoskeleton and exoskeleton of various animals are formed by sulphate salts.
Which statement among the two statement is true?

options.html: Both a and b are false

questions.explanation: One percent reduction of ozone in the ozonosphere allows 2% higher energy ultraviolet radiations to reach earth. UV radiations cause skin disease to human beings. Endoskeleton and exoskeleton of animals are formed by calcium and magnesium salts.

questions.question:
Find the incorrect statement

  1. All biochemical reactions occur in the medium of alcohol.
  2. 60% of the global greenhouse effect is due to {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex}.
  3. Ozone is highly poisonous to living things.
  4. Bacillus ramosus causes ammonification.
questions.explanation: 60% of the global greenhouse effect is due to CO2. Ozone is highly poisonous to living things. Bacillus ramosus causes ammonification in the soil. All biochemical reactions occur in the medium of water.

questions.question: Which one is non-biodegradable?
questions.explanation: Those substances that do not decompose into a simpler substance by the action of microbes is called non-biodegradable. DDT is a non-biodegradable substance.

questions.question: Which of the following measures can be taken to reduce the wastage of energy?
questions.explanation: To reduce the wastage of energy smokeless stoves should be used, biogas should be used and growing fuelwood trees. Biogas and smokeless stoves use maximum energy.

questions.question: Sanctuaries are established to
questions.explanation: Sanctuaries and national parks are established to protect the animals from human interference so that they can breed and grows in natural habitat.

questions.question: Daytime temperature of moon is
questions.explanation: The daytime temperature of the moon is {tex}{110^o}C{/tex}  due to the presence of a higher amount of carbon dioxide gas. During night temperature goes beyond {tex}{0^o}C{/tex}.

questions.question:
Find the incorrect statement

  1. Denitrification is oxidation of NH3 into nitrate.
  2. Biotechnology is mainly based on the principle of fermentation.
  3. The annual rainfall is from 100 cm to 200 cm in intermediate zone
  4. Natural gas is about 80% methane.
questions.explanation: Denitrification is a microbially facilitated process where nitrate is reduced and ultimately produces molecular nitrogen (N2) through a series of intermediate gaseous nitrogen oxide products.

questions.question: The death of the last individual of a species is called
questions.explanation: The death of last individual of a species is called extinction. The species that get extinct cannot be replenished.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(1) Organic nitrogenous Compounds (A) Protein
(2) Top soil (B) eco-friendly
(3) Bio-fertilizers (C) Humus
(4) Atmosphere (D) Regulates temperature
questions.explanation:
• Protein is a polymer of Amino acids which is a nitrogenous organic compound.
  • Dead And decaying part of the organic matter remains present in the topsoil in the form of humus.
  • Manures are the biofertilizer that does not contain any nitrogenous components hence it is biofriendly.
  • Atmosphere consists of several gases like carbon dioxide which help in regulating temperature.

questions.question: Which of the following is not macronutrient?
questions.explanation: The nutrients that are required in larger quantities by plants are called macronutrients. Iron is required in small quantities, so, iron is not a macronutrient.

questions.question: Xanthium and Parthenium are
questions.explanation: Unwanted plants that grow in the crop field are called weeds. Weeds compete with the main crop plant for space and nutrients. Xanthium and Parthenium are weeds.

questions.question: The branch of agricultural science dealing with the production of field crops and management of the soil is called
questions.explanation: The branch of agricultural science dealing with the production of field crops and management of the soil is called agronomy. The type of soil and type of plant that can be grown is the part of agronomy.

questions.question: The Principal cereal crop of India is
questions.explanation: Rice is the principal cereal crop of India. It is grown in most of the part of India and used by most of the people as staple food. Rice is the most widely grown tropical cereal, and over 400 million tonnes of milled rice is produced each year.

questions.question: Identify the macronutrient-
questions.explanation: The nutrients required in larger quantities are called macronutrients. Sulphur is required in larger quantity in comparison to Copper, Zinc, and Boron.

questions.question: M. S. Swaminathan is regarded as father of
questions.explanation: M.S. Swaminathan is regarded as the father of genetics in India. The green revolution leads to production of wheat many folds due to hybridization experiments carried out by him. He is also regarded as the father of Economic Ecology.

questions.question: Who is known as the father of white revolution in India?
questions.explanation: Dr. V.Kurien is known as the father of the white revolution in India. White revolution increased milk production in India many times by the use of new breeds of Cows and Buffalo.

questions.question: The unwanted plants which grow with main crops are called-
questions.explanation: Weeds are unwanted plants that grow with main crops and compete with the main crop for nutrients, space, and water.

questions.question: Choose the nutrient which is not available in fertilizers.
questions.explanation: Fertilizers contain nitrogen, phosphorous and potassium but iron (Fe) is not present in fertilizers.

questions.question: Growing two or more crops but in the definite row pattern is known as
questions.explanation: Growing of two or more crops but in the definite row, the pattern is known as intercropping. Intercropping is an example of a mixed cropping.

questions.question: Root nodules of leguminous plants contain-
questions.explanation: Root nodules of leguminous plants contain Rhizobium bacteria that convert atmospheric nitrogen into nitrite and nitrate to increase the fertility of the soil.

questions.question: Vegetables and fruits are rich in-
questions.explanation: Vegetables and fruits are rich in vitamins and minerals that increase the disease-fighting ability and provide minerals and vitamins.

questions.question: __________ is a method of control of pest or weed in which one living organism is used to control other living organisms.
questions.explanation: Biological control is a method of control of pest or weed in which one living organism is used to control other living organisms. A biological weed control regiment can consist of biological control agents, bioherbicides, use of grazing animals, and protection of natural predators. Bioagents like insects, pathogens, etc., and other animals are used to control weeds. Insect and pathogens infest weeds and they either reduce growth or kill weeds. The biological control method can reduce weeds but it is not possible to eradicate weeds.

questions.question: Find the incorrect statement.

options.html: Puccinia causes rust disease in wheat
Blast is a viral disease of Paddy
The painted bug is a pest of mustard plant

questions.explanation: Blast is a bacterial disease of paddy, not a viral disease. The other statements are correct.

questions.question: Gundhi bug is a pest of
questions.explanation: Gundhi bug is a pest of rice that cuts the stem and leaf of rice plants that reduce the production of rice.

questions.question: The element which is required in the largest quantity of plants is
questions.explanation: Plants require a number of nutrients to complete their life cycle. Copper, iron, molybdenum, boron, zinc are the important micronutrients required by plants. Nitrogen is required in the largest quantity in plants. Fertilizers and manure increase the nitrogen nutrient in the soil.

questions.question: Which is the most important source of food and fodder?
questions.explanation: The most important source of food and fodder are cereal which includes rice and wheat. Grains of rice and wheat are used as food and stems and leaves of cereals are used as fodder.

questions.question:
Statement A: Pesticides used for spraying are not volatile.
Statement B: Fumigants are volatile
Which of the two statement is true

options.html: Both A and B are false

questions.explanation: Volatile means readily vaporizes. Fumigants are intentionally volatile substances used as gases or vapors, so Statement B is true. Statement A is an absolute statement that spraying pesticides "are not volatile" — that is not universally true (many sprays are formulated to be low-volatility, but some active ingredients can volatilize or are volatile), so Statement A is false.

questions.question: The degradation of organic waste through the consumption by the earth worms is called-
questions.explanation: Vermiform composting is the degradation of organic waste through consumption by the earthworms. Earthworms hasten the process of composting.

questions.question: Marinate of potash is
questions.explanation: Marinate of potash is a Potashic fertilizer used in the soil having acidic nature.

questions.question: Mycorrhiza is a symbiotic association of

options.html: Fungi and roots of higher plant
Algae and roots of higher plant

questions.explanation: Mycorrhiza is a symbiotic association of Fungi and roots of higher plant. Fungi help in increasing absorption of water and other nutrients and angiosperm plants provide foods to fungi.

questions.question: The science of vegetable culture is called
questions.explanation: The science of vegetable culture is called horticulture. Horticulture is the science and art of growing (plants) -fruits, vegetables, flowers, and any other cultivar. It also includes plant conservation, landscape restoration, soil management, landscape and garden design, construction, and maintenance, and arboriculture. Horticulturists apply their knowledge, skills, and technologies used to grow intensively produced plants for human food and non-food uses and for personal or social needs. Their work involves plant propagation and cultivation with the aim of improving plant growth, yields, quality, nutritional value, and resistance to insects, diseases, and environmental stresses.

questions.question: Plants can be made disease – resistant by
questions.explanation: Disease resistant varieties of plants can be obtained by breeding with their wild relatives which are generally resistant to disease.

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(1) Symbiosis (A) Rabi
(2) Cropping pattern (B) Intercropping
(3) Primary nutrients (C) Legume – Rhizobium association B
(4) Crop season (D) NPK
questions.explanation:
(1) Symbiosis (C) Legume – Rhizobium association - Symbiotic relationships are a special type of interaction between species. Sometimes beneficial, sometimes harmful, these relationships are essential to many organisms and ecosystems, and they provide a balance that can only be achieved by working together. A relationship of mutual benefit or dependence.... any interdependent or mutually beneficial relationship between two persons 
(2) Cropping pattern (B) Intercropping-  Intercropping is multiple cropping practices involving growing two or more crops in proximity. The most common goal of intercropping is to produce a greater yield on a given piece of land by making use of resources or ecological processes that would otherwise not be utilized by a single crop
(3) Primary nutrients (D) NPK-  Micronutrients are essential for plant growth and play an important role in balanced crop nutrition. They include boron (B), copper (Cu), iron (Fe), manganese (Mn), molybdenum (Mo), zinc (Zn), nickel (Ni), and chloride (Cl). This study compared the availability of boron (B), copper (Cu), manganese (Mn), and zinc (Zn) in the soil and their effect in developing maize plants, supplied from a physical mixture of nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium (NPK) granules and micronutrients or from a granulated mixture of NPK coated.
(4) Crop season (A) Rabi- Rabi crops or Rabi harvest are agricultural crops sown in winter and harvested in the spring in South Asia. The term is derived from the Arabic word for "spring", which is used in the Indian subcontinent, where it is the spring harvest also known as the "winter crop".

questions.question:
Match the following with the correct response.

(1) Eutrophication (A) Increases fertility of the soil
(2) Crop rotation (B) Improves genetic makeup of crop plants
(3) Fumigants (C) Vapourises and kills insects
(4) Hybridization (D) Kills aquatic organisms
questions.explanation:
(1) Eutrophication     (D) Kills aquatic organisms- Eutrophication arises from the oversupply of nutrients, which leads to an overgrowth of plants and algae. After such organisms die, the bacterial degradation of their biomass consumes the oxygen in the water, thereby creating the state of hypoxia
(2) Crop rotation   (A) Increases fertility of the soil- Crop rotation mitigates the buildup of pathogens and pests that often occurs when one species is continuously cropped, and can also improve soil structure and fertility by increasing biomass from varied root structures.
(3) Fumigants     (C) Vapourises and kills insects- Soil fumigation is a pre-plant chemical treatment of soil, using a pesticide product that converts to form a volatile gas. The gas is able to diffuse through the open pore.  At low temperatures, the fumigant vaporizes and diffuses slower. 
(4) Hybridization (B) Improves genetic makeup of crop plants-  The natural or artificial process that results in the formation of hybrid is known as hybridization. The production of a hybrid by crossing two individuals of unlike the genetical constitution is known as hybridization. Hybridization is an important method of combining characters of different plants. Hybridization does not change the genetic contents of organisms but it produces a new combination of genes.

questions.question:
Weeds are harmful because

  1. They compete for space
  2. They compete for nutrients
  3. They compete for sunlight
  4. They compete for pesticides
questions.explanation: A weed is a plant considered undesirable in a particular situation, plant in the wrong place. Weeds threaten the survival of many plants as the weeds compete with native plants for space, nutrients, and sunlight. Weeds compete with flowers, grasses, vegetables, and fruit plants for water, sunlight, and nutrients leaving non-weed plants starving. Because most weeds grow at such an astounding rate, they often absorb more of one nutrient than another, leaving an imbalance. competing with the desired plants for the resources that a plant typically needs, namely, direct sunlight, soil nutrients, water, and (to a lesser extent) space for growth; providing hosts and vectors for plant pathogens, giving them greater opportunity to infect and degrade the quality of the desired plants.

questions.question: Who is the architect of the green revolution in India?
questions.explanation:
The Green Revolution in India was a period when agriculture in India increased due to improved methods & technology.
Swaminathan is known as the "Indian Father of Green Revolution" for his leadership and success in introducing and further developing high-yielding varieties of wheat in India. He is the founder and chairman of the MS Swaminathan Research Foundation.

questions.question:
Which of the following is a method of weed control?

  1. Pulling out by hand
  2. Crop rotation
  3. Using chemicals
  4. Hybridization
questions.explanation: A weed is a plant considered undesirable in a particular situation, a plant in the wrong place which competes for sunlight, soil, nutrients, a place with the main plant. Weed control is the botanical component which attempts to stop weeds, especially injurious weeds, from competing with desired flora and fauna, this includes domesticated plants, and in natural settings, it includes stopping nonlocal species competing with native, local, species, especially so in reserves and heritage areas. Weed control is important in agriculture Many strategies have been developed in order to contain these plants. Methods include hand cultivation with hoes, powered cultivation with cultivators, smothering with mulch, lethal wilting with high heat, burning, and chemical attack with herbicides.

questions.question: Which is the largest producer of milk in the world?
questions.explanation: The largest producer of milk in the world is India. The number of cattle in India is more than any other country but the quantity of milk per cow is less in comparison to other countries.

questions.question: Sahiwal is
questions.explanation: Sahiwal is an indigenous breed of cow. This breed of cow is superior to other dairy cows. The animal is large and of heavier built.

tests.name: Improvement In Food Resources Test-03
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Improvement In Food Resources Test-03 for CBSE class 09 Science. Students are instructed o read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: The normal body temperature of cow is
questions.explanation: The normal body temperature of cow is {tex}{\text{38}}.{{\text{3}}^0}{\text{C }}{/tex}. The body temperature gives information about the health of the animals.

questions.question: Cow Pox is a
questions.explanation: Cowpox is a viral disease in which small blisters develop on the body of animals with a high fever.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a basic need of man?
questions.explanation: A traditional list of immediate "basic needs" is food (including water), shelter, and clothing. Many modern lists emphasize the minimum level of consumption of 'basic needs' of not just food, water, clothing, and shelter, but also sanitation, education, and healthcare.

questions.question: Rinderpest disease of poultry is caucused by
questions.explanation: Rinderpest disease or cattle plague is an infectious viral disease of cattle, domestic buffalo, and other species.

questions.question: The method that is maximum used in cattle breeding is
questions.explanation: Cattle breeding is done to improve the varieties for animals for milk and meat. Cattle breeding involves artificial insemination. The insemination involves the collection of semen from the exotic or superior male and delivering to the female genital tract for fertilization.

questions.question: Aseel is a popular breed of
questions.explanation: Aseel is a popular breed of fowl. This breed provides high yield of meat and also is a good egg layer.

questions.question:
Find the correct statement

  1. Mehsana is a breed of buffalo.
  2. Jersey is an Indian breed of cow
  3. Rinder pest is a parasitic disease
  4. Karan Swiss is produced by crossbreeding between Jersey and red sindhi
questions.explanation:
• Jersey is an exotic breed of cow.
  • Rinder pest is is a viral disease.
  • Karan Swiss is produced by crossing Brown Swiss and Sahiwal.
  • Mehsana is a breed of buffalo.

questions.question: What is the full form of NDRI.
questions.explanation: NDRL stands for National dairy research institute. This institution is responsible for development of milch animals for milk producing ability.

questions.question:
‘The production of food from animal sources has increased greatly in last few decades’.
The above statement is
questions.explanation: Production of food from animal sources has increased greatly in last few decades due to increased facility of preserving milk food.

questions.question: Inland fisheries is referred to

options.html: Trapping and Capturing fishes from the sea coast.

questions.explanation: Inland fisheries are referred to as culturing fish in freshwater like rivers, ponds, and lakes.

questions.question:
Statement A: Methyl Bromide is a liquid fumigant.
Statement B: Excreta of birds contain bacteria salmonella which causes food poisoning.
Which of the two statements is true?
questions.explanation: Methyl bromide is a gaseous fumigant. Excreta of birds contain bacteria salmonella which causes food poisoning.

questions.question: The exotic variety of bee commonly used for commercial honey productions is:
questions.explanation: The exotic variety of bee commonly used for commercial honey productions is an Italian bee (Apis mellifera).

questions.question: What is the yield of milk from Murrah buffalo?
questions.explanation: Murrah is the original breed of Haryana and Punjab. During its lactation period, its average yield of milk is 1800 to 2500 liters.

questions.question:
Find the Incorrect pair

  1. Buffalo – Red Sindhi
  2. Cow – Gir
  3. Buffalo – Murrah
  4. Cow – Sahiwal
questions.explanation: Gir, Red Sindhi, and Sahiwal are breeds of cows and Murrah is a breed of buffalo.

questions.question: Which of the following is the composition of Honey?
questions.explanation: Honey contains sugar, mineral and vitamins. It is a highly nutritious food that provides energy along with energy. Due to its high sugar content, on average 80–83%, honey is an excellent energy source (304 kcal/100 g)
